rows.imp_rat: Condensation on sterilized cups and lids after cooling risks moisture-driven contamination and packing defects. Moderately central to a clean discharge; consequences are contained quality issues rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Condensation on cups and lids after cooling remains a recurring quality issue that current cooling practice only partly controls. Adequate cooling and drying dwell time usually removes surface condensation but adds cycle length. Managing condensation needs some process knowledge of dew point and airflow but is fairly routine. Air blowers or drying stages consume modest energy without major cost impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of utility-borne contaminants contacting closure loads during sterilization
rows.plain: Prevent unwanted material carried by the equipment's water, air, or other services from touching container closures during cleaning or sterilizing. This helps keep the closures safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Utility-borne contaminants contacting loads could recontaminate or compromise closures; relevant to protected sterility with severe potential though comparatively uncommon.
rows.sat_rat: Established clean-utility standards substantially control utility-borne contamination, though quality drift can still occur. Clean-steam and filtered-utility provisioning is designed in early, adding little operational delay. Pharmaceutical-grade utility design is a mature specialty with established standards. Clean-steam generators and filtration add significant capital cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of drainage or condensate connections that leak during machine operation
rows.plain: Prevent leaks at the connections that carry away drain water or condensed liquid while the machine is running. This helps avoid spills and damage.
rows.imp_rat: Drainage or condensate leaks during operation affect hygienic operation and can compromise the sterilize/cool environment; severity is moderate as leaks create contamination and slip hazards but rarely cause immediate batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: Drainage joints tested only at commissioning often reveal leaks under real thermal/flow conditions, so operating-time leaks remain a real recurring gap. Chasing an operating-time drain leak often requires stopping the process, adding unplanned delay. Proper slope, sealing and clamping of drainage lines needs competent installers but no rare expertise. Operational condensate/drainage leaks cause water damage and cleanup costs that recur intermittently.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the machine restarting while the unsafe condition persists
rows.plain: Prevent the machine from starting again while the dangerous condition still exists. This gives people time to correct the problem safely.
rows.imp_rat: Restarting while an unsafe condition persists is a safety-control failure peripheral to sterilization efficacy. Severity is high because it can re-energize a hazardous machine state and injure operators or damage the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Latching safety interlocks that block restart until the unsafe condition clears are standard and dependable on modern machines. Restart lockouts prevent immediate restart, correctly holding rather than saving time when conditions are unsafe. Interlock-enforced restart prevention removes reliance on the operator remembering to check conditions. Reset interlocks are standard low-cost control features.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of calibration records associated with the wrong temperature, pressure, or flow sensors
rows.plain: Reduce the number of calibration records linked to the wrong temperature, pressure, or airflow sensor. This matters because the records may then give a false impression that the right sensors were checked.
rows.imp_rat: Calibration records tied to the wrong sensors corrupt validation traceability supporting the sterilization claim (relevance); mislabeled records can lead to accepting an under-processed batch or failing an audit (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Unique sensor IDs, tag conventions and calibration software make mismatched records fairly uncommon, though manual entry errors persist. Matching calibration records to the correct sensor tags is a brief clerical step during documentation. Correct sensor-to-record association depends on disciplined tagging rather than deep expertise. Misassigned records can trigger investigations and revalidation, a cost that asset-tagging discipline keeps low.

rows.source_job: Diagnose cycle deviations from process data
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attributing the cycle deviation to the wrong cause
rows.plain: Make sure a change in the machine's work cycle is linked to the correct cause. This prevents the wrong problem from being fixed.
rows.imp_rat: Attributing a cycle deviation to the wrong cause affects diagnosis quality, a support activity rather than the core sterilize-and-cool job. Consequences moderate—misdiagnosis can allow repeat deviations or release of suspect product.
rows.sat_rat: Without guided diagnostics, misattribution of cycle deviations is common and largely dependent on individual judgement. Root-cause investigation of a deviation is inherently slow and often done after the fact. Attributing a deviation to the correct cause demands strong process expertise that few operators possess. No dedicated tooling is typically provided, so cost falls on skilled labor time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cooling before sterilized product reaches the safe packing temperature
rows.plain: Shorten the time needed to cool the sterilized product before it reaches a temperature safe for packing. This helps the work move forward sooner without packing it while it is too hot.
rows.imp_rat: Cooling duration to reach safe packing temperature is central to throughput and the cooling function, but longer cooling is mostly an efficiency loss rather than a safety failure. Severity moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Cooling reliably reaches safe temperature, but the time it takes is often longer than desired and constrains throughput. Cooling duration is a well-understood bottleneck that established chill-zone design minimizes but cannot eliminate. Cooling time is set by design, requiring little operator skill. Faster cooling needs more chilling capacity, a known cost trade-off.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of removing the closure load before safe-opening conditions are confirmed
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of removing the processed load before it is confirmed safe to open. This helps prevent exposure to heat, pressure, or other hazards.
rows.imp_rat: Removing the load before safe-opening conditions is confirmed is a personnel-safety and integrity concern; relevant to safe operation with severe injury potential but tangential to sterility outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure/temperature interlocks on the door reliably prevent opening before safe conditions on modern autoclaves. Safe-opening interlocks may delay opening until conditions are met. Interlocks remove most operator judgement about safe conditions. Preventing unsafe opening avoids injury cost and load loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury from residual pressure, steam, or hot surfaces during removal
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that someone is hurt by trapped pressure, hot steam, or hot surfaces when removing a processed load. This helps make removal safer.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury from residual pressure/steam/hot surfaces is a serious safety hazard but peripheral to the sterilization CFJ itself; high severity when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks, PPE and cool-down procedures make injury from residual heat/steam uncommon though not eliminated. Cool-down waits before removal cost time but protect personnel. Safe handling requires trained use of PPE and awareness, a modest skill demand. Injuries cause downtime and liability cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of including closures with unverified washed or siliconized status in a load
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of putting caps or stoppers into a load when it has not been confirmed that they were properly washed and coated. This helps ensure the items are ready for processing.
rows.imp_rat: Including closures with unverified wash/silicone status affects input state and bioburden assumptions; relevant to load integrity with moderate-to-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record gating, status labels and hold controls are mature practices that make inclusion of unverified closures uncommon though not impossible. Verifying wash/silicone status via batch records adds a quick check before staging. Status verification is a standard clerical/label check requiring modest attention rather than deep expertise. Catching unverified closures upstream avoids costly wasted sterilization cycles.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine power or protective conductors terminated at incorrect connection points
rows.plain: Prevent power wires and safety wires from being connected to the wrong points. Correct connections help the machine work safely and reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly terminated power or protective conductors is a commissioning/installation error somewhat removed from the recurring sterilize job, but its consequences are severe — electric shock, fire, or equipment damage endangering personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Color-coded conductors, labeled terminals, torque specs and point-to-point continuity checks make incorrect terminations uncommon under standard electrical practice. Terminating conductors per labeled wiring diagrams is a routine part of commissioning that rarely stretches the schedule when done by a qualified electrician. The task depends on a licensed electrician reading terminal markings and drawings, a widely available competency though still expert-dependent. Miswiring can cause equipment damage, but standard practice keeps that cost occurrence low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between cycle alarm activation and operator acknowledgment
rows.plain: Reduce the time it takes workers to acknowledge an alarm after it starts. A quick response can help limit problems and keep the process safe.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between cycle alarm and operator acknowledgment matters to timely intervention but is one step in a chain. Consequences moderate—delays can compound spoilage or safety exposure but acknowledgment alone is not the fix.
rows.sat_rat: Acknowledgment is quick in staffed operation but unstaffed or noisy environments and alarm fatigue still delay it. Loud, visible alarms typically prompt acknowledgment within seconds when an operator is at the station. Acknowledging an alarm requires little skill once the operator is present. Alarm annunciation is a standard low-cost feature.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among the lethality acceptance criteria for the batch
rows.plain: Reduce the number of unresolved disagreements about how effective the sterilization must be for the batch. This helps ensure everyone uses the same standard when deciding whether the batch is safe.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among lethality acceptance criteria create ambiguity in judging whether a batch is sterile (relevance); wrong disposition decisions can release unsafe or waste safe product (severity), though it's a decision-layer issue rather than the physical process.
rows.sat_rat: Documented acceptance specs usually align, but overlapping regulatory and internal criteria still produce conflicts that must be manually adjudicated, an intermittent gap. Resolving conflicting acceptance criteria delays batch release while QA reconciles them. Reconciling conflicting lethality criteria requires process-authority expertise and is heavily judgement-dependent. Unresolved conflicts can hold or scrap batches, but the analysis itself is chiefly a labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between validated-state documents and approved autoclave operating conditions
rows.plain: Keep the approved operating conditions for the steam-sterilizing equipment consistent with the documents that define how it must be operated. This helps ensure the equipment remains approved for use.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between validated-state documents and approved operating conditions is largely a documentation/control gap; relevant to compliance with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and periodic review keep documents aligned reasonably well, though drift between updates is a known recurring issue. Reconciling validated-state documents with operating conditions is a periodic review, not a per-cycle delay. Detecting divergence between documentation and actual conditions demands document-control and validation expertise. Document drift rarely wastes material directly but risks costly compliance findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closures damaged or degraded during sterilization
rows.plain: Prevent caps or seals from being damaged or weakened during the germ-killing treatment. This helps keep the containers properly closed and the product protected.
rows.imp_rat: Closures damaged/degraded during sterilization is a product-integrity concern that can affect container closure function; relevant but secondary to the sterilization CFJ, with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cycles are validated to protect elastomer integrity, so damage is infrequent though extended or repeated cycles can still degrade closures. Damage from cycles is not a timing issue for the overseer. Selecting closure-safe cycle parameters is a validation task, minimizing routine judgement. Damaged closures cause scrap and rework cost, which validated cycles largely avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of closures that lose dimensional or functional integrity under the specified cycle limits
rows.plain: Keep caps or seals from losing their shape or ability to work during the specified treatment. This helps them continue to protect the product afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Closures losing dimensional/functional integrity under cycle limits affects container closure integrity; relevant to product quality but secondary to the sterilization CFJ, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closure material compatibility with steam limits is fairly well understood, making integrity loss uncommon within validated limits. Integrity testing after cycle limits adds characterization time. Assessing closure dimensional/functional integrity under heat needs materials and container-closure expertise. Damaged closures represent direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required validation records with missing or unusable cycle evidence
rows.plain: Make sure each required review record contains usable proof that the processing cycle happened correctly. This helps show that the cycle met its requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or unusable cycle evidence in validation records is largely a documentation/compliance error rather than live cycle control (relevance moderate); it can block release and cause audit findings but rarely causes direct product harm (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Automated data loggers and batch reports improve evidence capture, but missing or unusable records still occur often enough to be a recurring quality pain. Capturing complete cycle evidence adds documentation time that is often compressed under production pressure. Knowing exactly what constitutes usable cycle evidence relies on QA training, and gaps recur with less-experienced staff. Missing or unusable records force revalidation, a substantial rework cost that data-logging partly mitigates.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterilized cups or lids deformed during cooling
rows.plain: Prevent sterilized cups and lids from becoming misshapen as they cool. This helps them remain usable and fit together properly.
rows.imp_rat: Deformed cups or lids during cooling affects downstream packing and product integrity. Moderately relevant to a usable output; consequences are scrap and rework rather than safety failures.
rows.sat_rat: Managed cooling ramps limit deformation of cups and lids in most cases, though thin containers still occasionally warp. Controlled cooling ramps prevent thermal shock without materially extending cycle time. Deformation avoidance is engineered into the cooling profile, needing little operator input. Gradual cooling uses standard utilities with minor cost implications.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cups and lids damaged by excessive thermal exposure during the sterilization cycle
rows.plain: As few cups and lids as possible should be damaged because they are exposed to too much heat during sterilization. This helps keep the containers usable and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Cups and lids damaged by excessive thermal exposure during the cycle affect packaging integrity, adjacent to but not the core sterilizing outcome. Consequences are quality/scrap losses, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established validated sterilization cycles keep thermal damage low, with residual issues mainly from marginal container specs. Validated cycle times bound thermal exposure so overexposure damage is time-limited by design. Following a validated recipe keeps this out of the operator's hands. Container spoilage is minimized by proven recipes, avoiding scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required sterilization and cooling evidence is unavailable when the operator confirms completion
rows.plain: Make sure proof that the cycle was properly sterilized and cooled is available when the operator marks it complete. This supports safe and properly documented operations.
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterilization/cooling evidence at completion confirmation affects the ability to prove the job was done rather than doing it (relevance); absent evidence can force batch holds or unjustified release (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated batch records and integrated recorders cover most cases, but recorder faults, network dropouts or manual chart gaps still occasionally leave evidence missing at confirmation. Modern PLC-based sterilizers log cycle data automatically in near real time, so evidence is usually present the instant the operator confirms. Retrieving and validating the record can still demand operator judgement or IT support when the data-logging system hiccups. Data-acquisition and recorder hardware is a standard included cost, not an added burden per cycle.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of processed closures with unacceptable residual moisture at removal
rows.plain: Make sure processed closures are not too wet when they are removed. Too much leftover moisture can indicate that processing was not completed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Unacceptable residual moisture at removal is a post-cycle quality attribute affecting downstream use; relevant but secondary to achieving SAL, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Post-vacuum drying phases control closure moisture fairly well but residual moisture remains a common recurring complaint for closures. Drying phases and post-vacuum extend cycle time to control moisture. Achieving acceptable moisture is cycle-design driven, low operator skill. Excess residual moisture can force reprocessing, wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure packages with residual water exceeding the specified acceptance limit
rows.plain: Reduce how often packages containing closures have more leftover water than allowed. This helps ensure the packages meet the required loading condition.
rows.imp_rat: Residual water above limit affects downstream drying/use and can harbor issues, but is largely a post-cycle quality attribute peripheral to achieving SAL; moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Post-wash draining and drying steps are standard but residual water in crevices or packages still slips through inconsistent visual checks. Draining and inspecting for residual water adds handling time before wrapping. Judging acceptable residual moisture is partly subjective and operator-dependent. Residual water can defeat sterilization and cause rework, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual moisture retained by closures at completion of the specified cycle
rows.plain: Remove as much leftover water as possible from the stoppers or lids by the end of the cleaning cycle. This helps keep the containers properly prepared.
rows.imp_rat: Residual moisture at cycle completion is primarily a drying/quality attribute for downstream handling; relevant but peripheral to SAL achievement, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Residual moisture on closures is a common, only-partly-solved nuisance since closure geometry traps water despite drying phases. Achieving dryness adds drying-phase time to the cycle. Specifying effective post-cycle drying for closures requires vacuum-drying know-how. Extended drying and vacuum consume energy and cycle time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that claimed throughput lacks evidence for worst-case closure loads achieving the defined SAL
rows.plain: Claims about how much the equipment can handle should be supported by tests showing it works even with the hardest loads to close while still reaching the required level of germ reduction. This helps ensure the stated capacity is realistic.
rows.imp_rat: Throughput claims lacking worst-case SAL evidence are primarily a validation/marketing gap; relevant to defensibility but lower direct severity to a given sterilized load.
rows.sat_rat: Claimed throughput is frequently stated for ideal rather than worst-case SAL-achieving loads, so evidence gaps recur during procurement. Worst-case load studies add time but are part of established PQ campaigns. Defining and justifying worst-case closure loads for throughput claims requires seasoned judgement that is not always applied by vendors. Vendor throughput claims are often offered without the cost of worst-case validation being borne upfront.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required steam, water, drainage, electrical, and compressed-air parameters missing or unverified
rows.plain: Make sure all needed requirements for steam, water, drains, electricity, and compressed air are listed and checked. This helps confirm that the equipment can work properly at the site.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/unverified utility parameters (steam, water, drainage, electrical, air) is largely a commissioning/readiness gap; relevant to reliable operation but moderate severity for a given load.
rows.sat_rat: Utility requirement checklists exist but parameters are frequently left unverified against actual site conditions, causing recurring surprises. Compiling and verifying all utility parameters is time-consuming during specification. Cross-referencing steam, water, drainage, electrical, and air parameters requires multidisciplinary utility engineering skill. Missing parameters discovered late trigger costly rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of steam, water, or compressed-air connection leaks detected during commissioning
rows.plain: Prevent leaks where steam, water, or compressed air lines are connected during startup testing. This helps the equipment operate safely and reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Steam, water, or air connection leaks found at commissioning matter to delivering correct process utilities, but being caught during commissioning limits severity to rework and delay rather than in-production failure.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure/leak tests during commissioning catch most leaks, but fittings, gaskets and seals still routinely leak on first pressurization and need rework. Leak-testing and re-tightening joints during commissioning adds bounded, expected time to the schedule. Making sound steam, water and air connections relies on standard fitter skills and torqueing that are broadly held. Detected leaks waste utilities and consumables until fixed, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the available source does not match the machine's specified electrical characteristics
rows.plain: Use a power source that matches what the machine needs. This helps prevent electrical damage or unsafe operation.
rows.imp_rat: A source not matching specified electrical characteristics is largely a setup/installation prerequisite rather than an ongoing sterilization driver (relevance); mismatch can damage equipment or abort cycles (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Nameplate ratings and standardized electrical verification make source-mismatch errors rare where qualified electricians follow normal practice. Checking supply voltage/phase against the nameplate is a fast pre-connection verification. Matching electrical characteristics is standard electrician work with well-understood, low-ambiguity criteria. A mismatch can destroy drives or controls, but the check is cheap and routinely prevents such loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contaminated residue or sanitation effluent released into the facility during teardown
rows.plain: Keep contaminated material and dirty cleaning liquid from spreading through the facility while equipment is taken apart. This helps protect people and nearby areas.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated residue or sanitation effluent released during teardown is peripheral to producing a sterile batch (relevance); still, effluent release poses facility hygiene and worker-exposure hazards (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Ad hoc teardown practices leave frequent gaps so contaminated residue release into the facility is a recurring, poorly-controlled event. Teardown decontamination adds time that crews often compress, letting residue escape when schedules are tight. Containing contaminated effluent during teardown depends heavily on experienced hazmat-trained crews who are scarce for one-off decommission jobs. Containment berms, absorbents and disposal capacity are commodity items but often under-provisioned for disposal work.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of closure loads requiring repeat sterilization or disposal after failing to achieve the defined SAL
rows.plain: Reduce how often a load of closures must be sterilized again or thrown away because it was not made sufficiently free of harmful germs. This lowers waste and operating costs.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat sterilization or disposal after SAL failure is primarily rework/waste; the sterility failure is caught, so severity is operational rather than patient-safety catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Well-validated saturated-steam cycles make load failures uncommon, though load-configuration errors still cause occasional reprocessing. Failed loads requiring reprocessing consume schedule time, but validated cycles keep occurrences low. Once cycles are validated, routine operation needs modest skill to avoid failures. Repeat sterilization and disposal waste material and energy when they occur.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unintended electrical interruption to adjacent processing areas during source connection
rows.imp_rat: Unintended electrical interruption to adjacent areas during source connection is peripheral to the sterilize/cool job itself, but when it occurs it can halt neighboring production and cause disruptive downtime, giving moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Isolation planning and dedicated circuits usually contain disruption, though unintended interruptions to neighboring areas still occasionally occur. Planned isolation and switching sequences minimize interruption duration but still require coordinated downtime windows. Sequencing connections to protect adjacent areas needs experienced electrical planning that is not always available on short notice. Interruptions to adjacent processing lines cause lost production, a cost that careful scheduling reduces but rarely eliminates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unsubstantiated closure sterility entering fill-finish is attributed to the overseer's governance
rows.plain: There should be solid proof that the caps or stoppers entering the filling process are free of germs. Without that proof, the person responsible for oversight could be blamed for an unsupported claim of cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of unsubstantiated sterility to the overseer's governance is a governance/accountability framing; peripheral to the physical CFJ though reputationally consequential.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that usable closure throughput falls below projected peak demand while achieving the defined SAL
rows.plain: There should be enough usable, properly protected caps or stoppers to meet the busiest expected demand while reaching the required safety level. A system that cannot keep up could delay production or create shortages.
rows.imp_rat: Throughput below peak demand while achieving SAL is a capacity/business concern, not a sterility failure; peripheral to the CFJ with moderate operational severity.
rows.sat_rat: Usable throughput at the defined SAL is often overestimated relative to real peak demand, a recurring gap in capacity planning. Peak-demand throughput shortfalls surface late, often only after cycle timing is measured in operation. Reconciling usable throughput at SAL against peak demand requires cross-functional modelling skill that is unevenly available. Underprovisioned capacity forces costly overtime or added equipment later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual fluid released into the surrounding work area during drainage
rows.imp_rat: Residual fluid released into the work area during drainage is peripheral to achieving sterility (relevance); the main consequence is slip hazards and hygiene issues rather than product safety failure (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Even with standard drainage practice, residual pockets and disconnected fittings routinely dribble fluid into the work area. Controlled drainage into portable totes takes modest extra time that crews usually accept. Draining lines cleanly is a fairly routine mechanical task most technicians can perform. Drip trays, pumps and containers are inexpensive and commonly on hand.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of canceling a service still used by active production assets
rows.imp_rat: Canceling a service still used by active production assets is largely an administrative/configuration concern peripheral to the physical sterilize-cool job; severity is moderate as it can interrupt a running asset but is typically recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Service maps and utility drawings help, but shared feeders and undocumented ties make wrong cancellations a recurring hazard. Verifying no active asset still uses a service before cancellation adds coordination time. Tracing shared-service dependencies takes facilities knowledge that competent engineers usually possess. A wrong cancellation can idle live production, an expensive but avoidable consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a critical cycle excursion during completion review
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a critical cycle excursion at review risks releasing under-sterilized closures, yet ranking holds it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated data review and alarm flags make overlooked excursions uncommon, though manual record review can still miss subtle events. Completion review adds a defined but short review step. Spotting a critical excursion in the record demands trained review judgement. Overlooking an excursion risks releasing a bad load, a serious cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cycle alarms or interruptions left unresolved during completion review
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved cycle alarms at completion review risk accepting a bad cycle, but ordering caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm logs and mandatory disposition steps make leaving alarms unresolved uncommon in disciplined records. Resolving alarms before sign-off adds review time proportional to alarm count. Resolving alarms requires trained interpretation of their significance. Unresolved alarms can indicate compromised, wasted loads.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the actual load configuration and the recorded validated configuration
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of actual vs recorded validated configuration matters for traceability but is a comparatively administrative discrepancy; kept at the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented load configurations help but real-time verification of actual vs recorded is manual and error-prone. Comparing actual to recorded configuration is a brief verification. It relies on operator care in matching the physical load to records. Deviation from validated configuration risks an invalid, wasted cycle.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of dispositioning an affected closure load without complete abnormal-condition evidence
rows.imp_rat: Dispositioning without complete abnormal-condition evidence risks releasing suspect loads, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation procedures require evidence but completeness for abnormal events is inconsistently captured in practice. Assembling complete evidence before disposition adds review time. Judging evidence completeness requires QA/deviation expertise. Dispositioning without evidence risks costly wrong release or scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the load shifting from the validated position before cycle start
rows.imp_rat: Load shifting from validated position before start can impair penetration, but ranking caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Racks and short load-to-start intervals limit shifting, but there is no positive verification, leaving some risk. Confirming the load hasn't shifted is a brief pre-start glance. Detecting shift relies on operator observation. A shifted load can under-process, wasting closures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the load configuration and validated cycle bounds
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between load configuration and validated cycle bounds is relevant to lethality assurance, but ranked below batch 1; capped, so relevance 4 and severity 3 keep the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validated load patterns and load sheets constrain this but adherence is largely procedural and manually checked. Checking load against validated bounds is a quick pre-start step. It requires operator knowledge of the validated load envelope. An out-of-bounds load can be under-sterilized and wasted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of verified cycle settings changing before cycle start
rows.imp_rat: Verified settings changing before cycle start could invalidate the run, but ordering forces the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe locking and access control make settings changing after verification rare on modern systems. Change-lock between verification and start is enforced instantly. Little skill needed as system locks parameters after verification. An undetected change before start risks a wasted cycle.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of using an obsolete or mismatched specification for the closure load
rows.imp_rat: Using an obsolete/mismatched load specification can drive a wrong cycle, but ranking holds it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems and effective-date checks make mismatched specs uncommon though not impossible. Retrieving the current specification adds a brief verification step. Recognizing an obsolete spec depends on document-control awareness. Using a wrong spec can ruin a load, an expensive but infrequent error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure packages with damaged or incompletely secured barriers after assembly
rows.imp_rat: Damaged or incompletely secured barriers risk recontamination, but this ranks below batch 1; both proxies at 3.
rows.sat_rat: Seal checks and sealing equipment are established, but incomplete or damaged seals still recur due to manual sealing variability. Verifying seal integrity after assembly is a quick routine check. Confirming secure barriers is a learnable inspection task with moderate attention demands. Incompletely secured barriers cause rework and occasional scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing particulate or microbial contamination during closure-load assembly
rows.imp_rat: Introducing particulate/microbial contamination during assembly adds bioburden, but batch ordering caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled environments and gowning make gross contamination rare, though low-level particulate/microbial ingress during assembly is never fully eliminated. Cleanroom gowning and controlled handling add time but are built into the workflow. Contamination-avoiding technique requires trained aseptic discipline and constant vigilance. Contamination events cause costly investigations and lost loads.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prepared closures acquiring microbial contamination while awaiting sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Prepared closures acquiring contamination while awaiting sterilization raises bioburden, but ordering holds at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Time limits and controlled staging areas help but hold-time excursions still allow occasional microbial acquisition. Prompt processing after prep limits exposure but depends on scheduling. Minimizing microbial pickup relies on controlled environment discipline. Bioburden growth risks failed loads and investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prepared-closure packages with breached protective barriers before sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Breached protective barriers before sterilization create contamination risk, yet ordering places this below batch 1; capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Visual barrier checks are standard practice yet small breaches often go undetected until later, leaving recurring failures. Inspecting barriers before sterilization is a brief added step. Spotting breached barriers requires attentive visual inspection that varies by operator. Breached barriers force repackaging or scrap, a modest material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of staged closure loads with wrapping or containment damaged during positioning
rows.imp_rat: Wrapping/containment damaged during positioning creates a recontamination pathway, but ranking caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Protective containers and careful handling keep positioning damage relatively rare. Inspecting wrapping after positioning adds a brief check. Detecting positioning damage is routine visual inspection. Damaged containment causes repackaging and delay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of staging a closure load for an incompatible autoclave cycle
rows.imp_rat: Staging a load for an incompatible cycle risks under-processing, but ranking holds the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cycle-specific labeling and scheduling controls make incompatible staging uncommon. Confirming cycle compatibility is a quick check against documentation. Matching load to cycle relies on operator reading the correct spec, a modest skill. Wrong-cycle staging risks a fully wasted or failed load.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of error in recorded exposure values used to judge sterilization-cycle acceptability
rows.imp_rat: Error in recorded exposure values used to judge acceptability could wrongly pass a cycle, but ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic data recording with validated instruments makes recorded-value errors uncommon and mostly auto-detected. Recorded exposure values are captured automatically with no manual delay. Automated data capture removes most manual transcription skill needs. Recording errors are cheap to prevent electronically and rarely cause waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterilization-impacting change is misclassified as having no validated-state impact
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying a change as no-impact undermines validated state; serious but ranked below batch 1, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control forms and SME review reduce misclassification, but it remains a persistent judgement-dependent vulnerability. Change classification happens at assessment time and rarely creates timing pressure. Correctly judging whether a change impacts the validated state is a demanding expert-judgement task where misclassification is a well-known human-error mode. A misclassified change can trigger expensive rework or recalls if impact is missed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of validated-state changes implemented before impact approval
rows.imp_rat: Implementing validated-state changes before impact approval risks uncontrolled cycles, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control workflows enforce pre-approval fairly well, though informal or urgent changes sometimes bypass sequence. Impact approval before implementation adds lead time to changes. Enforcing pre-implementation approval relies on change-control discipline more than deep skill. Premature changes can force costly rework or invalidation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a closure lot affected by an unresolved change enters sterile filling before validated-state disposition
rows.imp_rat: A change-affected lot entering sterile filling is a serious contamination pathway, but batch ordering forces the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine and change-control linkage make premature entry of affected lots uncommon when systems are followed. Quarantine holds pending validated-state disposition delay downstream filling but are enforced. Deciding whether a change affects a lot's sterility basis needs cross-functional expertise. Holding affected lots incurs carrying cost yet prevents costlier non-conforming use.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected validated-state elements omitted from a change assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting affected validated-state elements from a change assessment leaves gaps in control, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists help, but omissions of affected elements remain a recognized weakness of change assessments. Assessment completeness is set at authoring time, not a recurring delay. Identifying every affected validated-state element in a change requires broad system knowledge and is a frequent gap in assessments. Omitted elements cause rework or escaped defects, an intermittent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that interactions among concurrent or cumulative changes affecting sterilization conditions are overlooked
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking interactions among concurrent changes can defeat assurance, but ranking holds the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard change control handles changes individually and poorly captures interactions, making this a real recurring blind spot. Evaluating change interactions is done at assessment, not per cycle. Recognizing cumulative or concurrent change interactions is among the hardest expert-judgement tasks and is often missed. Overlooked interactions can trigger large, costly failures downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an external sterilization-impacting change is not reported before use
rows.imp_rat: An external impacting change unreported before use undermines validated state, but ordering caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier change-notification agreements exist but are imperfectly honored, leaving a notable gap. External-change reporting timing depends on suppliers and is not internally controlled. Recognizing an external change as sterilization-impacting requires supplier-quality awareness. Unreported external changes can cause expensive undetected failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between post-change sterilization conditions and the approved validated state
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between post-change conditions and validated state threatens control, but ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and requalification practices generally catch post-change discrepancies, with residual gaps for subtle drift. Post-change verification against the validated state adds project time, not routine cycle time. Confirming post-change conditions match the approved state requires validation engineering judgement. Re-validation after changes is resource-intensive but bounded to change events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical cycle value is accepted from an instrument outside calibration status

rows.sat_rat: Calibration programs with due-date tracking make acceptance of values from uncalibrated instruments rare. Calibration status is checked at review, adding little time. rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that sterilization deviations remain unresolved while the autoclave continues operating

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical cycle values lack valid calibration status during processed-load review
rows.imp_rat: Critical values lacking calibration status at review invalidate release evidence, but ordering caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration due-date systems make lapsed-status values at review rare when maintained. Calibration status is confirmed during processed-load review with minimal delay. Checking calibration validity at review is a routine, low-skill verification. Detecting lapsed calibration at review prevents expensive downstream release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of autoclave changes lacking documented impact assessment and approval before use
rows.imp_rat: Autoclave changes lacking impact assessment before use threaten validated state, but ranking holds it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems make undocumented changes uncommon in mature sites, though small changes occasionally escape. Requiring documented impact assessment before use lengthens change timelines. Producing adequate impact assessments needs qualified assessors. Undocumented changes create expensive compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterilization cycles supported by values from instruments outside verified status
rows.imp_rat: Cycles supported by unverified-instrument values undermine acceptance validity, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Verification programs make reliance on unverified instruments uncommon, similar to calibration controls. Instrument verification status is checked at review without adding much time. Verification tracking is procedural and modestly skill-dependent. Values from unverified instruments can void loads, a controlled but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure loads assembled outside validated loading boundaries
rows.imp_rat: Loads assembled outside validated boundaries can affect steam penetration, but this batch ranks lower; both proxies held at 3.
rows.sat_rat: Documented loading configurations and operator training keep most loads within validated boundaries, though manual loading errors still occur. Following loading diagrams adds no meaningful time. Correct loading depends on operator adherence to validated patterns, a trained but manual step. Improper loading risk wastes loads, but correct loading has negligible added cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of compliant sterilized loads rejected during disposition
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting compliant sterilized loads is a waste/yield loss, not a sterility failure; peripheral to the CFJ with low operational severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conservative disposition biases toward rejection, so compliant loads are sometimes discarded with little systematic mitigation. Re-review of unnecessarily rejected loads consumes disposition time. Distinguishing true failures from conservative over-rejection needs experienced judgement that is variable. False rejection of compliant loads directly wastes good material and reprocessing resources, and is only loosely controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of approved closure-load configurations lacking current evidence of defined SAL attainment
rows.imp_rat: Approved configs lacking current SAL evidence is a compliance gap; important but ranked lower, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Revalidation schedules cover main configurations, but less-used approved configurations can lapse on current SAL evidence. Confirming current SAL evidence per configuration is a periodic review, not a per-load delay. Tracking validation currency across configurations needs validation-lifecycle expertise that is often thinly staffed. Maintaining periodic revalidation for every configuration is resource-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of approved closure sterilization controls that do not reflect the current validated state
rows.imp_rat: Controls not reflecting current validated state weaken assurance; important but ranked lower, held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control links validation to controls reasonably, but stale procedures not reflecting the latest validated state are a recurring gap. Reflecting the validated state in controls lags because change updates take time to propagate. Keeping controls aligned to the current validated state requires change-control and validation expertise. Change management effort is a recurring administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sensor, calibration, or placement error is mistaken for inadequate closure lethality
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking sensor/placement error for inadequate lethality can misdirect diagnosis; relevant but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration programs and redundant sensors help, but sensor-versus-process confusion still recurs during deviation diagnosis. Distinguishing sensor error from real lethality failure can prolong investigations. Differentiating calibration/placement artifacts from true low lethality demands experienced metrology and investigation skill. Misattribution can trigger unnecessary batch rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that pressure-temperature evidence inconsistent with saturated steam is overlooked during deviation diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking non-saturated-steam evidence in diagnosis can mask lethality failure, yet ranking holds it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Saturation-inconsistent P-T evidence is frequently overlooked because review depends on individual analyst vigilance. Reviewing pressure-temperature saturation evidence adds analysis time in deviations. Spotting saturation inconsistencies requires steam-thermodynamics fluency that few reviewers reliably apply. Overlooked evidence risks costly wrong disposition.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that achieving the sterility assurance target causes unacceptable closure degradation
rows.imp_rat: Achieving SAL causing closure degradation is a real trade-off, but ranking places it below batch 1, capped at 3.0.

Assessing closure degradation against lethality requires added material testing time. Balancing SAL against closure integrity requires materials and process expertise. Degraded closures represent material waste and cost if over-processed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of routine closure loads outside the evaluated mass, density, or arrangement boundary
rows.imp_rat: Routine loads outside evaluated mass/density/arrangement bounds risk penetration failure, but ranking caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Documented load limits exist, but routine loads can drift outside evaluated boundaries without detection. Defining and monitoring load boundaries adds procedural time. Setting valid load boundaries requires process expertise. Enforcing load limits has low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified exposure parameters exceed validated autoclave capability

rows.sat_rat: Equipment qualification envelopes are well documented, so specifying beyond capability is rare and readily caught. rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incoming closure bioburden limits conflict with assumptions underlying the sterility assurance and lethality parameters

Reconciling bioburden limits with parameter assumptions is a periodic review activity. Detecting conflicts between limits and assumptions requires cross-functional judgement. Reconciliation consumes analyst time but few materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an upward shift in incoming closure bioburden remains undetected before sterilization parameters are reassessed
rows.imp_rat: Undetected upward bioburden shift before reassessment can defeat the cycle margin, but ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alert-limit trending exists but detection lag means shifts can go unaddressed before reassessment. Trending incoming bioburden for upward shifts is an ongoing monitoring task. Recognizing meaningful upward shifts requires trending and statistical skill. Routine trending consumes ongoing monitoring resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of nominal chamber capacity unavailable because closure load geometry must permit saturated-steam contact

rows.sat_rat: Nominal-versus-usable capacity gaps are known but often underestimated until PQ load studies expose them. Determining usable versus nominal capacity requires load-mapping trials that take time. Load geometry optimization for steam contact needs experienced sterilization engineers. rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a recovery facility rejecting separated components because residual contamination exceeds acceptance requirements
rows.imp_rat: Recovery facility rejecting separated components over residual contamination concerns end-of-life recycling, not the core sterilizing job. Consequences are limited to disposal cost and rework, so relevance is low.
rows.sat_rat: Without standardized pre-shipment verification against recovery criteria, rejections are a fairly frequent unpredictable outcome. rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required evidence missing from the completed cycle record

rows.sat_rat: Automated printouts and electronic records capture most required evidence, though manual entries can still be missed. Completing the record adds documentation time at cycle end. Knowing what evidence is required depends on trained record-keeping. Missing evidence can force reprocessing or reject, wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the cycle without required authorization
rows.imp_rat: Starting a cycle without authorization is a governance breach with compliance stakes but tangential to steam action; relevance 2.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to pressurized steam or hot condensate during abnormal-condition response
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to pressurized steam is a serious safety consequence, but it is peripheral to the sterilization CFJ, so relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and interlocks reduce exposure but abnormal events are inherently less controlled than routine operation. Abnormal-condition response may require waiting for depressurization, costing time. Safe response under abnormal conditions demands trained judgement and composure. Steam/condensate exposure causes injury and downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the chamber remains above the safe opening pressure after an alarm
rows.imp_rat: Chamber remaining above safe opening pressure after alarm is a safety-delay hazard, peripheral to sterilization efficacy; relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Depressurization behavior is predictable, but the wait itself is inherent and not tightly optimized. The duration above safe pressure after an alarm depends on venting/cool-down that can be slow. Little operator skill affects this as it is governed by equipment. Prolonged hold ties up the chamber but consumes little extra material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator actions worsening the abnormal condition before stabilization
rows.imp_rat: Operator actions worsening an abnormal condition is a safety/response issue tangential to steam sterilization; relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm response procedures guide operators but outcomes still hinge heavily on operator competence, so consistency is moderate. Correct stabilization actions may shorten or lengthen response depending on operator. Avoiding actions that worsen the condition is a deep skill/judgement dependence under stress. Wrong actions can escalate damage and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty about chamber pressure, temperature, or door state during the abnormal condition
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about chamber state during an abnormal condition affects safe response; peripheral to the CFJ, relevance 2.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cycle intervals lacking traceable critical parameter records

rows.sat_rat: Electronic dataloggers reliably record all cycle intervals, making untraceable gaps rare where logging is validated. Continuous logging captures intervals without operator time. Little skill needed as recording is automatic. Gaps in traceable records can invalidate a load.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the final load location and the validated chamber position

rows.sat_rat: Fixed racks and load diagrams aid consistent positioning, though manual placement still varies. Positioning to validated location adds modest handling time. Correct placement depends on operator following load maps. Wrong placement can create cold spots and waste the load.

rows.sat_rat: Racks and load patterns support spacing but manual arrangement leaves recurring deviation risk. Ensuring spacing adds a little arranging time during loading. rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury from loss of load control during chamber positioning

Preventing loss of load control depends on operator handling skill and equipment aids. Dropped or uncontrolled loads cause injury and material loss.

rows.sat_rat: Label and batch-record checks help but manual verification leaves recurring misidentification risk. Lot/status verification at setup is a quick check. Correct identification relies on operator reading and matching documentation. Misidentification can mix lots and waste product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of comparing cycle settings with a superseded approved definition
rows.imp_rat: Comparing settings to a superseded definition risks wrong acceptance; a checking error, relevance 2 under cap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misinterpreting parameter units during setup verification
rows.imp_rat: Misinterpreting parameter units at setup could set a wrong cycle; a verification error, relevance 2.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unresolved doubt that all load-acceptance criteria were met

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of closure packages placed outside the specified load pattern

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the closure arrangement shifting during movement of the assembled load
rows.imp_rat: Closure arrangement shifting during load movement can disturb pattern; moderate consequence, severity 2.

rows.sat_rat: Covered staging and clean areas make foreign-matter deposition uncommon though not fully prevented. Covering and protecting staged loads adds minor handling time. Keeping foreign matter off is basic housekeeping discipline. Contamination by foreign matter causes rejection and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of prepared-closure staging beyond the permitted presterilization limit

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prepared closures physically damaged while awaiting sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Physical damage to prepared closures during hold affects downstream integrity; moderate consequence, severity 2.

rows.imp_rat: Change in silicone coating distribution during hold affects downstream function modestly; severity 2 under cap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a completed closure load reaching the autoclave after its scheduled sterilization start

rows.sat_rat: Scheduling systems help but late arrivals to the autoclave still happen under load surges. rows.stmt: Reduce the number of staged closure loads whose identity or processing status conflicts with accompanying records
rows.imp_rat: Staged-load identity/status conflicts risk misprocessing; a records issue, relevance 2 under cap.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling and record accompaniment are mature but occasional mismatches persist. Verifying load identity against records is a quick labeling check. rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of closure loads shifting, tipping, or colliding during autoclave positioning

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterilization outcome dimensions omitted from the acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting outcome dimensions from acceptance criteria is a specification gap; peripheral to steam action, relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized protocol templates and review cycles make omission of critical criteria fairly uncommon. Missing a criterion is cheap to fix on paper but costly if it leads to bad release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acceptance thresholds unsupported by approved validation evidence
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance thresholds unsupported by validation evidence is a defensibility gap; peripheral to steam delivery, relevance 2.

Unsupported thresholds risk regulatory rework rather than direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of validated closure and load configurations not covered by the acceptance criteria

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required release-evidence elements missing from a processed-load record

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of autoclave load records that do not demonstrate fulfillment of validated cycle acceptance criteria

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed sterilization cycles lacking required monitoring evidence

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of closure loads whose disposition evidence is linked to the wrong load

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure-load records lacking required compliance evidence

rows.sat_rat: Batch-record templates and review steps catch many gaps, but missing compliance evidence remains a common paper-system finding. Completing all records adds documentation time to each load. Good documentation depends on operator discipline and remains a frequent source of human error. Documentation labor is a recurring cost but not material-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required cycle evidence that is missing or unusable during deviation diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Missing cycle evidence during deviation diagnosis hampers investigation; retrospective relevance, so relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Required cycle evidence being missing or unusable is a common, only-partly-mitigated deviation problem. Hunting for missing cycle evidence slows deviation closure. Knowing what evidence should exist and is usable requires documentation and QA expertise. Missing evidence drives repeat studies and delayed batch value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cycle data points associated with the wrong load, closure location, sensor, or cycle phase

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in separating inadequate air removal from inadequate exposure as the cause of low closure lethality
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity separating poor air removal from poor exposure aids root cause; relevance to the core job is diagnostic, so relevance 2.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between detection of a failed lethality result and preservation of transient cycle evidence

Knowing which transient signals to preserve quickly needs alert, trained personnel not always present at detection. Lost transient evidence forces costly re-runs or indeterminate dispositions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the lethality deviation cannot be reconstructed from documented cycle and load conditions
rows.imp_rat: Inability to reconstruct a lethality deviation hampers investigation; relevance to the core job is retrospective, relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Reconstructability is often compromised by incomplete or disconnected records, making this a recurring investigation failure. Reconstructing a deviation from records is slow when data is scattered. Rebuilding the event from cycle and load documentation demands investigative expertise. Incomplete reconstruction extends investigations and delays release, raising cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether the specified cycle supports the defined SAL before specification approval
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty whether the cycle supports SAL before approval is an upstream validation concern; relevance 2 under cap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt created by conflicting lethality evidence in the SAL rationale
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting lethality evidence in the SAL rationale creates doubt; interpretive concern, relevance 2 under cap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt about which criterion governs when sterilization requirements conflict
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity over governing criterion when requirements conflict is an interpretive concern; relevance to steam sterilization is limited, so relevance 2.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of viable microorganisms not recovered from sampled closures
rows.imp_rat: Under-recovery from sampled closures distorts bioburden estimates; indirect impact, severity 2 within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Non-recovery of viable organisms from closure surfaces remains a well-known limitation of the method. Recovery from sampled closures follows standard incubation timelines. Effective extraction and recovery from closures requires method expertise. Recovery testing consumes routine lab resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the defined sterility assurance target conflicts with applicable product, quality, or regulatory requirements
rows.imp_rat: SAL target conflicting with regulatory requirements is a governance defect with serious compliance stakes but indirect to steam action; relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Well-established regulatory conventions for SAL make conflicts with requirements uncommon. Checking regulatory alignment of the SAL target is a defined upfront step. Interpreting product and regulatory requirements requires QA expertise. Compliance review is low material cost but high consequence if wrong.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that closure-source or microbial-challenge changes make the sterility assurance target obsolete
rows.imp_rat: Source/challenge changes making the SAL target obsolete undermines assurance basis but is upstream; relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems help, but slow-moving microbial-challenge changes can quietly make targets obsolete. Reassessing targets after challenge changes takes review time. Judging when a change obsoletes the target requires expert change control. Reassessment consumes analyst resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which resistance data omit protective effects from closure geometry, materials, or residues
rows.imp_rat: Resistance data omitting closure protective effects can understate challenge; upstream modeling issue, relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Standard resistance data rarely capture closure-geometry or residue protection, leaving a significant blind spot. Studying protective geometry and residue effects requires extended experimentation. Accounting for protective effects requires deep microbiological and materials expertise. Testing protective effects is resource-intensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a genuinely resistant closure isolate is dismissed as anomalous
rows.imp_rat: Dismissing a genuinely resistant isolate as anomalous could understate challenge; upstream analysis, relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure to dismiss outliers as anomalies risks discarding genuinely resistant isolates. Investigating whether an isolate is truly anomalous adds turnaround time. Distinguishing genuine resistant isolates from anomalies requires expert judgement. Follow-up characterization of suspect isolates adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that extrinsic contamination is mistaken for closure bioburden
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking extrinsic contamination for closure bioburden distorts inputs; testing-integrity concern, relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Controls help, but extrinsic contamination is still sometimes mistaken for closure bioburden. Distinguishing extrinsic contamination requires investigation time. Separating true bioburden from contamination requires experienced interpretation. Negative controls and repeat testing add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of viable microorganisms not recovered during routine incoming-closure bioburden measurement
rows.imp_rat: Under-recovery in routine incoming bioburden measurement biases input data; consequence indirect, severity 2 under cap.
rows.sat_rat: Routine recovery efficiency is inherently imperfect, so a fraction of viable organisms is consistently missed. Routine bioburden recovery methods add standard incubation time. Improving routine recovery depends on trained analysts and validated methods. Recovery media and labor are ongoing costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a closure-source change occurs without reassessing the bioburden and resistance basis
rows.imp_rat: Closure-source change without reassessing resistance basis risks invalid parameters; upstream concern, relevance 2.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier change control usually flags source changes, though the bioburden/resistance basis is not always revisited. Triggering reassessment on source change adds change-control time. Recognizing that a source change requires basis reassessment needs procedural discipline. Reassessment on source change consumes resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of qualifying a supplier unable to achieve the defined SAL for required closure loads under saturated steam
rows.imp_rat: Qualifying an incapable supplier is a procurement risk with delayed impact; relevance to the immediate job is 2.
rows.sat_rat: Standard vendor audits and URS-based prequalification catch many gaps but SAL capability is often accepted on claims rather than demonstrated evidence, leaving real residual risk. Supplier qualification cycles for capital sterilizers take months and rarely accelerate the certainty of SAL capability. Judging whether a supplier can truly hit the SAL demands scarce sterilization-validation expertise that procurement seldom has in-house. The cost of qualifying the wrong supplier is enormous but the direct qualification spend is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of interrupted cycles with incomplete sterilization records
rows.imp_rat: Interrupted cycles with incomplete sterilization records touch both process assurance and documentation, but recordkeeping is peripheral to the core job; severity moderate, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Fault-handling that preserves records through interruptions is inconsistent across installations, so incomplete records after aborts remain a common gap. Interruptions abort logging mid-stream, and reconstructing the partial record afterward consumes operator time. Deciding how to document an interrupted cycle requires operator judgement and QA interpretation that is error-prone. Interrupted cycles often mean scrapped product plus the labor to investigate, a real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading product from the wrong lot into the processing chamber
rows.imp_rat: Loading product from the wrong lot is a handling/identity error adjacent to the sterilization job (low relevance) that could compromise traceability and recalls (moderate severity), but ranking limits it.
rows.sat_rat: Lot verification at load is largely manual with limited scan-to-confirm gating, so wrong-lot loading remains a real risk. Verifying the correct lot before loading adds a short check that is often rushed. Preventing wrong-lot loading depends on operator identification and attention rather than a physical interlock. Loading the wrong lot can spoil an entire batch, a high-cost failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed sterilization cycles with missing critical process parameter records
rows.imp_rat: Missing critical process parameter records is a documentation/compliance concern rather than central to achieving sterilization itself; severity is modest and the item is held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated automatic recording is mature and widely deployed, but power loss, sensor dropout, or unconfigured channels still occasionally leave critical parameters unrecorded. Modern PLC/SCADA controllers log cycle parameters automatically in real time, so capturing records adds no operator time. Automated data logging on retort/sterilizer control systems requires little operator skill once configured, though setup demands a controls specialist. Data historians and validated recorders are a standard, amortized part of the machine cost with modest incremental expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that process data are assigned to the wrong batch cycle
rows.imp_rat: Misassigning process data to the wrong batch cycle is a traceability/record-keeping error that does not stop the machine from sterilizing food; relevance to the core sterilizing job is modest, and severity is bounded since the food itself is still processed.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/MES integration helps where present, but many lines still rely on manual batch selection, so wrong-batch data assignment recurs. Correct batch-to-data association is usually instantaneous when a batch ID is scanned, but manual entry adds delay. When batch assignment is manual, it depends heavily on operator attention and is easily mis-keyed. Misassigned data can force reprocessing or product holds, a meaningful cost when it happens.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cup or lid jams during high-volume processing
rows.imp_rat: Cup or lid jams during high-volume processing hurt throughput (relevance somewhat higher) but each jam is a low-consequence, recoverable event (severity), keeping it under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Modern feed and guide systems keep jam rates moderate at high volume, though occasional jams remain a normal part of running. Jams directly stop flow, so avoiding them protects throughput during peak runs. Clearing and preventing jams depends on operator attentiveness and setup knowledge. Jams cause some scrap and lost productivity but are usually recoverable without large material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surfaces retaining residue from the prior product after changeover
rows.imp_rat: Residue retained from prior product after changeover risks cross-contamination (relevance to conserving food) but is treated as low-consequence and below the prior batch in ordering (severity, ceiling).
rows.sat_rat: CIP and validated cleaning cover most cases, but complex geometries and hard-to-reach surfaces still retain prior-product residue at times. Thorough changeover cleaning to remove residue consumes significant downtime. Effective cleaning and verification requires trained procedures and judgement about acceptable cleanliness. Cleaning consumes water, chemicals and labor, and residual contamination can scrap product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrectly characterizing batch variation in product size, density, or fill level before processing
rows.imp_rat: Mischaracterizing batch variation in size/density before processing can affect cycle adequacy (relevance), but it is a pre-process characterization step ranked below batch 1, so severity stays low.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on periodic sampling and operator judgement rather than inline measurement, so variation is frequently misjudged. Characterizing batch variation before processing takes measurement time that is often skipped. Judging size, density, and fill-level variation reliably demands experienced process expertise that is scarce. Mischaracterization leads to under/over-processing and product loss, a notable cost.

rows.source_job: Register batch identity to cycle record
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cycle records associated with another batch's identity
rows.imp_rat: Cycle records carrying another batch's identity is a record-integrity error peripheral to physically conserving the food; relevance is modest and severity is contained to traceability confusion.
rows.sat_rat: Identity mix-ups persist wherever batch linkage is not interlocked, a common and consequential gap. Correct identity association is fast under automated systems but manual reconciliation adds effort. Preventing cross-batch identity errors relies on disciplined operator procedure where automation is absent. Records tied to another batch's identity cause traceability failures with high downstream cost.

rows.source_job: Register batch identity to cycle record
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that processing begins before the loaded batch identity is associated with the cycle record
rows.imp_rat: Processing beginning before batch identity is linked to the cycle record is a traceability sequencing gap that does not stop the food being sterilized; relevance is modest and severity is bounded to record repair.
rows.sat_rat: Some controllers block start until a batch ID is entered, but many still allow processing to begin unlinked. Associating identity before start adds a brief step that operators sometimes bypass to save time. Requiring identity-before-start relies on procedural discipline unless the control enforces it. Starting without linked identity produces unidentifiable product that may be scrapped.

rows.source_job: Register batch identity to cycle record
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the loaded batch identity is associated with multiple active cycle records
rows.imp_rat: A loaded batch identity tied to multiple active cycle records is a data-association ambiguity that does not impair sterilizing; relevance to the core job is low and consequences are record reconciliation rather than food harm.
rows.sat_rat: Few status-quo systems enforce single-cycle-per-batch constraints, so this ambiguity recurs and is poorly guarded. Detecting a duplicate active-cycle association is not time-consuming when systems flag it, but manual checking is slow. Ensuring one batch maps to one cycle requires tracking that overtaxes operator attention on busy lines. Duplicate active records confuse traceability and can waste QA investigation time and product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the displayed cycle results are associated with the wrong batch
rows.imp_rat: Displaying cycle results against the wrong batch is a data-labeling defect adjacent to the sterilizing job; relevance is limited and the consequence is a records mixup rather than unsterilized food, keeping severity moderate.
rows.sat_rat: HMI batch display is standard, but without enforced ID linkage the shown batch can still be wrong on manually operated machines. Displayed results map to a batch instantly once the identity is set, adding no time. Confirming the displayed batch is correct still relies on operator vigilance rather than a foolproof interlock. A mislabeled result batch can trigger costly investigations or recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product-contact surfaces becoming recontaminated between sanitation and production
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination of product-contact surfaces between sanitation and production is relevant to conserving food but is capped by the batch ceiling; its consequences here are treated as modest (severity) and its centrality moderate (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Hold-time limits and covered-surface practice help, but recontamination in the gap between sanitation and startup remains a persistent recurring gap. Minimizing the sanitation-to-production gap pressures scheduling and can force re-sanitation if delays occur. Maintaining protected surfaces during the hold window requires disciplined handling practice. Re-sanitation and lost production when the window lapses are real recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering sterile product-contact areas during maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant reaching sterile product-contact areas is a near-duplicate contamination risk with moderate relevance to the sterilizing job, but held under the ceiling with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Food-grade practice and sealed bearing designs limit incidents, yet lubricant reaching sterile surfaces remains an intermittent contamination risk. Careful application and cleanup near sterile-contact zones add small increments of service time.
