Preventing lubricant migration into sterile zones depends on meticulous technician technique and placement judgement. Food-grade lubricants and any re-sanitation after over-application add modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of sterilization and cooling parameters from validated settings after the update
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of sterilization/cooling parameters from validated settings after an update touches the core job, but as a post-update verification concern within this low-ranked batch its relevance and severity are held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and post-update verification are standard in regulated food plants, keeping parameter deviation uncommon though not eliminated. Post-update requalification runs add scheduled but bounded time before release. Verifying parameters match validated settings needs qualified validation personnel and correct reference documentation. Requalification consumes test product, energy and staff hours at moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unsafe operating-parameter excursion remaining undetected
rows.imp_rat: An undetected unsafe parameter excursion is important for safety but sits below the batch-1 items in ranking (low relevance here); undetected excursions could harm product or people (moderate severity), held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated critical-parameter monitoring with alarms makes an undetected unsafe excursion uncommon in properly instrumented machines. Continuous monitoring and alarming detect excursions in real time without added procedural delay. Interpreting and responding to excursion alarms needs trained operators but is well-supported by instrumentation. Sensors, data logging and alarm systems carry setup and calibration costs that are routinely absorbed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the scheduled maintenance scope and the machine's maintenance requirements
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between scheduled maintenance scope and actual requirements is a planning defect adjacent to reliability; relevance to the sterilizing job is modest and severity is bounded to deferred servicing.
rows.sat_rat: OEM-based maintenance plans and CMMS cover the norm, but generic schedules still miss machine-specific or emerging requirements. Aligning scheduled scope to actual needs is a planning task that rarely delays the work itself. Matching maintenance scope to the machine's real requirements depends on planners' experience and accurate equipment history. Scope mismatch causes either wasted over-maintenance or costly missed tasks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting a steam, water, or compressed-air line to the wrong machine inlet
rows.imp_rat: Connecting a line to the wrong inlet is an installation error affecting setup, but usually caught during commissioning and peripheral to the running job; kept at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Distinct fitting sizes, color coding and labeled inlets make wrong-inlet connection fairly rare in standard practice. Matching lines to inlets is quick when connections are labeled and keyed. Correct hookup relies on following labels rather than special skill. A cross-connected utility can damage the machine, an avoidable and infrequent cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety devices assigned settings intended for another machine location
rows.imp_rat: Safety devices set for another machine location could fail to protect (some severity), yet it is a configuration/setup edge case rather than central to the running job, so relevance is low.
rows.sat_rat: Setting sheets and validation help, but transposed or generic safety-device settings from another location still occur during preparation. Verifying every safety device setting against the correct location adds detailed configuration time. Assigning correct safety settings requires knowledge of the specific installation, a location-specific expertise. Wrong safety settings risk unsafe operation with potentially large consequential cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to steam, hot surfaces, or pressurized media during hands-on training
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to steam or hot surfaces during hands-on training is a bounded training-time safety issue rather than core to sterilizing food; capped low despite injury potential.
rows.sat_rat: PPE, lockout, guarding and supervised training substantially limit operator exposure, though hands-on training still carries residual burn/pressure risk. Safety briefings and controlled hands-on exposure add modest planned time to training. Avoiding burns and pressure exposure during training depends on trainer discipline and PPE use rather than rare skill. Injuries carry high cost but are infrequent when precautions are followed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of operators unable to correctly explain and execute required control and safety actions after training
rows.imp_rat: Operators unable to execute control and safety actions after training affects safe running somewhat, but is a training-competency issue rather than the machine's core function; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured curricula and competency checks help, but a meaningful fraction of operators still cannot correctly execute required actions afterward. Training and verifying operator competence takes substantial scheduled time before the machine can be run. The whole point is to build scarce control-and-safety competence into operators, and outcomes vary widely with trainer quality. Retraining and errors from under-trained operators impose ongoing labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile-contact areas becoming contaminated during hands-on training
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating sterile-contact areas during training risks sterility but is confined to a training scenario peripheral to production; held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Covers, protocols and supervision help, but hands-on training on live sterile surfaces frequently risks contamination. Protecting or re-cleaning sterile-contact areas around training adds time to the preparation window. Keeping trainees from contaminating sterile-contact surfaces requires disciplined aseptic handling that trainees by definition lack. Recontamination forces re-cleaning or re-sterilization, a recurring cost during training.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of particulate or biological contamination introduced into the sterile fill-finish area during connection work
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during connection work threatens sterility but occurs in an installation/service context peripheral to routine operation; capped at the batch ceiling despite real severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cleanroom protocols and gowning reduce ingress, but physical connection work in a sterile zone still frequently introduces particulate or biological contamination. Cleaning and controlling contamination introduced during connection work adds time to preparation. Keeping a sterile fill-finish area clean during connection work demands disciplined aseptic technique that is skill-intensive. Contamination events can force re-sterilization and product loss, a significant intermittent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process effluent backing up into the machine during discharge
rows.imp_rat: Process effluent backing up during discharge is a contamination risk that could touch the conserved food, but as a discharge-phase concern in this low-ranked batch its relevance and severity are held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Air gaps, check valves and proper sizing generally prevent effluent backup, though peak surges still occasionally cause it. Sizing and sloping discharge lines to prevent backup is a normal, bounded design/install step. Preventing backflow relies on standard drainage design knowledge that is broadly available. Backup causes contamination and cleanup costs that are occasional rather than constant.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of facility drainage overflowing during peak machine discharge
rows.imp_rat: Drainage overflow during peak discharge is a facility-utility concern rather than central to the sterilize/cool job (low relevance); an overflow can cause hygiene and safety issues (moderate severity), but ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Facility drainage is generally sized with margin, but peak simultaneous discharge occasionally overwhelms existing drains. Checking facility drain capacity against peak discharge is a modest planning task. Matching machine peak flow to drain capacity requires basic hydraulic sizing skill widely held by facility engineers. Overflow causes cleanup and downtime costs that are intermittent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process effluent being routed to an incompatible facility drainage destination
rows.imp_rat: Routing effluent to an incompatible drainage destination is peripheral to sterilizing and cooling food (low relevance), though a misrouted discharge could cause environmental/compliance harm (moderate severity). Capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Site drawings and permits usually direct effluent correctly, but mismatches to incompatible destinations still happen during preparation. Confirming the drainage destination during install is a quick verification against site drawings. Judging effluent-destination compatibility needs facility and regulatory knowledge that a competent site engineer holds. Wrong routing can trigger regulatory fines and remediation, a large but rare cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that machine configuration discrepancies against approved specifications go unrecognized
rows.imp_rat: Unrecognized configuration discrepancies against approved specs is a compliance/verification defect that does not directly stop sterilizing; relevance to the core job is modest and severity is bounded to audit exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and FAT/SAT records help, but subtle configuration deviations against spec routinely go unnoticed. Verifying configuration against specifications is a slow manual audit that is often abbreviated. Recognizing configuration discrepancies requires an expert who deeply knows the approved spec, a scarce competence. Unrecognized misconfiguration leads to failed batches whose cost is high but sporadic.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of cup or lid material degradation caused by the configured sterilization cycle
rows.imp_rat: Cup or lid material degradation from the cycle affects packing integrity somewhat, but is peripheral to the core sterilize-and-cool job and consequences are contained product-cosmetic; capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recommended cycle profiles help, but matching them to specific cup/lid materials during preparation is imperfect and degradation still occurs. Validating that a cycle does not degrade cup or lid material requires trial runs that consume setup time. Judging material compatibility with thermal cycles needs packaging and process expertise that is not always on hand. Material degradation scraps packaging and product, a direct recurring cost when parameters are wrong.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a stored recipe is associated with the wrong product type
rows.imp_rat: Associating a stored recipe with the wrong product type could cause an incorrect sterilization cycle, relevant to the CFJ, but as a configuration/administrative error it is largely catchable before harm; capped low per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems reduce mis-association, but manual selection and loose naming conventions leave a real error rate. Selecting and confirming the correct recipe for a product is quick when the recipe library is organized. Correct association relies on operator attention and product knowledge rather than deep expertise. A mismatched recipe can spoil a whole batch, a costly but avoidable event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among approved criteria used to judge cycle results
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among criteria for judging cycle results touch on validating sterilization (relevance), but as a definitional ambiguity ranked below batch 1, severity is held down.
rows.sat_rat: There is little standard machinery for harmonizing approved cycle-result criteria, so contradictory criteria frequently persist unresolved. Reconciling conflicting acceptance criteria is a slow negotiation often left unfinished before startup. Resolving criteria conflicts requires senior process and quality judgement that is scarce. Unresolved conflicts cause disputed batches and rework whose cost is diffuse.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a machine sub-assembly shifting or falling while components are being joined
rows.imp_rat: A sub-assembly shifting or falling during joining is a servicing safety/damage risk (some severity), but far from the operational sterilizing job, so relevance is minimal and the mean stays under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Lifting fixtures, temporary supports and rigging practice keep sub-assembly shifts or falls infrequent. Securing sub-assemblies before joining is a standard step that adds little time. Rigging and bracing components during assembly uses common mechanical-fitting skills. A dropped sub-assembly damages parts and risks injury, costly but uncommon events.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable temperature or pressure information during the sterilization cycle
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable temperature/pressure readings during the cycle affect monitoring rather than the sterilizing action itself (low relevance) but blind operation risks unverified lethality (moderate severity), capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Live monitoring is mature and reliable, but sensor faults cause brief data gaps until failover or repair. Continuous real-time temperature and pressure display keeps unavailable-information gaps very short on functioning instruments. Reading live gauges/HMI requires minimal skill, though diagnosing sensor loss needs a technician. Redundant sensors add cost but are common on validated retorts, limiting blackout duration cheaply.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unloaded cups and lids are assigned to the wrong batch before packing
rows.imp_rat: Assigning unloaded cups and lids to the wrong batch before packing is a downstream traceability ambiguity, not a sterilizing failure; relevance to the CFJ is low and severity is contained to labeling mixups.
rows.sat_rat: The transfer from chamber to packing is typically manual with weak identity carry-over, making wrong-batch assignment a frequent hazard. Reassigning unloaded product to its batch is a quick physical step but easy to skip under time pressure. Keeping unloaded cups/lids matched to their batch before packing depends almost entirely on manual discipline. Misassignment at unload can spoil traceability of finished goods, a costly rework or scrap trigger.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterilization records linked to the wrong cup or lid batch
rows.imp_rat: Linking sterilization records to the wrong cup or lid batch is a documentation traceability defect that does not affect the actual sterilization; relevance to the CFJ is low and severity is limited to recall/audit inconvenience.
rows.sat_rat: Component-level (cup/lid) traceability is less commonly automated than product batch, so wrong-batch linkage is a recurring weak point. Linking records to cup/lid batches is quick when material traceability is scanned, slower when logged by hand. Correctly tying component batches to a cycle demands careful cross-checking that is easy to get wrong manually. Wrong component-batch linkage undermines traceability and can invalidate lots, an expensive failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the sterile product path during component replacement
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the sterile path during component replacement is relevant to the conserve-food CFJ, but as a maintenance-window event it is capped low here; severity kept minimal per the batch ceiling despite potential spoilage.
rows.sat_rat: Established SIP-then-verify routines make outcomes fairly consistent, but component-replacement events remain a known source of intermittent contamination excursions. Guarding the sterile product path during component swaps forces slow, staged cleaning and re-sterilization cycles that extend service windows. Preventing path contamination during replacement relies on experienced aseptic-maintenance practice that is not uniformly available across shifts. Re-sterilization media, downtime and scrapped first-batch product make breaches costly, though standard practice contains most incidents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of corrosion on product-contact and internal machine surfaces during storage
rows.imp_rat: Corrosion on product-contact surfaces during storage could later affect hygiene, but it is a dormant-state maintenance issue peripheral to active sterilizing; held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Stainless surfaces plus standard passivation control most corrosion, though damp crevices and long storage still produce occasional pitting. Protective conditioning before storage is quick, but periodic inspection over long idle periods adds recurring time. Recognizing early corrosion and applying correct passivation/coatings requires trained maintenance eyes. Corrosion inhibitors, desiccants and dry-storage measures are modest ongoing costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a nonfunctional safeguard passing periodic testing
rows.imp_rat: A nonfunctional safeguard passing testing is a safety-verification defect somewhat removed from the core sterilization function (low relevance) yet could hide dangerous failures (moderate severity); constrained by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard proof-testing catches most faults, but latent failure modes passing periodic tests remain a genuine residual risk. Periodic safeguard testing is scheduled and does not itself introduce meaningful delay. Detecting a safeguard that passes a test yet would fail in service requires deep functional-safety expertise that is scarce. Proper functional testing costs test rigs and skilled labour but far less than a safeguard failure event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to hazardous motion, pressure, heat, or steam during testing
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to hazardous motion, pressure, heat or steam during testing is a safety concern with potentially serious consequences (severity), but it is peripheral to the machine's core job of sterilizing and cooling food (relevance), and the ranking caps this below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks, guarding and test SOPs make injury during motion/steam testing uncommon where enforced, though rushed tests still create exposure. Guarded test sequences and safe-standback requirements add controlled delay during commissioning tests. Safe test execution follows established procedures that most maintenance staff are trained on. Guarding, barriers and PPE for testing are minor recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile processing conditions being contaminated during safeguard testing
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating sterile conditions during safeguard testing touches the conserving-food outcome (relevance) but is a testing-context edge case with limited consequence here (severity), capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Test-under-clean-conditions procedures contain most risk, but safeguard testing remains an intermittent source of sterile-condition compromise. Safeguard testing in sterile conditions requires clean-access setup that adds preparation time. Testing safeguards without breaching sterility depends on trained personnel following clean-test protocols. Area re-sanitation after intrusive testing adds modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cleaned food-contact surfaces becoming recontaminated before production resumes
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination of cleaned food-contact surfaces before restart is a hygiene concern somewhat removed from the machine's sterilize/cool cycle (low relevance) but can compromise product safety (moderate severity); held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice of closing lines and short hold windows limits recontamination, but open equipment and delays still cause occasional re-soiling. Protecting cleaned surfaces means restarting promptly, which pressures the window between cleaning and production. Preventing recontamination depends on trained staff observing hygiene zoning and closure discipline. Covers, sanitized closures and prompt restart are low-cost measures against contamination loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial contamination remaining on internal chamber surfaces after sanitation
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination remaining on chamber surfaces after sanitation directly undermines the sterilize goal (relevance), yet the batch ranking forces severity low to keep the mean at the 2.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/sanitation plus environmental monitoring make residual microbial survival uncommon, though occasional positive swabs still force re-cleaning. Validated sanitation cycles have fixed dwell times, and confirmatory swabbing adds turnaround delay. Consistent coverage and correct sanitant concentration depend on trained sanitation staff. Sanitant chemicals and microbiological verification testing are routine recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous chemical or thermal exposure to the sanitation technician while treating internal chambers
rows.imp_rat: Chemical or thermal exposure to the sanitation technician is a genuine safety hazard (severity), yet peripheral to executing the core sterilizing function, so relevance is low and the mean respects the cap.
rows.sat_rat: SDS-driven procedures and interlocks make exposure incidents fairly rare, but manual chemical handling leaves a persistent residual hazard. Chemical sanitation and thermal cooldown of chambers add fixed dwell and rinse time to the service. Safely handling hot chambers and caustic/acidic sanitants leans on trained sanitation technicians whose judgement about dosing and PPE drives the outcome. PPE, sanitant chemicals and controlled disposal are routine recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leakage from newly installed seals or gaskets when the sterilizing machine returns to service
rows.imp_rat: Leakage from newly installed seals when returning to service can compromise pressure and sterility (moderate relevance), but this is a post-maintenance defect ranked low, so severity is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Hydrostatic/pressure test-before-restart is mature standard practice that catches most leaks, so post-service leakage is now relatively uncommon. Post-installation pressure and integrity testing before return-to-service adds a defined but manageable delay. Correct gasket seating and torque require trained fitters, though torque specs and tools reduce reliance on judgement. Repeat teardown to fix a leak wastes labor and product, but this is uncommon with good practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating sterile fluid paths while replacing seals, gaskets, or utility filters
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating sterile fluid paths during seal/filter replacement is a maintenance-hygiene risk peripheral to the operational job (low relevance) yet could introduce product contamination (moderate severity), capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: SIP protocols and post-service integrity tests catch many breaches, yet occasional latent contamination from imperfect technique still slips through, leaving a real recurring gap. Aseptic seal-swap procedures add significant downtime for cleaning and re-sterilization before restart, which is accepted but not fast. Correctly maintaining sterility during seal and filter replacement demands trained aseptic technicians whose discipline varies, so error rates depend heavily on operator skill. Consumables, sterile filters and re-validation runs add real recurring cost but are budgeted as routine maintenance spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage to thermal circuit surfaces or seals during descaling
rows.imp_rat: Damage to thermal circuit surfaces or seals during descaling touches maintenance reliability that supports the sterilizing job, but as a maintenance-time defect its relevance is modest and severity is bounded to eventual repair.
rows.sat_rat: Manufacturer-specified descaling parameters reduce damage, but variable scale loads and operator deviation still cause intermittent seal harm. Careful controlled descaling to protect seals lengthens the procedure versus an aggressive flush. Choosing correct acid strength, temperature and dwell to avoid attacking seals and thermal surfaces relies heavily on technician expertise. Damaged seals or corroded surfaces from over-aggressive descaling create real repair and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering food-contact or sterile processing areas during maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering food-contact areas during maintenance compromises conservation (moderate relevance), but as a maintenance contamination event ranked below batch 1, severity is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Food-grade lubricant standards and controlled application reduce risk substantially, but migration into contact areas still occasionally occurs. Using food-grade lubricants and wiping excess adds minor time to lubrication tasks. Applying the right amount of food-grade lubricant in the right places relies on trained technician care. Food-grade lubricants cost more than industrial ones, a modest accepted premium.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exposure to residual heat, pressure, or hazardous energy during subsystem service
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to residual heat or hazardous energy during service carries real technician-harm severity, but it is a servicing safety concern rather than central to the sterilization job, keeping the average at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature energy-isolation procedures make residual-energy exposure uncommon where they are enforced, though lapses still occur on rushed jobs. Lockout-tagout and cooldown waits add mandatory delay before subsystem service can begin. LOTO and stored-energy discharge are widely trained standard skills, lowering dependence on scarce expertise. PPE and downtime for depressurization are minor, well-absorbed costs relative to the injury they prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants into product-contact areas during subsystem service
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants into product-contact areas during service undermines conservation, giving moderate relevance, but as a maintenance error it is ranked below batch 1 and severity is held down to stay under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard cleaning-after-service verification catches most issues, yet subsystem service remains a recurring recontamination risk point. Protecting product-contact areas during service requires slow covering, cleaning and re-sanitation steps that stretch downtime. Avoiding contaminant introduction depends on disciplined hygienic-maintenance technique that varies by technician. Re-cleaning media and lost startup product make lapses costly, though contained by routine practice.

rows.source_job: Test safety relief and interlock devices
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an uncontrolled pressure or steam release during device actuation
rows.imp_rat: An uncontrolled pressure/steam release during actuation is an operator-safety hazard tangential to the food-conservation outcome (low relevance) yet potentially injurious (moderate severity); capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Mature pressure-safety procedures and relief-valve testing standards make uncontrolled release during actuation an infrequent event. Pressure-system isolation and depressurization before actuation testing follow set, bounded procedures. Safe actuation of steam/pressure devices requires trained pressure-equipment personnel who are generally available in these plants. PPE, isolation hardware and procedures are modest costs against the hazard.

rows.source_job: Test safety relief and interlock devices
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a tested safety device remaining bypassed or incorrectly reset after testing
rows.imp_rat: A safety device left bypassed after testing is a maintenance-procedure error peripheral to the sterilize/cool job itself (low relevance) but carries real hazard exposure (moderate severity), kept under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout/permit systems and post-test verification checklists make this well-controlled, though human slips still occasionally leave bypasses. Restoring devices to service after testing is a brief step in the procedure. Ensuring nothing is left bypassed or mis-reset depends on disciplined technicians following restore checklists. A left-bypassed device costs little to prevent but can cause expensive incidents if missed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residual fluids during drainage
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residual fluids during drainage is a servicing safety hazard away from the core job; severity is real but the ranking caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Established PPE and SDS practice reduces exposure, but residual hazardous fluids still catch technicians when procedures are shortcut. PPE donning and cautious draining add handling time that is generally tolerated. Avoiding technician exposure hinges on hazmat handling competence and vigilance that is unevenly present. PPE and containment for drainage are inexpensive, standard consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible residual fluids being mixed during drainage
rows.imp_rat: Mixing incompatible residual fluids during drainage is a maintenance/decommissioning event far from the core sterilizing job; while it carries some hazard, the ranking caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: SDS-driven segregation practice exists but relies on manual identification, so incompatible mixing still occurs when residues are unlabeled. Segregating incompatible fluids into separate containers slows the drainage sequence. Knowing which residual chemistries must not be mixed requires chemical-compatibility knowledge not every technician holds. Separate waste streams and extra containers add disposal cost but avoid reaction hazards.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that biological contamination remains in the machine after sanitation
rows.imp_rat: Residual biological contamination after sanitation directly threatens the sterilizing job, yet the ranking places it below batch 1; relevance nudged slightly higher than severity while both stay under the 2.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Operational sanitation is mature, but verifying no biological residue remains after final sanitation for disposal is inconsistently done and often unconfirmed. Sanitation cycles and hold times before confirming cleanliness extend the schedule. Confirming residual biological contamination is removed requires microbiological validation expertise not resident in disposal crews. Sanitizing agents and swab/verification testing add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of worker exposure to hazardous product, cleaning-agent, or sterilant residues while separating machine components
rows.imp_rat: Worker exposure to hazardous residues while separating components is a real safety hazard, but it occurs during maintenance and is peripheral to the CFJ of sterilizing food; the batch ceiling caps its weight despite meaningful severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PPE practice mitigates but does not eliminate exposure when trapped residues surface during disassembly. Cleaning components before separation adds handling time to the teardown. Safe component separation without residue exposure depends on PPE discipline and hazard training that varies by crew. PPE and cleaning consumables are cheap and readily available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the machine being damaged in transit to the manufacturer
rows.imp_rat: Transit damage to the machine before it reaches the manufacturer is a logistics/administrative concern peripheral to the CFJ of sterilizing and cooling food; relevance is very low and consequences, while costly, are absorbed by shipping insurance rather than harming food safety.
rows.sat_rat: Professional rigging and packaging practice makes transit damage fairly uncommon, though sensitive components still occasionally arrive damaged. Proper crating and securing take time that logistics vendors normally build in. Machinery crating and rigging is a mature specialist trade widely available. Crating, blocking and insurance are standard, budgetable shipping costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unplanned contractual charges incurred when canceling services assigned to the food sterilizing machine
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned contractual charges from canceling services is a purely administrative/financial concern peripheral to the sterilizing job. Consequences are minor cost overruns, easily absorbed, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Contract terms are documented so avoidable charges are catchable, but coordination gaps make surprises common. Reviewing contracts before cancellation takes time that is often deprioritized during shutdown. Interpreting service-contract cancellation clauses requires procurement or legal judgement not always engaged for a single machine. The whole error is unplanned money lost to early-termination penalties, which frequently surprise the disposal budget.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from unreleased energy or pressure during decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Technician injury from unreleased energy during decommissioning is a serious but end-of-life safety event peripheral to routine sterilizing; capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and stored-energy release practice makes injury from unreleased energy uncommon where the procedure is followed. Energy verification and bleed-down steps add measured time to the decommissioning start. Stored-energy release is governed by trained LOTO practitioners, a well-established competency. Lockout hardware and verification are low-cost, universally available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterilant or product residues being released into the surrounding facility during decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Sterilant or product residue release during decommissioning is a one-time end-of-life hazard, not central to the ongoing CFJ; some severity but capped by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning protocols exist for operation but decommissioning-specific residue release is inconsistently controlled and frequently escapes into the facility. Thorough residue neutralization before decommissioning extends the shutdown window. Removing sterilant and product residues fully requires cleaning-validation expertise that disposal crews rarely have. Cleaning agents, rinse verification and effluent handling carry real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an electrical, steam, pneumatic, or thermal energy source is omitted from the isolation scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting an energy source from isolation scope is a lockout/decommissioning safety ambiguity peripheral to sterilizing food; relevance to the CFJ is low though severity of an energy oversight is non-trivial.
rows.sat_rat: Energy-control procedures and checklists catch most sources, yet hidden thermal or pneumatic energy is occasionally omitted from scope. Building a complete isolation scope takes upfront energy-survey time that is sometimes rushed. Identifying every energy source requires thorough engineering review of drawings and the physical machine. The survey is low-cost but an omitted source can cause expensive incidents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous stored energy remains in the food sterilizing machine after isolation
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous stored energy remaining after isolation can cause injury (severity moderate) but relates to servicing safety rather than the core conserving-food job (relevance low), and is capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and pressure-relief practice handles most stored-energy cases, but heat-transfer media and steam systems occasionally retain hazardous energy. Lockout-tagout and energy-isolation procedures add a modest fixed time to decommissioning. Trained maintenance technicians follow established LOTO practice, though residual thermal or pressure energy in sterilizers still needs judgement. Isolation consumables and labor are minor costs relative to the risk they mitigate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an isolation point permits energy transmission while locked
rows.imp_rat: An isolation point permitting energy transmission while locked is a lockout safety defect during maintenance rather than the sterilizing job; relevance to the CFJ is low though the consequence of energy escape carries some severity.
rows.sat_rat: Verify-zero-energy practice catches most defective isolation points, though degraded valves or breakers can still pass energy while locked. Post-isolation verification testing adds a brief confirmation step. Try-out verification that a locked point truly blocks energy needs trained diligence to perform correctly. Verification uses existing meters and gauges at negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of isolating the wrong electrical feeder, steam line, or pneumatic line for the food sterilizing machine
rows.imp_rat: Isolating the wrong feeder or line is a servicing safety error peripheral to routine sterilizing operation; consequences can be moderate but the item sits below the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO identification and labeling practices make correct isolation usually reliable, though mislabeled or shared lines still cause occasional errors. Verifying the correct feeder or line before isolation adds tracing time up front. Correct identification depends on electricians and pipefitters reading drawings, a mature but skill-dependent task. Mislabeled isolation is cheap to prevent with tagging yet costly if wrong.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an energy source is restored before decommissioning work is complete
rows.imp_rat: Restoring an energy source before decommissioning is complete is a safety-timing instability during teardown, not during the sterilizing job; relevance to the CFJ is low even if the consequence could be injury.
rows.sat_rat: Established LOTO makes premature re-energizing rare where locks are individually applied and removed, a well-controlled outcome. Group lockout and verification before re-energizing add controlled time to the process. Lock/tag/verify discipline is a widely trained competency among qualified technicians. Personal locks and tags are trivially cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of associating the completed cycle record with the wrong closure load
rows.imp_rat: Associating a cycle record with the wrong closure load risks release-decision errors, yet it ranks lower than prior items and is held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch numbering and record linkage help but manual association leaves residual mix-up risk. Associating record to load is a quick tagging step. Depends on operator correctly linking record and batch. Wrong association risks releasing the wrong load, a costly error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of associating the wrong closure load with the selected cycle
rows.imp_rat: Associating the wrong closure load with the selected cycle risks misprocessing, yet it ranks below prior batch and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Load sheets and recipe selection help but the link is largely manually maintained. Linking load to selected cycle is a brief step. Relies on operator matching the physical load to the chosen recipe. Wrong association can cause improper processing and waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable critical parameter readings during the cycle
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable critical parameter readings during the cycle threaten monitoring of lethality, but ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant sensors and continuous logging keep reading dropouts brief and rare on validated systems. Duration of unavailable parameter readings during a cycle is a direct time/monitoring gap. Little skill involved; it depends on instrumentation availability. Reading gaps can force load rejection, wasting material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attributing critical parameter data to the wrong load or cycle
rows.imp_rat: Attributing critical parameter data to the wrong load undermines record integrity for sterilization, but the ranking places it below prior batch; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cycle-numbered records reduce misattribution, though manual load-ID entry can still cross records. Attributing data to the right load is automatic with cycle tagging. Some operator care needed to enter correct load identifiers. Misattributed data can invalidate the wrong load, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing processed load identity or sterilization status during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Losing processed load identity or status during transfer risks release errors, yet this handling error ranks lower and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Status labels and segregated transfer routes help, but identity loss during transfer remains a recurring risk. Maintaining identity through transfer adds handling and labeling time. Preserving identity depends on operator labeling discipline. Lost identity can force rejection of processed closures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure loads with total closure quantity outside the specified limits
rows.imp_rat: Total closure quantity outside limits can affect load lethality, but ranking caps this error at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Defined load quantities and counting checks keep quantity excursions uncommon. Counting or confirming quantity adds minor time. Quantity verification is a simple counting/weighing task. Over/under-filled loads may need re-staging.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of closure loads containing unauthorized combinations of lots or formats
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized lot/format combinations could invalidate the load, but the ranking caps this below the prior batch at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation practices and labeling make unauthorized combinations infrequent. Verifying lot/format compatibility is a quick record check. Avoiding unauthorized combinations relies on following segregation rules. Mixed lots can cause quarantine of whole loads, a significant cost when it happens.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of assembled loads extending beyond the approved load envelope
rows.imp_rat: Loads exceeding the approved envelope can compromise steam penetration, but the ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed racks and envelope specs make envelope overruns rare. Checking against the load envelope adds negligible time. Confirming envelope compliance is a basic dimensional check. Oversized loads may fail to fit or sterilize, causing rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prepared closure lots with untraceable identity before sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable lot identity before sterilization risks mislabeled release, but the ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Lot labeling and batch records make untraceable identity rare in practice. Applying and verifying identity labels adds a quick step. Traceability labeling is routine clerical work. Lost traceability can force scrapping otherwise good lots.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the limits and evaluation conditions used to judge cycle compliance
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in limits used to judge cycle compliance affects accept/reject decisions, but ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Well-written protocols reduce ambiguity, but interpretive gray zones persist and drive inconsistent calls. Ambiguity is resolved at authoring or during disputes, not per cycle. Writing unambiguous limits and evaluation conditions is a specialized documentation skill and residual ambiguity forces interpretive judgement at review. Ambiguous limits cause inconsistent decisions and occasional rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between the acceptance criteria and approved validation conclusions or governing specifications
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between acceptance criteria and validation conclusions are documentation inconsistencies; both proxies low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Document review catches most conflicts, though subtle inconsistencies between documents do slip through. Reconciling criteria with validation conclusions is a one-time authoring/review effort. Detecting conflicts between acceptance criteria and governing specs demands careful expert cross-referencing that is error-prone. Unresolved conflicts risk misjudgement costs but rarely waste material directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that measurement uncertainty changes a cycle classification near an acceptance limit
rows.imp_rat: Measurement uncertainty flipping a borderline classification is a marginal edge case; both proxies low under cap.
rows.sat_rat: Measurement uncertainty is seldom formally incorporated at the acceptance boundary, so near-limit calls remain a recurring reliability soft spot. Near-limit decisions do not add routine time but require deliberate handling when they arise. Judging borderline cycles against measurement uncertainty is a nuanced skill many reviewers apply inconsistently. Misclassifying a borderline cycle wrongly scraps or wrongly releases a load, both costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the criteria reject a cycle satisfying the validated sterilization outcome
rows.imp_rat: Criteria wrongly rejecting a valid cycle wastes product but does not threaten sterility; both proxies low under cap.
rows.sat_rat: Conservative criteria occasionally reject compliant cycles, but this false-reject rate is generally low and tolerated. False rejects are handled at review and mainly cost re-processing time. Distinguishing a truly failing cycle from an acceptable one near limits requires judgement. Unnecessarily rejecting compliant cycles wastes good loads and re-run energy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of conflicting information in inputs used to assess a validated-state change
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting inputs for validated-state changes create ambiguity, but this administrative-assessment error is peripheral to the SAL job and stays at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is limited systematic control to detect conflicting inputs, so this is inconsistently managed. Resolving conflicting inputs adds assessment time when it occurs. Reconciling conflicting source information demands experienced judgement to sort out. Acting on conflicting data risks costly wrong decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unauthorized alterations to critical autoclave control-system parameters
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized alterations to control-system parameters could compromise cycle lethality, but ranking holds it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access and audit trails on control parameters make unauthorized alterations uncommon in validated systems. Detecting unauthorized parameter changes is an audit-trail review, not a delay. Access-control configuration needs IT/automation expertise but is set once. Unauthorized changes can invalidate cycles at high cost, which drives controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that closure quantity, arrangement, or packaging differs from the validated configuration at disposition
rows.imp_rat: Configuration differing from validated at disposition could invalidate sterility claims, yet it ranks lower here and stays capped.
rows.sat_rat: Batch records and loading documentation make configuration mismatches at disposition uncommon. Configuration verification at disposition is a routine record check. Confirming quantity/arrangement/packaging matches validation relies on reviewer attention. Configuration mismatches at disposition can scrap loads.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cycle evidence is linked to the wrong closure lot or processed load
rows.imp_rat: Cycle evidence linked to the wrong lot risks releasing on wrong data, yet it ranks lower and is held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Load identification systems make wrong-lot linkage uncommon, with residual risk from manual entry. Correct linkage is established at load setup, not a recurring delay. Preventing mis-linkage depends on operator care in labeling and data entry. Mislinked evidence can misdirect dispositions at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a closure lot cannot be traced through its autoclave load, cycle evidence, and disposition
rows.imp_rat: A lot untraceable through load/cycle/disposition undermines release integrity, but the ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Lot-genealogy practices generally allow tracing through load, evidence and disposition, though hybrid systems create occasional breaks. End-to-end traceability is retrieved during audits or investigations, not routinely. Reconstructing a lot's full trace requires familiarity with multiple record systems. Broken traceability can force large-scale holds, a major cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterilization deviations with overdue investigations or corrective actions
rows.imp_rat: Overdue deviation investigations/CAPAs is administrative timeliness; peripheral to the CFJ, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-tracking tools log due dates, but overdue items remain widespread, indicating a poorly served need. Overdue investigations and CAPAs are a chronic scheduling failure in most quality systems. Closing them on time needs dedicated investigator capacity that is frequently short. Overdue items inflate quality-system backlog cost and inspection risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterilization-assurance evidence contains an unauthorized or unexplained alteration
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained alteration of sterilization-assurance evidence harms integrity, yet ranks below prior batch and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic records with secure audit trails make unauthorized alterations to assurance evidence rare. Audit-trail checks for alterations add minimal review time. Interpreting audit trails needs modest data-integrity skill now widely trained. Data-integrity failures carry severe regulatory and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting parameter values, timestamps, or approvals across records for the same autoclave load
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting parameters/timestamps across records for one load impairs verification, but the ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated systems reduce cross-record conflicts, but manual and hybrid records still produce occasional mismatches. Reconciling values/timestamps/approvals across records adds review time. Cross-checking multiple records for consistency requires careful reviewer attention. Conflicting records cause holds and investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of monitoring records not traceable to the corresponding cycle, load, and closure batch
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring records not traceable to cycle/load/batch weaken release proof, but ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-numbering and load-ID conventions make most records traceable, though hybrid systems leave some linkage gaps. Traceability tagging is applied at capture, adding little time. Maintaining linkage between records and cycle/load/batch relies on disciplined data practices. Untraceable records force reconstruction effort and delay release.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that monitoring evidence is assessed against obsolete or conflicting acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Assessing evidence against obsolete criteria could pass unfit loads, yet the ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Document-control systems keep effective criteria current, so use of obsolete criteria is fairly uncommon. Verifying current criteria version at review adds little time. Ensuring evidence is judged against current criteria depends on document-control discipline. Using obsolete criteria can void reviews and cause rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recurring adverse trends in sterilization evidence remain unrecognized
rows.imp_rat: Unrecognized adverse trends can foreshadow sterilization failures, but this monitoring gap ranks lower and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Trending is often manual and infrequent, so recurring adverse trends can go unrecognized for extended periods. Trend analysis is periodic and does not create per-cycle delay. Recognizing subtle adverse trends across cycles requires analytical expertise that is unevenly applied. Missed trends allow drift that eventually causes costly failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure sterilization changes implemented without required assessment and approval
rows.imp_rat: Changes implemented without assessment/approval could invalidate the validated state, but the ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Formal change-control systems make unapproved changes uncommon, giving good governance over sterilization changes. Required assessment and approval add lead time before changes take effect. Proper change assessment requires cross-functional expertise but is procedurally driven. Change control is administrative cost already budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical sterilization data cannot be traced to its source record and responsible actor
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable sterilization data to source/actor weakens data integrity, but the imposed ranking holds it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic systems with audit trails give strong traceability, though hybrid paper steps can still break the chain to source and actor. Tracing data to source and actor takes time when records are fragmented. Reconstructing traceability requires experienced reviewers who know the record landscape. Audit-trail infrastructure carries system cost but is largely in place.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterilization compliance deviations open beyond required closure dates
rows.imp_rat: Compliance deviations open past closure dates is largely administrative; peripheral to steam action, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: QMS tracking flags due dates, yet deviations routinely run past target, so aging is only moderately controlled. Overdue deviations directly represent time overruns against required closure dates, a chronic pain. Timely closure depends on investigator bandwidth and management follow-up rather than special skill. Overdue deviations tie up staff and hold inventory, adding indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that closure-load compliance status conflicts among governing records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting compliance status among records is a records-consistency issue; peripheral, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple disconnected records (paper, LIMS, QMS) make status conflicts a real recurring vulnerability with limited automated cross-checks. Reconciling conflicting status across records adds investigation time when it happens. Detecting cross-record status conflicts requires diligent reviewers comparing multiple systems. Reconciliation labor is the main cost, modest per occurrence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of effectiveness records containing missing, altered, or untraceable autoclave data
rows.imp_rat: Missing or altered autoclave data in effectiveness records weakens verification integrity but is held below the prior batch at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated cycle-data capture reduces missing/altered data substantially, though transcription into effectiveness records can introduce gaps. Verifying effectiveness records for completeness adds modest review time. Judging whether autoclave data is missing or untraceable needs data-integrity competence. Recordkeeping systems carry established cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty when closure-load evidence gives conflicting signals about defined SAL attainment
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty when evidence conflicts on SAL attainment directly touches the release judgment, but ranking caps it at 2.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of doubt that every permitted closure load configuration is fully specified before approval
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that every configuration is specified before approval is a specification-completeness concern; both proxies low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant closure quantity, packaging, tray-position, or load-density differences omitted from the deviation record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting load-difference details from the deviation record weakens analysis; documentation issue, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Relevant load-configuration details are routinely omitted from deviation records, a persistent documentation weakness. Capturing all load-detail differences adds documentation time at the deviation. Recognizing which quantity, packaging, position and density details matter needs experienced judgement. Omitted details cause investigation rework and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which sampled closures misrepresent bioburden across incoming lots
rows.imp_rat: Sampled closures misrepresenting bioburden across lots biases estimates only indirectly; both proxies low under cap.
rows.sat_rat: Limited sample sizes mean sampled closures may misrepresent lot bioburden, a recurring concern. Sampling across lots to ensure representativeness extends the sampling program. Designing representative sampling plans requires statistical competence. Broader lot sampling increases testing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that extraneous microorganisms are introduced during closure sampling or testing
rows.imp_rat: Introducing extraneous organisms during sampling biases test results; peripheral to steam delivery, both low.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic technique and controls keep sampling contamination fairly low but never eliminate it. Aseptic sampling technique adds handling time. Avoiding introduced contamination depends heavily on analyst aseptic skill. Aseptic supplies add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of bias in bioburden estimates caused by microbial aggregation or nonuniform distribution
rows.imp_rat: Bias from microbial aggregation in bioburden estimates is a measurement subtlety; peripheral, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Microbial aggregation systematically biases counts and is poorly corrected in routine practice. Addressing aggregation effects requires additional dispersion steps and time. Recognizing and correcting for clumping requires specialized microbiological technique. Dispersion and homogenization methods add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncharacterized bioburden variation across seasons and closure suppliers
rows.imp_rat: Uncharacterized seasonal/supplier bioburden variation affects assumptions indirectly; peripheral, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Seasonal and supplier bioburden variation is often uncharacterized, a recognized gap. Characterizing seasonal and supplier variation requires long-duration data collection. Interpreting seasonal and supplier trends requires analytical expertise. Extended sampling across seasons and suppliers is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in units and reference conditions across bioburden, D-value, and lethality inputs
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in units across bioburden/D-value/lethality inputs is a data-clarity issue; peripheral, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Unit and reference-condition mismatches across bioburden, D-value, and lethality data still occur despite conventions. Harmonizing units across inputs is a modest documentation effort. Ensuring consistent units and reference conditions across inputs requires careful attention. Consistency work is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncharacterized microbial resistance variation across closure lots, suppliers, storage, and handling conditions
rows.imp_rat: Uncharacterized resistance variation across lots/handling is an upstream data-completeness issue; both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Resistance variation across lots, storage, and handling is rarely characterized, leaving substantial uncertainty. Characterizing resistance variation across many conditions is very time-consuming. Assessing resistance variability across lots and handling requires specialized study design. Such broad resistance testing is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of survivor-observation distortion caused by microbial clumping or nonuniform distribution
rows.imp_rat: Survivor-observation distortion from clumping affects resistance study data indirectly; peripheral, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Clumping-driven distortion of survivor curves is a known and inadequately mitigated source of error. Correcting for clumping in survivor observations adds analytical time. Interpreting clumping-distorted survivor data requires advanced statistical skill. Additional replicates to address clumping add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in the microbial survival relationship caused by sparse or censored survivor observations
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty from sparse survivor data affects modeling only; distant from the immediate sterilization job, both low.
rows.sat_rat: Sparse and censored data commonly leave the survival relationship uncertain, weakly served by routine methods. Generating enough survivor data to reduce uncertainty requires many additional runs. Handling sparse or censored survivor data requires specialized statistical modeling expertise. Additional survivor testing is resource-intensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which aggregated findings obscure resistance differences among closure lots
rows.imp_rat: Aggregated findings obscuring lot resistance differences is an analysis subtlety; peripheral, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregated reporting commonly masks lot-to-lot resistance differences in routine practice. Resolving lot-level resistance differences requires disaggregated analysis time. Detecting inter-lot resistance differences requires strong data-analysis skill. Lot-level resistance testing multiplies cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which specified lethality exceeds closure integrity tolerance
rows.imp_rat: Lethality exceeding integrity tolerance is an efficiency/waste concern; not central to attaining SAL, both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Validation typically confirms cycles stay within integrity limits, so overshoot is generally controlled. Determining the integrity tolerance margin requires added characterization time. Judging the lethality-versus-integrity trade-off requires materials expertise. Excess lethality wastes energy and can prematurely degrade closures, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the units, basis, and reference conditions of specified lethality parameters
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in units/basis of lethality parameters is a specification-clarity issue; both proxies low under cap.
rows.sat_rat: Standard F0 conventions reduce ambiguity, though inconsistent documentation still causes occasional confusion. Specifying clear units and reference conditions is a quick documentation step. Correctly expressing lethality units (F0, reference temperature) requires domain literacy. Clear specification costs nothing extra in materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between sampled-closure bioburden and incoming-lot bioburden
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between sampled and lot bioburden is a sampling-representativeness issue with indirect effect; both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling variability leaves a persistent mismatch between sampled and true lot bioburden. Aligning sample bioburden with incoming lots requires additional sampling time. Ensuring sample-to-lot representativeness requires sampling-design skill. Larger representative samples add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of contamination introduced during closure-sample collection, transport, or handling
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during sample collection/transport corrupts bioburden data indirectly; both proxies low under cap.
rows.sat_rat: Established handling procedures reduce collection contamination, though lapses still occur. Controlled collection and transport add handling time. Contamination-free handling depends on trained personnel following aseptic procedures. Validated transport containers and controls add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compressed-air pressure, flow, or quality fails to support valve and door functions throughout the cycle
rows.imp_rat: Compressed-air failure affecting valve/door function could halt a cycle, but the ranking caps this utility item at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compressed-air requirements are well-specified and reliably met, with occasional quality lapses affecting valve function. Compressed-air adequacy is verified during commissioning without notable delay. Compressed-air specification for valves and doors is standard mechanical practice. Air treatment and adequate supply add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of supplier proposals lacking evidence of achieving the defined SAL under saturated steam
rows.imp_rat: Supplier proposals lacking SAL evidence is a procurement-screening gap; distant from the immediate job, both low.
rows.sat_rat: Well-written URS and RFQ templates reduce evidence-free proposals, yet vendors still routinely submit generic marketing data rather than load-specific SAL proof. Chasing missing SAL evidence across proposal rounds adds weeks of back-and-forth. Recognizing that a proposal lacks credible SAL evidence requires technical literacy proposals reviewers may lack. Weak proposals waste evaluation resources but cost little directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of resuming the sterilization cycle before upstream or downstream lines are ready
rows.imp_rat: Resuming a cycle before adjacent lines are ready is a sequencing coordination issue rather than core to sterilization efficacy; kept low and within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Handshake/interlock logic on integrated systems handles resume gating well, though not universally deployed across installations. Resume interlocks check line readiness quickly, avoiding wasted restart time. Ensuring adjacent lines are ready before resume depends on operator coordination absent full automation. Premature resume can jam or spoil product, incurring rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of upstream filling or downstream packing operating while the sterilizing machine is not ready
rows.imp_rat: Upstream/downstream lines operating while the machine is not ready is a line-coordination error affecting throughput, but only tangential to the sterilize-and-cool job itself; both proxies held low.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated lines use ready-signal interlocks reliably, but manually coordinated or older lines still permit misaligned operation. Line-ready interlocks signal upstream/downstream status quickly on integrated lines. Coordinating line readiness relies on operator communication where automation interlocks are absent. Running upstream/downstream while the sterilizer is not ready wastes product and energy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cups or lids damaged during handoffs between the sterilizing machine and adjacent line equipment
rows.imp_rat: Damage during handoffs to adjacent line equipment is a minor waste error (severity) and marginal to the sterilize-and-cool CFJ (relevance), ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Interface transfers between machine and adjacent equipment are a known weak point where mismatched timing and gaps still cause recurring damage. Handoff points seldom add standalone delay unless a jam or damage event forces a stop. Reliable handoffs depend on correct equipment alignment and setup skill at the interfaces. Damage at handoffs scraps containers already carrying sterilization value, so waste cost is meaningful.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required temperature, pressure, or timing setpoint cannot be changed during the active cycle
rows.imp_rat: Inability to change a setpoint mid-cycle is a control-flexibility limitation less central than core sterilization delivery (low relevance); it may cause a wasted cycle (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated recipes deliberately lock parameters for safety, so the 'cannot change' condition is partly by design; controlled override exists but varies. Adjusting a setpoint mid-cycle is immediate on most modern controllers when permitted. Making mid-cycle changes safely requires process knowledge, and lockouts intentionally limit who can do it. Inability to adjust may force aborting and rerunning a cycle, wasting energy and product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency between the cycle chronology and the associated batch record
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency between cycle chronology and batch record is an administrative record-keeping issue not central to actually conserving food (low relevance); consequences are recall/traceability confusion (low-moderate severity), held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Synchronized clocks and timestamped records reduce inconsistency, but clock drift and manual edits still create mismatches. Timestamped logs align chronology automatically, saving reconciliation time. Detecting chronology-vs-batch inconsistency requires analytical review skill when systems don't reconcile automatically. Chronology mismatches trigger investigations that consume QA resources.

rows.source_job: Register batch identity to cycle record
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cycle records missing one or more required batch identity fields
rows.imp_rat: Missing batch identity fields in cycle records is a documentation defect largely peripheral to the physical sterilization job (low relevance) and its consequence is traceability gaps rather than immediate food-safety failure (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Required-field validation is common on modern HMIs, though free-text and optional fields still leave records incomplete at times. Mandatory-field prompts capture identity fields quickly at cycle setup. Form templates reduce the skill needed to remember all required identity fields. Missing identity fields cause record rejection and rework, a modest recurring cost.

rows.source_job: Diagnose cycle deviations from process data
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required cycle operating records that are missing or incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Missing operating records is a pure documentation gap, minimally relevant to physically sterilizing and cooling food, with easily-absorbed consequences; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Mature continuous logging handles most operating records, though sensor faults and configuration gaps still cause occasional incompleteness. Operating parameters are captured automatically during the run with negligible added time. Preconfigured logging reduces the skill needed to ensure the right operating variables are recorded. Recording operating data uses existing instrumentation with little marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of cups and lids damaged while being removed from the chamber
rows.imp_rat: Cups and lids damaged during removal from the chamber cause material waste (severity) but are peripheral to whether the food is actually sterilized and cooled (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Mature chamber designs with racks and controlled discharge keep removal damage fairly consistent, with occasional format-specific defects remaining. Gentle removal practices add modest cycle time but rarely dominate the operation. Careful unloading depends on operator technique, though guides and trays reduce the finesse needed. Damaged cups and lids are direct material loss, giving a clear cost incentive to minimize breakage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of mixing unloaded cups and lids between production batches
rows.imp_rat: Mixing unloaded cups and lids between batches is a low-stakes traceability/organization error (severity) and only tangentially connected to the sterilize-and-cool CFJ (relevance), placing it low in the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice uses physical separation, batch tickets and cleared-line checks, but human-dependent segregation still leaves recurring risk of commingling. Batch segregation checks add little handling time when done, but confusion between batches can trigger time-consuming reconciliation. Preventing batch mix-ups leans heavily on operator vigilance and disciplined labeling rather than foolproof interlocks. A mix-up wastes materials and can force rejection of an entire batch, so the cost stakes are high.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the cooled batch left inside the chamber after unloading
rows.imp_rat: Cooled batch left inside the chamber after unloading is a minor rework error (severity) peripheral to the core sterilize-and-cool job (relevance), ranked at the bottom of the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Established discharge mechanisms clear most of the batch consistently, with small residual carryover being an intermittent issue. Ensuring complete discharge adds minimal time to the cycle. Complete batch removal is a routine operation requiring little expertise. Residual product left behind represents lost yield and potential cross-batch contamination.

rows.source_job: Scale processing output to batch size
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cycle restarts caused by invalid parameter configurations for the selected load
rows.imp_rat: Cycle restarts from invalid parameter configs are efficiency/rework issues not central to whether food is sterilized (low relevance); consequences are wasted cycles and time (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and pre-run checks catch many invalid configs, but mismatches between load and selected recipe still cause restarts. Parameter validation catches bad configs at setup, avoiding time lost to a restart later. Recipe selection tied to load type reduces reliance on operator to configure parameters correctly. Restarts waste energy, product, and throughput, so avoided restarts save real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying lubricant incompatible with a moving component or its sterile-use context
rows.imp_rat: Applying incompatible lubricant in a sterile-use context is a maintenance defect only loosely central to the sterilize/cool job (low relevance); contamination risk exists but is localized (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Color-coded food-grade lubricant programs reduce mix-ups, but wrong-grade application still occurs when controls are weak. Selecting the right lubricant adds negligible time when references are at hand. Avoiding incompatible lubricants in a sterile-use context relies on operators knowing food-grade (e.g. H1) requirements and component compatibility. Correct food-grade lubricants cost more, and a wrong choice risks costly product contamination recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which lubricant quantity applied to each moving component deviates from its requirement
rows.imp_rat: Deviation in lubricant quantity applied is a maintenance-precision matter only loosely tied to the sterilize/cool CFJ (low relevance); over/under-lubrication risks minor wear (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Lubrication charts help, but manual application routinely deviates from specified quantities, causing recurring over/under-lubrication. Manual lubrication to spec takes little time but doing it precisely per point adds up. Applying the exact required quantity per component relies on technician feel and adherence rather than precise metering. Over- or under-greasing wastes lubricant or causes wear-related repair costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of control software and firmware incompatibility with the machine after the update
rows.imp_rat: Software/firmware incompatibility after an update is a defect that can halt operation, but it is peripheral to the physical sterilize-and-cool job and administrative in nature, so both proxies are held low.
rows.sat_rat: Version pinning and staged rollouts make most updates predictable, yet undocumented incompatibilities still occasionally surface after the update. Firmware validation and rollback testing before deployment lengthen the update window but are now a scheduled step. Vendor-supplied version-compatibility matrices reduce the expertise needed, though automation-control specialists are still required for edge cases. Update tooling and test time cost modestly; a botched update causing extended downtime is the rare expensive tail.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the source of a mechanical or electrical fault during diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the source of a fault during diagnosis lengthens downtime but is a troubleshooting/servicing concern peripheral to the sterilization job; both proxies held minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic aids and alarm logs help, but ambiguous fault signatures still frequently lead technicians to misidentify the true source. Misdiagnosis lengthens downtime through trial-and-error part swaps and repeated troubleshooting cycles. Correctly pinpointing mechanical versus electrical fault sources leans heavily on scarce experienced troubleshooters and remains error-prone. Wrong diagnoses waste parts and labor replacing components that were not actually faulty.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between training instructions, configured machine controls, and approved safe operating procedures
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between training instructions and machine controls is a documentation-alignment error only loosely connected to the sterilizing job; low stakes, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Document-control practices exist but training, controls and SOPs frequently drift out of alignment, leaving recurring contradictions. Reconciling training material, machine controls and SOPs is a slow cross-checking effort often deferred. Spotting discrepancies requires someone fluent in all three documents, a rare integrative competence. Undetected discrepancies cause errors and rework whose cost is diffuse but real.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the food sterilizing machine tips or shifts unexpectedly during positioning and leveling
rows.imp_rat: Tipping or shifting during positioning/leveling is an installation-time stability issue barely tied to the running sterilize/cool job (very low relevance) but could injure personnel or damage equipment (moderate severity), constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Leveling feet, anchoring and load-rated rigging make unexpected tipping during positioning rare. Leveling and anchoring a machine is a routine rigging step that fits standard install schedules. Positioning and leveling relies on standard rigging competence that is broadly available. A tip event is costly and dangerous but very infrequent under normal practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between mating machine sub-assemblies during joining
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between mating sub-assemblies during joining is an assembly/manufacturing error (severity modest) and peripheral to the runtime sterilize-and-cool function (relevance low).
rows.sat_rat: Machined locating features and factory dowel pinning make correct alignment reasonably repeatable when parts arrive to spec. Alignment during joining consumes fitter time when shimming or re-seating mating faces, but locating dowels and jigs keep this manageable on most assemblies. Precise mating still leans on experienced millwrights reading dial indicators and feeler gauges, so outcomes vary with the crew's finesse.
