Misalignment rarely wastes major material but can gall bores or damage gaskets, adding modest rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterilized cups and lids damaged during unloading or transfer to packing
rows.imp_rat: Damage during unloading or transfer to packing is a yield-loss error (severity low) and only loosely tied to the core sterilizing/cooling function (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Guided transfer equipment handles most cases consistently, but tipping, scuffing and edge damage still recur during transitions. Transfer to packing is generally continuous, so protecting containers rarely slows the line materially. Smooth transfer relies on well-set conveyors and operator monitoring rather than rare expertise. Every damaged sterilized container is wasted product-contact material plus lost sterilization effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterilized cups and lids remaining in the chamber after unloading
rows.imp_rat: Sterilized cups and lids remaining in the chamber after unloading is a rework/completeness error (severity low) only marginally tied to whether sterilization itself succeeds (relevance low).
rows.sat_rat: Standard unloading sequences and visual checks usually clear the chamber, but stray units occasionally remain undetected. Verifying the chamber is empty adds a short check rather than major delay. Confirming full unloading is largely routine and needs little specialized skill. Left-behind sterilized units risk waste or contamination in the next cycle, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required sterilization-cycle records that are missing or incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Missing or incomplete cycle records is an administrative traceability issue only loosely tied to the functional sterilizing job, with absorbable consequences; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Established validated-record practice covers most fields, but partial writes, manual annotations, and unmapped channels leave recurring incompleteness. Automated logging fills required fields without added cycle time, though manual review of completeness still takes time. Standard recorder templates enforce which fields are captured, reducing reliance on operator judgement about what to log. Compliance recording systems are routine capital already present on GMP-regulated sterilizers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of degradation of seals and gaskets while the machine remains idle
rows.imp_rat: Seal and gasket degradation while idle is an off-duty maintenance defect; relevance to the active sterilizing job is low and severity is limited to pre-use replacement.
rows.sat_rat: Idle elastomer seals commonly take a compression set or dry out, and standard storage practice only partially prevents this recurring issue. Seal preservation is quick to apply but degradation manifests slowly and is only caught at periodic checks. Judging elastomer condition and choosing storage protection requires component-specific knowledge often lacking on the floor. Replacement seals are inexpensive, but undetected degradation causes costly leaks at restart.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of degradation of lubricated moving components during storage
rows.imp_rat: Degradation of lubricated components during storage is an off-cycle maintenance concern; relevance to the active sterilizing job is low and severity is limited to startup servicing.
rows.sat_rat: Standard layup preservation practices work well, though lubricant migration or dry-out over extended idle periods still degrades some components. Applying preservation lubricant before layup is fast and routine. Knowing which components need protective lubrication and re-lubrication intervals during storage takes some maintenance knowledge. Preservation greases and oils are cheap relative to the components protected.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of production restart delays caused by cleaning activities
rows.imp_rat: Restart delays from cleaning affect throughput efficiency, not the core sterilization outcome (low relevance); consequences are lost time, largely recoverable (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized cleaning sequences give fairly predictable restart timing, though verification holds and re-cleans occasionally extend it. Cleaning-driven restart delay is the core loss, and optimized CIP scheduling and parallel prep substantially shorten it. Efficient turnaround depends on coordinated, trained crews executing the changeover smoothly. Lost production time during cleaning is a real but bounded and quantified cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loosened mineral deposits blocking narrow thermal circuit passages during descaling
rows.imp_rat: Loosened mineral deposits blocking thermal passages during descaling can impair heating, but this is a maintenance-procedure error ranked low; both proxies kept minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Descaling reliably removes scale but loosened fragments unpredictably lodging in fine passages remains a poorly controlled, recurring failure mode. Flushing and inspecting narrow thermal passages after descaling adds time and sometimes forces repeat cycles. Judging when deposits are fully cleared versus merely loosened requires experienced descaling technicians. Blocked passages can mean chemical re-treatment or heat-exchanger replacement, a real waste when it happens.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying lubricant incompatible with the designated component or processing environment
rows.imp_rat: Near-duplicate of the incompatible-lubricant error for the designated component/environment; peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with contained contamination consequence (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Lubrication specification systems and segregated stores control this reasonably well, yet incompatible-lubricant application remains a recurring lapse. Matching lubricant to the designated component is quick with proper labeling and charts. Correct selection for the component and processing environment depends on maintainer knowledge of specs and compatibility. Using the wrong lubricant wastes product and causes premature wear, both real costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile production area while accessing the sterilizing machine's process instruments
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced while accessing process instruments is relevant to sterility but narrow and infrequent; both proxies kept minimal to honor the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Clean-access SOPs and area monitoring keep contamination events infrequent, but breaches during instrument access still occur intermittently. Accessing process instruments in a sterile area triggers gowning and clean-access protocols that add setup time. Cleanroom-discipline maintenance access needs trained, gowning-competent personnel not always on hand. Gowning consumables and area re-cleaning are modest but recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the backup and the machine's current approved configuration
rows.imp_rat: A mismatch between backup and current approved configuration is a recovery/records defect that does not impede live sterilizing; relevance is low and severity is limited to restore-time rework.
rows.sat_rat: Backups drift out of sync with the live approved configuration frequently, and disciplined version control is inconsistently applied. Comparing backups against current approved config is time-consuming when done manually and often deferred. Confirming a backup reflects the current approved configuration demands controls/automation expertise that many sites ration. Backup storage is cheap, but a stale backup restored later causes expensive misconfiguration.

rows.source_job: Test safety relief and interlock devices
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recording invalid actuation measurements because test instruments are outside calibration status
rows.imp_rat: Recording invalid actuation measurements due to out-of-calibration instruments is a data-quality/administrative error (severity low) with little direct bearing on sterilizing food (relevance low).
rows.sat_rat: Calibration-management systems with due-date labels make out-of-cal usage uncommon, though the occasional overlooked instrument still slips through. Checking calibration status before testing adds a brief verification step to the workflow. Reading calibration labels and due-dates requires little specialized skill. Periodic instrument calibration is a scheduled, well-budgeted recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that archived decommission records are unreadable or corrupted when retrieved
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted archived decommission records are an end-of-life documentation concern almost unrelated to the operational sterilize/cool job (very low relevance); losing them creates compliance gaps (moderate severity) but ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic document systems help, but format obsolescence, poor indexing and corruption still make retrieval unreliable years later. Archiving records adds minor closeout time that is often deferred. Proper document capture and archiving needs records-management discipline rather than deep expertise. Digital archiving is cheap, so cost is not the barrier.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the abnormal condition cause before responding
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying an abnormal-condition cause could delay correct response, but this is a response-stage ambiguity held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm codes give clues but root-cause identification during events is inconsistent and operator-dependent. Diagnosing cause quickly can shorten response but is variable. Correctly identifying an abnormal condition's cause is a high skill/experience dependence. Misdiagnosis leads to wrong fixes and wasted effort/material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same abnormal condition recurring before stabilization
rows.imp_rat: Recurring abnormal conditions before stabilization affect reliability but rank below prior batch; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Without good root-cause handling, the same abnormal condition can recur, so consistency is moderate. Recurring conditions extend total downtime before stabilization. Preventing recurrence depends on operator applying an effective response. Repeated events waste steam and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from critical excursion onset to operator awareness
rows.imp_rat: Delay in operator awareness of an excursion could worsen an event, but as a response-latency item it is held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time monitoring and audible alarms make operator awareness of excursions prompt and consistent. Time from excursion onset to operator awareness is minimized by real-time alarms. Little skill needed as automated detection drives awareness. Faster awareness limits wasted processing on a bad load.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of processed closures left in the chamber after unloading
rows.imp_rat: Processed closures left in the chamber cause loss/rework but do not undermine the sterilization already achieved; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual end-of-unload checks reliably prevent leaving closures in the chamber. Checking the chamber is empty after unload is a quick glance. No real skill needed to confirm the chamber is clear. Left-behind closures are lost or reprocessed, a minor waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty about whether the chamber is safe to open
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about chamber safety to open touches operator safety but is a brief transitional ambiguity; capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure packages assembled with materials outside the approved specification
rows.imp_rat: Packaging materials outside spec could affect sterilization, but it is peripheral to the chamber cycle job and stays capped.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled material issue and approved BOMs make out-of-spec packaging materials uncommon. Confirming approved materials is a quick verification against spec. Material verification relies on reading specs rather than expertise. Wrong materials can ruin loads, an expensive but infrequent error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt caused by conflicting indications of prepared-closure exposure
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting exposure indications create doubt about sterility status, but this is a review ambiguity held at the ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of change records closed while required validated-state actions remain incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Closing change records with incomplete validated-state actions is largely administrative and peripheral to achieving SAL, so relevance is low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control closure checks generally prevent closing with open actions, though premature closures still occur. Change closure verification adds procedural time at the end of the change. Confirming all required actions are complete before closure is a checklist review task. Prematurely closed changes create rework and audit findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that required lifecycle requalification remains overdue
rows.imp_rat: Overdue requalification risks operating outside a validated state, but as a scheduling-lag item it ranks below prior batch; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Requalification schedules exist but overdue items are common due to competing priorities, leaving this only moderately served. Overdue lifecycle requalification is a duration problem where scheduling frequently slips. Keeping requalification current depends on planning and resource allocation, not per-event skill. Overdue requalification risks compliance holds and rushed catch-up spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexplained alterations in evidence supporting a processed-load disposition
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained alterations in disposition evidence affect trust in release but are peripheral to the cycle; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Secure audit trails make unexplained alterations in disposition evidence uncommon. Checking disposition evidence for alterations is a standard review step. Audit-trail tooling reduces the skill needed to spot unexplained changes. Unexplained alterations force reinvestigation and possible rejection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among evidence sources supporting a processed-load disposition
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among disposition evidence sources is a reconciliation issue peripheral to the cycle; held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant monitoring usually agrees, but reconciling occasional disagreements lacks strong systematic support. Resolving evidence disagreement adds disposition time when it happens. Reconciling disagreeing evidence sources needs experienced review judgement. Unreconciled disagreement delays release and can cause errors.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterilization-assurance records missing from the audit evidence set
rows.imp_rat: Missing audit-evidence records harms compliance standing but is an archival-completeness issue held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Document-control systems keep most required records available, though completeness gaps occasionally appear at audit. Missing audit records surface during audit prep and delay it. Assembling a complete audit set relies on records-management thoroughness. Missing audit records risk findings and reconstruction effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of a lapse in documented qualification support for the validated autoclave state
rows.imp_rat: A lapse in qualification support jeopardizes the validated state but is a governance-duration issue held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Requalification programs are scheduled, but lapses in documented support do occur between cycles. This need concerns the duration of any lapse in qualification support, which periodic-review scheduling can let extend. Maintaining continuous qualification coverage requires validation-planning discipline. A qualification lapse can force operational holds, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of monitoring records with unexplained alteration or loss
rows.imp_rat: Altered or lost monitoring records weaken proof of sterility but are a records-integrity issue held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated electronic systems with audit trails keep unexplained alteration or loss uncommon. Reviewing records for unexplained alteration/loss is routine. Recognizing alteration or loss is aided by audit trails, lowering skill demand. Lost or altered records trigger costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among monitoring records for the same sterilization cycle
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved monitoring-record conflicts for a cycle complicate review but are secondary to achieving SAL; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts between records for the same cycle are relatively rare with integrated data systems but not fully eliminated. Conflicts among monitoring records surface at review and delay closure. Diagnosing why records conflict for one cycle requires technical skill. Unresolved conflicts hold loads and consume investigation effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of undocumented alterations to closure-load evidence used for disposition
rows.imp_rat: Undocumented alterations to disposition evidence harm data integrity but are peripheral to steam-sterilization efficacy; kept at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails on electronic records deter undocumented alteration well, but paper corrections remain a residual data-integrity gap. Detecting undocumented alterations is not a timing burden but a review activity. Spotting undocumented changes demands forensic attention that manual review provides unevenly. Controls cost little beyond existing review labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among evidence elements used to disposition a closure load
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting disposition evidence creates decision risk, but it is a review-stage ambiguity ranked below prior batch and held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured review catches many conflicts, but no systematic cross-validation means some evidence conflicts pass unnoticed. Resolving conflicting evidence elements adds investigation time when detected. Recognizing and adjudicating evidence conflict needs experienced disposition judgement. Conflict resolution is labor cost, modest per load.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among disposition decisions supported by equivalent closure-load evidence
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent disposition decisions on equivalent evidence is a judgment-consistency issue, peripheral to steam sterilization; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria constrain decisions, but reviewer-to-reviewer variation on equivalent evidence persists with limited standardization aids. Inconsistent decisions do not primarily cost time. Consistency across equivalent evidence hinges on individual reviewer judgement, which varies without strong decision rules. Inconsistent dispositions cause occasional rework cost but not primarily.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory requirements omitted from the closure sterilization compliance scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting regulatory requirements from scope risks compliance gaps but is an administrative-scoping error ranked below prior batch; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory intelligence processes capture most requirements, but omissions from compliance scope do occur and are hard to detect proactively. Scope-mapping is periodic and not time-critical to daily operation. Identifying all applicable regulatory requirements demands specialized regulatory-affairs expertise that is scarce. Maintaining regulatory scope is an administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the targeted sterilization failure recurs after effectiveness observation ends
rows.imp_rat: Recurrence of a targeted failure after observation ends is a durability concern ranked below prior batch; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Effectiveness monitoring periods are often too short, so recurrence after observation ends is a recognized and poorly mitigated failure. Recurrence surfaces after monitoring ends, so timing is not the core issue. Designing durable corrective actions needs strong root-cause skill that varies widely. Recurrence drives repeat investigation and scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of new sterilization compliance failures introduced but missed during effectiveness verification
rows.imp_rat: Missed new failures during effectiveness verification risks latent gaps but is a CAPA-verification concern held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Effectiveness checks catch many issues, but new failures introduced by a fix are frequently missed due to narrow verification scope. Effectiveness verification adds a defined observation period, not a daily delay. Detecting newly introduced failures during verification needs skilled review of appropriate scope. Extended verification consumes analyst effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement between supplier and site bioburden findings for the same closure lot
rows.imp_rat: Supplier vs site bioburden disagreement is an upstream data conflict, peripheral to running the autoclave cycle; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Method differences between supplier and site frequently produce disagreements that are hard to close. Reconciling supplier and site findings requires coordination time. Resolving supplier-site discrepancies requires analytical and communication skill. Duplicate testing to reconcile is costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a closure sterility failure is attributed to the recommended sterility assurance and lethality criteria
rows.imp_rat: A sterility failure attributed to the recommended criteria implicates the design basis but is a downstream-attribution risk held at ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cooling-water temperature, pressure, flow, or quality delays cooling or prevents cycle completion
rows.imp_rat: Cooling-water inadequacy delaying cooling/completion affects cycle finish but is a utility-support issue held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cooling-water needs are generally well-characterized, though flow/temperature shortfalls occasionally stall cycles in shared systems. Inadequate cooling water directly extends cooling time and delays cycle completion. Cooling-system sizing is established HVAC/utility engineering. Undersized cooling water forces chiller additions at added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between available electrical supply characteristics and autoclave requirements
rows.imp_rat: Electrical supply mismatch could disrupt operation, but as a facility-fit item it ranks below prior batch and is held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electrical requirements are well-documented by vendors and reliably matched during installation. Electrical matching is resolved during design engineering with minimal operational delay. Electrical supply matching is routine facility-engineering work with clear standards. Mismatch requires transformer or feeder upgrades that add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of qualifying a supplier with deficient GMP, validation, or data-integrity controls
rows.imp_rat: Qualifying a deficient supplier is an upstream sourcing risk with potential quality impact but peripheral to the autoclave job; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Formal supplier qualification and QMS audit programs are a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably surfaces deficient GMP or data-integrity controls. GMP and data-integrity audits are scheduled well within normal procurement timelines. Trained quality auditors are a mature, available resource for assessing GMP and data-integrity controls. Audit costs are routine and budgeted line items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residual energy during the upgrade
rows.imp_rat: Residual-energy exposure during upgrade poses real safety risk (severity), but upgrades are peripheral to the core sterilizing job (relevance), constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard isolation procedures make residual-energy exposure during upgrades uncommon, with lapses tied to procedural shortcuts. Discharging stored energy before an upgrade adds a short verification delay. Residual-energy isolation for upgrades uses the same standard LOTO skills most technicians hold. Minimal incremental cost beyond standard lockout materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that upgraded components cause the machine to fail sterile fill-finish qualification
rows.imp_rat: Upgraded components failing sterile fill-finish qualification is a change-management risk that arises only during upgrades, tangential to the ongoing CFJ. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and requalification protocols exist but component substitutions still frequently reveal unanticipated qualification impacts. Requalification after an upgrade is a lengthy scheduled activity, but the failure mode itself is a qualification miss, not a delay. Judging whether an upgraded component preserves sterile-fill validation requires scarce validation and process-engineering expertise. A failed qualification triggers expensive rework, re-validation and possible batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of microbial or particulate contamination accumulated in product-contact areas during prolonged idle periods
rows.imp_rat: Contamination accumulating during idle periods carries hygiene consequences, but as an idle-state condition it ranks below the prior batch and is capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Practices for post-idle sanitization exist but are inconsistently applied, so contamination in stagnant product-contact zones still slips through. Pre-startup flush/sanitize after idle periods adds startup time but is bounded. Deciding on re-sanitization after idle relies on operator judgement about how long is too long. Idle-period contamination can waste the first production or extra sanitizer, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of electrical and control components damaged by temperature or humidity excursions during storage
rows.imp_rat: Electrical/control components damaged by temperature or humidity during storage is a pre-operational logistics error only tangentially tied to running sterilization; the ranking places it below batch 2, keeping relevance and severity minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Most plants store spares in reasonable conditions, yet occasional excursions in non-conditioned storerooms still damage sensitive electronics. Proper storage handling adds negligible time once procedures exist. Basic warehouse staff can follow humidity/temperature storage rules without specialist skill. Climate-controlled storage costs money, but damaged control components are far more expensive to replace.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that backed-up process and configuration data cannot be restored when needed
rows.imp_rat: Unrestorable backup data is a recovery/IT concern peripheral to running the sterilizing job (low relevance) with contained consequences (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are commonly taken but seldom validated by test-restore, so undiscovered restore failures are a frequent, painful surprise. Restoring backups can be slow and is rarely rehearsed, so real restores drag under pressure. Successful restoration often needs specialist knowledge of the control system that few site staff hold. Backup storage is cheap, but a failed restore can mean costly reconstruction or extended downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that process records and configuration settings are captured in mutually inconsistent states
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent record/configuration capture is a data-integrity concern adjacent to the core job (low relevance) with reconcilable consequences (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Process records and configuration are typically captured through separate, unsynchronized processes, so mutually inconsistent states are a common latent problem. Capturing records and config together consistently requires coordinated timing that adds effort. Ensuring records and settings are snapshotted in sync needs disciplined procedure most sites lack. The systems exist, but inconsistent states can force costly investigation and re-validation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of food-contact surface area retaining food residue or biofilm after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residue or biofilm remaining after cleaning has genuine contamination stakes, but the ranking places it below the prior batch; consequences are meaningful yet capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP and swab/ATP checks handle most surfaces well, but geometry-dead-legs and biofilm still leave recurring residue gaps. CIP cycles are time-boxed and routine, so cleaning rarely blows the schedule. Validated CIP recipes reduce operator dependence, though verifying residue-free surfaces still needs trained inspection. Cleaning consumes water, chemicals and energy that are budgeted and manageable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of food-contact surface defects caused during cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Food-contact surface defects from cleaning relate to hygiene but the ranking fixes this item below batch 2; capped by the ceiling, both proxies stay low even though the error touches sanitation.
rows.sat_rat: Standard SSOPs and approved cleaning agents reduce damage, but manual cleaning still produces recurring surface defects. Careful cleaning to avoid surface damage takes only marginally longer than routine cleaning. Avoiding scratches and abrasion on food-contact surfaces relies heavily on operator technique and choice of tools, so untrained staff cause defects. Surface refinishing or component replacement after cleaning damage is a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sanitizing agent residue remaining in the chambers before the next production run
rows.imp_rat: Residual sanitizing agent in chambers touches on contamination control (relevance) and could taint product (severity), but the ranking places it below the prior batch, capping importance at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Conductivity/rinse-water testing catches most sanitizer carryover, making residual chemical a well-controlled but not fully eliminated risk. Rinse cycles and hold steps are scheduled and rarely delay production materially. Validated rinse recipes limit reliance on operator skill for residue removal. Extra rinse water and testing reagents are minor recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of internal chamber surfaces, seals, or components damaged during sanitation
rows.imp_rat: Chamber surfaces or seals damaged during sanitation is a maintenance-related error; ranked below batch 2, it is held to low proxies despite its loose link to chamber integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Established sanitation SOPs limit damage, but harsh chemicals and manual handling still cause recurring component wear and damage. Careful sanitation to avoid damage is only slightly slower than rushed sanitation. Avoiding damage to chamber surfaces and seals during aggressive sanitation depends on trained, attentive technique. Replacing damaged internal seals and components is a meaningful recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to retained pressure or hot process fluids during replacement
rows.imp_rat: Retained pressure or hot fluid exposure during replacement carries injury risk (severity) yet is peripheral to core function (relevance), constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature depressurization and drain-down practice makes retained-pressure or hot-fluid incidents uncommon, though trapped pockets occasionally remain. Bleed-down and cooldown before part replacement are established, time-bounded steps in most maintenance procedures. Replacement under isolation is well-drilled work that most maintenance staff can execute without specialist expertise. Relief valves, drain points and isolation valves are low-cost standard fittings already fitted on process equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incorrect seals, gaskets, or filters installed on the sterilizing machine
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect seals, gaskets, or filters installed could affect sterilization integrity, yet the mandated ceiling ranks it below batch 2, holding relevance and severity to minimal values.
rows.sat_rat: Parts kits and BOMs help, but visually similar seals and filters are still misinstalled with some regularity. Verifying the correct seal, gasket or filter adds little time when part references are available. Correct selection depends on technician familiarity with the specific machine's parts catalog, and mistakes rise with unfamiliar staff. Wrong parts cause scrapped components and repeat maintenance visits, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterilizing machine shutdown caused by inaccessible components, missing prerequisites, or difficult removal
rows.imp_rat: Shutdown from inaccessible components or difficult removal is a serviceability delay adjacent to the job (relevance) with absorbable impact (severity); capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Machine design and prerequisite gaps make disassembly time unpredictable, a persistent maintenance pain. Poor access, missing prerequisites, or seized components regularly extend shutdowns beyond plan. Working around difficult removal demands experienced hands-on mechanical skill. Extra labor and occasional damaged parts during forced removal add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of food sterilizing machine unavailability caused by descaling
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from descaling reduces availability which relates to the job (relevance) but is routine maintenance with absorbable impact (severity); ranking caps importance at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled descaling reliably restores function, though scale buildup timing is sometimes underestimated causing longer stops. Descaling is a scheduled downtime activity with fairly predictable duration. Routine descaling is a well-documented procedure needing modest skill. Descaling chemicals and lost production time are moderate, budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterilization subsystem service delays caused by unavailable replacement components
rows.imp_rat: Sterilization subsystem service delays from unavailable parts touch the core subsystem (relevance) but are parts-logistics with recoverable effect (severity); ranking caps importance at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Critical sterilizer part availability is unreliable, leaving the most safety-critical subsystem idle for extended periods. Sterilization-subsystem repairs stall badly when specialized replacement parts are unavailable. Diagnosing and replacing sterilizer components needs specialized technicians in short supply. Sterilizer spares are costly and often single-sourced, so inventory is thin.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that post-sterilization cooling remains outside the required operating range after service
rows.imp_rat: Cooling out of range after service touches the cool step of the CFJ, but its post-batch ranking caps relevance and severity at the mandated ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Functional checks after service catch many issues, but cooling drift outside range still slips through without formal post-service qualification. Post-service verification of cooling range takes a test run that lengthens return-to-production. Judging whether cooling meets spec after service needs process knowledge that varies by technician. Verification uses existing instruments but a failed cooling range risks spoiled product cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that service work contaminates product-contact areas adjacent to the cooling subsystem
rows.imp_rat: Service work contaminating cooling-adjacent product-contact areas has real hygiene stakes, but as a servicing-window event it is peripheral to routine job execution. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard hygienic-zoning practice reduces adjacent contamination, but spillover during service still happens more than occasionally. Masking and re-cleaning cooling-adjacent contact areas adds time to service tasks. Avoiding cross-contamination near the cooling subsystem depends on trained, attentive technique. Protective covers and cleaning materials are low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to pressurized refrigerant during cooling-subsystem service
rows.imp_rat: Pressurized refrigerant exposure during cooling service is a genuine hazard (severity) but tied to servicing rather than the core job (relevance); ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated refrigerant-handling procedures and recovery tools make pressurized-refrigerant exposure uncommon where certified staff perform the work. Recovering and evacuating refrigerant before service adds a required, fixed-duration step. Refrigerant handling requires certified technicians with specialized recovery skills that are not universally on-site. Recovery equipment, refrigerant loss and certified-tech labor are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption caused by cooling-subsystem service
rows.imp_rat: Interruption from cooling-subsystem service touches the cool step (relevance) but is scheduled service with limited stakes (severity); ranking holds importance at or below 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled cooling maintenance is fairly predictable, though refrigerant leaks and compressor issues cause occasional overruns. Cooling-subsystem service is usually planned, but component access and refill can extend the interruption. Servicing refrigeration/cooling circuits needs certified technicians who are not always immediately available. Refrigerant, coolant and specialist labor make cooling service a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to residual heat or pressure while calibrating the sterilizing machine's process instruments
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to residual heat/pressure during calibration is a safety hazard (severity) but incidental to the sterilizing job itself (relevance); capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established LOTO and depressurization protocols reliably reduce exposure, but calibration often requires live or hot instruments where residual energy still surprises technicians. Lockout/tagout and cooldown waits add time but are routine and predictable steps in calibration procedures. Technicians are trained on energy-isolation procedures, though sensing residual heat/pressure still relies on their judgement and diligence. Standard LOTO hardware and pressure-relief practices are inexpensive and already in place.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unscheduled sterilizing-machine unavailability caused by periodic calibration
rows.imp_rat: Unscheduled unavailability from periodic calibration affects availability (relevance) but is routine calibration with limited consequences (severity); capped at the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Because calibration intervals are often reactive rather than predictive, unavailability tends to strike at inconvenient, unplanned moments. Calibration on food sterilizers routinely forces the machine offline for hours or a shift because most facilities lack redundant units or in-line calibration, and scheduling rarely aligns with production gaps. Calibration requires trained metrology or vendor technicians whose availability constrains when the line can be freed, adding to downtime. Lost production during unscheduled unavailability is expensive, and rush vendor call-outs compound the cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required control configurations, recipe parameters, and calibration data omitted from the backup
rows.imp_rat: Omitting configurations from the backup harms recoverability, a support-function concern rather than central job execution. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backups commonly cover the obvious PLC program but silently omit calibration or ancillary parameters, a gap discovered only at restore. Capturing configs and recipes into a backup is quick when tooling supports it. Knowing every calibration file and recipe parameter that must be captured demands system-specific expertise often held by one person. The capture itself costs little, but omissions cause expensive reconstruction later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stored backup data is corrupted or unreadable
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted backup data affects recoverability, not the immediate sterilizing/cooling job; consequences surface only during restore. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are seldom integrity-checked, so silent corruption of stored data is a chronic, rarely-mitigated risk. Detecting corruption only at restore time causes major unplanned delay. Verifying backup integrity requires deliberate procedures and skills rarely applied at sites. Redundant and checksummed storage is cheap but frequently not implemented.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the stored backup is inaccessible when restoration is required
rows.imp_rat: An inaccessible backup during restoration is a recovery-scenario problem with limited relevance to producing sterilized product day to day. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many sites keep backups but rarely test restores, so inaccessible or corrupt backups surface only at the worst moment. Retrieving a backup is fast when it exists, but locating an unverified or stale backup can consume hours. Restoration needs a controls/IT-savvy person, and few maintenance teams routinely verify backup integrity themselves. Backup storage is cheap, so failures here cost mainly the downtime rather than materials.

rows.source_job: Verify cleaning and sanitation validation records
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between validation records and corresponding machine, batch, or sanitation logs
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between validation records and machine/batch/sanitation logs are an administrative traceability error, peripheral to the sterilize-and-cool CFJ; consequences are documentation cleanup rather than product harm, so both proxies stay low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: With records kept in disconnected paper and digital systems, mismatches between validation and operational logs are a chronic, frequently-recurring problem. Reconciling validation records against machine, batch and sanitation logs is slow manual cross-checking that often happens only at audit time. Spotting discrepancies across separate log systems demands experienced QA reviewers who know what should match. Discrepancies drive costly investigations, batch holds, and rework when found late.

rows.source_job: Verify cleaning and sanitation validation records
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the regulatory and internal acceptance criteria applicable to the review period
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in regulatory/internal acceptance criteria for a review period is an administrative concern almost unrelated to the physical sterilizing job; kept minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria are frequently vague or fragmented across documents, so inconsistent interpretation is a chronic, poorly-mitigated pain. Interpreting overlapping regulatory and internal criteria often stalls the review while people seek clarification. Resolving ambiguity demands scarce quality/regulatory expertise and seasoned judgement that not every site has on hand. Ambiguity drives rework, consultations and occasional audit findings that carry real cost.

rows.source_job: Verify cleaning and sanitation validation records
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in obtaining required validation records after the compliance review begins
rows.imp_rat: Delay in obtaining validation records is a compliance-documentation error, tangential to the machine's functional sterilizing job (relevance) though a slow audit response can hurt standing (severity); capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Records usually exist, so the failure is retrieval speed rather than missing data, though gaps do occasionally appear. Electronic records shorten retrieval, but scattered paper or siloed systems still cause slow record pulls during audits. Finding the right validation records fast depends on a knowledgeable QA person who knows the filing system. Delays mainly cost audit labor hours rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine is authorized for removal before sanitation completion is confirmed
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing removal before sanitation confirmation is an administrative/procedural error with low centrality to the sterilizing job (relevance) though a lapse could allow unsanitized use (severity); ceiling keeps it at or below 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Where sign-off gates are enforced they work, but authorization sometimes proceeds on informal confirmation without documented sanitation completion. Sanitation sign-off gates can hold up removal authorization, adding wait time. Verification depends on quality staff judgement about when sanitation is truly complete. Premature authorization risks re-work and rescheduling costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that buyer inspection identifies machine condition differing from seller disclosures
rows.imp_rat: A buyer inspection finding condition differing from disclosures is a commercial/transactional error, essentially irrelevant to the running sterilization job, so relevance is minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on informal seller declarations with no standardized used-machine condition report, so inspection surprises remain common. Buyer inspections add scheduling delay, but the core failure is a mismatch that surfaces regardless of how fast the walkthrough happens. Accurate condition disclosure for used process equipment demands experienced appraisers who are scarce in a disposal context, so gaps persist. Undisclosed condition triggers price renegotiation and re-inspection expense that sellers routinely absorb.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual product or hazardous cleaning agents remain in the machine at handover
rows.imp_rat: Residual product or cleaning agents at handover touches contamination but occurs during handover only (bounded relevance) and is remediated before production (low severity); constrained by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard clean-out procedures exist but residual product or cleaning chemicals in dead legs and valves still occasionally remain at handover. Flushing and cleaning cycles add measurable time before handover. Sanitation crews follow validated CIP/cleaning protocols, requiring moderate but available skill. Cleaning agents and disposal of rinse waste carry recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of services becoming unavailable before retirement activities for the food sterilizing machine are complete
rows.imp_rat: Services becoming unavailable before retirement is an end-of-life logistics concern, essentially irrelevant to the machine's day-to-day sterilizing job; kept minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Project schedules address this but cross-team handoffs make premature service loss a recurring coordination failure. Sequencing service disconnection to the end of retirement work requires scheduling foresight that can slip. Coordinating utility availability with teardown milestones needs project-planning skill. Premature service loss can force costly rework or standby crews.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production-line stoppages caused by incompatible speeds across filling, sterilization, cooling, and packing
rows.imp_rat: Line-speed mismatch stoppages relate to throughput (relevance) but cause recoverable delay rather than catastrophe (severity); ceiling holds it low.
rows.sat_rat: Buffering and line-control logic smooth some mismatch, but incompatible rates across filling, sterilizing, cooling and packing remain a persistent bottleneck. Speed mismatches across line stages directly create accumulating stoppage duration that is hard to eliminate. Balancing line speeds requires experienced line engineering and tuning. Idle equipment and lost throughput from imbalance carry real productivity cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of different cup or lid formats being active across filling, sterilization, and packing during a changeover
rows.imp_rat: Mixed cup/lid formats active across stations during changeover is a defect risk (relevance) causing rework rather than catastrophe (severity); ceiling holds it low.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe controls and checklists help align formats, but coordination across filling, sterilizing and packing still allows mismatches during changeover. A format mismatch discovered mid-line forces time-consuming stops and corrections. Preventing active-format conflicts depends on careful operator verification across stations. Running incompatible formats scraps product and containers, a significant waste cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of food product spilled while loading the processing chamber
rows.imp_rat: Food spilled during chamber loading is a minor loading-stage waste issue (low relevance) with trivial, recoverable consequences (low severity); held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled fill and loading equipment keeps most product in the chamber, with occasional spillage during handling. Spill cleanup during loading adds minor incidental time. Careful loading is largely routine handling not needing special expertise. Spilled product is direct material waste, giving a clear cost focus that dosing controls partly address.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve conflicting product or container identification before recipe selection
rows.imp_rat: Resolving conflicting product identification before recipe selection is a setup-stage ambiguity (low relevance) with recoverable delay consequences (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode and MES-driven recipe selection resolve most identity quickly, though conflicting or ambiguous IDs still occasionally stall selection. Conflicting identification delays recipe selection and thus batch start time. Resolving ID conflicts needs operator familiarity with the identification system. Delay costs idle time but rarely destroys material if caught before processing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of process-profile deviation before the needed setpoint change is applied
rows.imp_rat: Delay in applying a needed setpoint change touches process control but is scored below batch 2; the deviation window is brief and correctable, so relevance and severity remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems flag deviations reliably, but the response time to apply the corrective setpoint change varies with operator attentiveness. Automated closed-loop control corrects most process-profile drift quickly, but operator-initiated setpoint changes still lag while the deviation is noticed and confirmed. Deciding when and how to change a setpoint mid-process requires experienced operator judgement. Prolonged deviation risks product loss, giving the delay a real cost dimension.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between sterilization cycle completion and availability of the complete quality record
rows.imp_rat: Delay in availability of the complete quality record is a documentation-timing concern, peripheral to actually sterilizing and cooling. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual transcription steps in many facilities create gaps or errors that must be corrected, delaying a trustworthy final record. Complete quality records often require manual collation of charts, printouts and batch data after the cycle, introducing a real lag before the record is available. Assembling and verifying the quality record needs trained QA judgement to confirm all parameters met approved conditions. The record-preparation labor adds modest cost but no material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned stoppages caused by sterilization or cooling faults during extended runs
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned stoppages from sterilization/cooling faults touch the core job (relevance), but the ranking placed this batch below prior items, so both proxies are held near the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Monitoring and preventive maintenance reduce faults, but extended runs still see sporadic sterilization or cooling failures that halt production. Unplanned stoppages during long runs cause direct and unpredictable lost production time. Diagnosing and recovering from sterilization or cooling faults needs skilled maintenance judgement. Faults during a run can spoil in-process product and incur restart costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaving machine components misaligned for the incoming container format
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned components for the incoming container format is a changeover defect (relevance) with recoverable consequences (severity), kept below the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Setup guides, marks and change parts reduce errors, but format-specific misalignment still slips through on manual changeovers. Misaligned components discovered after start-up trigger rework time. Correct alignment for a new format depends heavily on setup technician skill and attention. Misalignment produces scrap and can damage tooling, incurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the batch remains on hold while variation in product size, density, or fill level is resolved
rows.imp_rat: A batch held while product-size variation is resolved is a minor throughput delay (low relevance) that is temporary and recoverable (low severity); held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe standards and inspection catch variation, but resolving out-of-spec inputs still causes recurring holds. Batches held while resolving size, density or fill variation lose direct processing time. Resolving such variation requires experienced judgement on process parameters. Holds risk product quality and idle capacity but limited direct material loss.

rows.source_job: Register batch identity to cycle record
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the batch identity registration delay caused by rejected identity data
rows.imp_rat: Batch identity registration delay from rejected identity data is peripheral to the CFJ of sterilizing and cooling food; consequences are minor administrative rework. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Digital identity registration systems handle most records reliably, with occasional rejections forcing short re-registration delays. Rejected identity data delays registration and start, but retries are usually quick. Re-entering or correcting identity data is a low-skill clerical task. The delay carries little material cost beyond brief idle time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an observer or operator being injured during the machine demonstration
rows.imp_rat: Injury during a demonstration carries real safety weight (severity) but demonstration is peripheral to the sterilizing job (relevance); the batch ceiling caps importance at 1.5 regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding, interlocks and enforced safe-distance practices make injury during a supervised demonstration rare and well controlled. Safety briefings and guarding checks add a little setup time before a demonstration. Following established lockout, guarding and standoff rules requires basic training that demonstrators typically already have. Safety measures use minimal consumables and impose little cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the demonstration introducing contamination into the sterile fill-finish area
rows.imp_rat: Demonstration-introduced contamination is a rare demo-context event, only loosely tied to routine job execution though hygiene stakes exist. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established aseptic protocols and access controls reduce contamination risk, but every extra body in the sterile area raises the chance of a breach that standard practice only partly contains. Contamination-control setup for a demonstration in a sterile fill-finish area takes preparation time but is a known, planned activity. Preventing contamination during a demo depends heavily on skilled aseptic technique and disciplined gowning by observers and operators. Gowning, filtered air and cleaning consumables add cost each time a demonstration is staged.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the demonstrated cycle deviates from approved cup-and-lid sterilization conditions
rows.imp_rat: A demonstrated cycle deviating from approved cup-and-lid conditions is a demo-fidelity issue with limited relevance to routine sterilization. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-locked PLC control keeps demonstrated cycles close to approved conditions, though manual parameter overrides can still introduce deviations. Loading an approved recipe takes little time and does not delay the demonstration. Selecting the correct validated cup-and-lid sterilization program requires operator awareness but is guided by locked recipe menus. Running a validated recipe uses standard resources with no added waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of viable microorganisms remaining on product-contact surfaces after sanitation
rows.imp_rat: Viable microorganisms on contact surfaces relates directly to the conservation goal, but the ranking places it below the prior batch, so relevance and severity are capped at the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP and swab testing are established, but residual biofilm and dead-legs mean viable organisms occasionally survive despite standard practice. Full CIP/sanitation cycles and verification swabbing take significant time within the maintenance window. Effective sanitation depends heavily on correct chemistry, contact time and technique, demanding trained sanitation staff. Sanitation chemicals, water and downtime represent a real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the production start delay caused by incomplete sanitation
rows.imp_rat: Production start delay from incomplete sanitation touches readiness (relevance) but is a delay rather than a contamination breach (severity); ranking holds it below 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-start sanitation checks catch many gaps, but residue or missed steps still occasionally delay the start. Incomplete sanitation forces re-cleaning that delays production start unpredictably. Verifying sanitation completeness relies on trained QA judgement and sampling. Re-cleaning wastes chemicals, water and startup time when sanitation is incomplete.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that temperature or pressure calibration is performed against a reference with invalid calibration status
rows.imp_rat: Calibrating against an invalid reference could compromise sterilization accuracy, but this maintenance-time error is one step removed from routine job execution. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Traceable-standard programs with certificate and due-date checks are mature, making use of an out-of-cal reference uncommon where discipline holds. Checking a reference standard's calibration certificate before use is a quick, routine step. Verifying calibration status of references is basic metrology practice well established in regulated plants. Maintaining calibrated reference standards has a recurring calibration cost that is well understood and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that calibration values are applied to the wrong temperature or pressure sensor channel
rows.imp_rat: Applying calibration to the wrong sensor channel could affect process accuracy, but the ranking places it below prior items, so both proxies are held at the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Channel mix-ups persist because many systems lack automated channel verification, leaving the outcome dependent on manual care. Verifying channel mapping adds checking time that is often skipped under schedule pressure. Correctly matching calibration values to sensor channels requires careful, experienced attention that is error-prone. No special equipment is needed, but a misapplied calibration can cause costly bad batches or rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterilizer unavailability beyond the scheduled maintenance window due to sensor calibration
rows.imp_rat: Sterilizer unavailability beyond the window due to sensor calibration affects uptime (relevance) but is a bounded maintenance overrun (severity); held at or below the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established calibration procedures usually keep it within window, but out-of-tolerance sensors occasionally force extended work. Sensor calibration sometimes overruns the maintenance window when references or drift issues appear. Proper calibration requires metrology skill and correct reference standards not always at hand. Calibration standards and technician time are moderate recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an interruption leaves the control software or firmware installation incomplete
rows.imp_rat: An incomplete firmware install disrupts control software rather than the primary sterilize/cool cycle (low relevance) and is typically recoverable by reinstall (low severity); bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Some controllers support dual-image or rollback, but many machines lack interruption protection, so incomplete installs remain a real hazard. A failed firmware update can force lengthy re-flashing or rollback, extending downtime unpredictably. Recovering an interrupted install often requires vendor-level expertise beyond typical site staff. Firmware update tooling is low-cost, but a bricked controller can incur expensive vendor callouts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine is returned to service with the original fault unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Returning the machine to service with an unresolved fault matters for reliability but is a repair-process error, not central to a single cycle's job. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on post-repair functional runs and checklists, which catch obvious faults but still let intermittent or misdiagnosed issues recur. Return-to-service checks add time, but the real issue is a fault reappearing after the machine is nominally repaired. Confirming a true fix depends heavily on the diagnosing technician's judgement, and root-cause skills vary widely across maintenance crews. Repeat failures waste labor and cause costly re-teardowns, though the direct part cost is usually modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous energy, pressure, or temperature during repair
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous energy during repair is a serious safety hazard (severity), but it is peripheral to the sterilizing machine's core job of conserving food (relevance); the batch ceiling caps it low regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Mature energy-isolation practice makes hazardous-energy exposure during repair uncommon where procedures are enforced. Isolation and cooldown before repair add mandatory but accepted delay. LOTO and depressurization for repair are broadly trained standard competencies. PPE and downtime are minor costs against the injuries avoided.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that repair work introduces contamination into the machine's sterile product path
rows.imp_rat: Repair work introducing contamination into the sterile path relates to conservation quality, but its lower ranking caps both relevance and severity at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Hygienic maintenance protocols exist, but repair work still occasionally introduces contamination that only later testing reveals. Protecting and re-sanitizing the product path after repair adds significant time to the job. Hygienic repair discipline requires trained aseptic-aware technicians whose care varies. Barriers, covers and re-sanitation chemicals are modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine downtime caused by unavailable replacement components
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from unavailable replacement components hurts uptime (relevance) but is a logistics/parts issue with recoverable impact (severity); ceiling caps it at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Spare-part availability is inconsistent, and long-lead components routinely leave machines idle far longer than planned. Waiting for unavailable spares is a chronic source of prolonged, unpredictable downtime. Sourcing or fabricating substitute parts under pressure demands scarce procurement and engineering judgement. Holding comprehensive spares inventory is costly, so sites under-stock and pay in downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of gaps in visibility of the machine's operating status
rows.imp_rat: Gaps in operating-status visibility affect monitoring rather than the sterilization itself (relevance) with generally minor consequences (severity), kept below the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established control-system telemetry keeps visibility gaps short and rare on well-integrated machines. Modern SCADA/HMI gives near-continuous status visibility with only brief gaps during reboots or comms drops. Reading standard status dashboards needs little specialized skill. Monitoring infrastructure is largely already in place, so incremental cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production disruption caused by a conflicting maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish disruption from a conflicting maintenance window is a scheduling conflict adjacent to the job (relevance) with recoverable consequences (severity); held below 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling tools help but cross-team coordination gaps still let conflicting windows disrupt production. Conflicting maintenance windows still frequently collide with sterile-fill production, causing avoidable disruption. Coordinating windows needs skilled planning across production and maintenance schedulers. Lost sterile-fill production time is expensive when windows conflict.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required replacement parts or maintenance supplies are unavailable for scheduled work
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability of replacement parts for scheduled work touches uptime but is a logistics/maintenance concern with modest consequences relative to the core sterilizing job. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Spare-parts planning via CMMS/BOM works for common items, but obsolete or long-lead parts still occasionally leave scheduled work unsupplied. Kitting parts before a window is standard, though procurement lead times can delay work. Parts staging is a routine stores function needing little specialist skill. Holding spares ties up inventory cost, the main trade-off in this need.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that valid reference standards are unavailable at the scheduled calibration
rows.imp_rat: Missing reference standards at calibration delays a support activity rather than the core sterilizing function (low relevance), and reschedule is a manageable inconvenience (low severity); held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration management systems with due-date tracking make valid standards available almost always, so lapses are rare. Scheduling calibration standards ahead is routine and seldom causes waiting when planned. Managing valid reference standards is a defined metrology task handled by calibration technicians. Maintaining in-date standards carries recurring cost but avoids expensive recalibration trips.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the installation surface deforms under the food sterilizing machine load
rows.imp_rat: Installation surface deforming under machine load is a site/foundation concern that only indirectly affects operation; ranked below batch 2, it is capped at low relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard floor-loading calculations against known machine weights make deformation surprises rare when done. Floor load verification is a planning-stage task that rarely delays preparation. Structural load checks need a civil/structural engineer's judgement. Reinforcement if needed is costly, but the assessment itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that shipping damage remains undetected before installation
rows.imp_rat: Undetected shipping damage is peripheral to the core sterilize-and-cool job (low relevance) and is typically caught at commissioning with modest consequences (low severity); capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Surface damage is caught reliably, but internal or concealed damage frequently escapes visual-only inspection. Visual damage inspection on arrival is fast and rarely delays the schedule. Spotting concealed shipping damage requires trained inspectors who know where hidden impact damage hides. Undetected damage found after install is very costly, and photographic documentation adds little expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of recipe configuration delay caused by conflicting product requirements
rows.imp_rat: Recipe configuration delay from conflicting product requirements is a setup-time inconvenience peripheral to core sterilization; the impact is minor lost time, keeping relevance and severity capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe templates and prior configurations reduce conflicts, though new product combinations still create recurring setup delays. Recipe configuration conflicts directly delay getting the machine ready to run. Resolving conflicting product requirements needs food-process recipe expertise. Configuration delay mainly costs labor time, not materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of product jams at machine-to-conveyor transfer points during continuous running
rows.imp_rat: Product jams at conveyor transfer points affect line flow but sit outside the sterilize-and-cool core function, with recoverable consequences. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer-point jamming is a chronic, hard-to-eliminate issue that standard practice mitigates only partially. Jams at transfer points repeatedly halt continuous running and eat commissioning time. Tuning transfer geometry and timing to stop jams requires experienced line-integration skill. Jams waste product and labor but not usually equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between machine throughput and adjacent conveying capacity during continuous running
rows.imp_rat: Throughput-to-conveyor mismatch is a line-balancing concern peripheral to the core sterilize-and-cool job (relevance) with mild consequences (severity), held below the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Rate capacities are usually specified up front, so gross mismatches are uncommon though fine balancing gaps persist. Throughput mismatch surfaces during runs and can force rate re-tuning, taking time. Balancing rates across equipment needs process engineering judgement. Mismatch causes underutilization or backup waste of modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a machine network address conflict disrupting an active facility device during commissioning
rows.imp_rat: A network address conflict during commissioning is a one-time IT integration error, not central to sterilizing food; the disruption is transient and recoverable, so both proxies stay minimal under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Managed address schemes and IT coordination make conflicts avoidable, but ad-hoc commissioning still occasionally clashes with active devices. An address conflict can disrupt live devices and force troubleshooting time. IP/address planning is a defined network-admin skill widely available. Conflicts cost troubleshooting labor rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required machine components unavailable when assembly begins
rows.imp_rat: Missing components at assembly start delays build but does not directly govern the machine's sterilize-and-cool function; a schedule slip is a modest, recoverable consequence, so both proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Kitting and receiving checks reduce shortages, yet late or back-ordered parts still frequently delay assembly starts. Missing components at assembly start cause direct waiting and stall the build. Kitting and staging require ordinary logistics coordination, not scarce expertise. Idle crews waiting on parts is an expensive but often-realized loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the closure load hold while completion evidence is reviewed
rows.imp_rat: Load hold during evidence review is a queue delay with modest stakes to sterilization efficacy; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Review holds are predictable but manual record review keeps the duration longer than necessary. The load hold during completion-evidence review is an inherent delay before release. Efficient review depends on reviewer familiarity with the record. Hold time delays availability but consumes little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cycle starts blocked by unmet chamber or load prerequisites
rows.imp_rat: Cycle starts blocked by unmet prerequisites is a throughput delay, though prerequisites protect the job; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks appropriately block starts, but the resulting delays are a routine and only partly mitigated nuisance. Prerequisite blocks delay cycle starts, costing throughput time. Resolving blocks needs some operator knowledge of prerequisites. Blocked starts waste operator time but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of start-command rejection after prerequisites are satisfied
rows.imp_rat: Start-command rejection after prerequisites met is a control-reliability nuisance with limited stakes; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Modern controls rarely reject a valid start once prerequisites are satisfied, so this is fairly well served. Start-command rejection after prerequisites are met causes lost time troubleshooting. Clearing spurious rejections may need operator familiarity with the control system. Delays cost time but not material directly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from alarm onset to operator recognition
rows.imp_rat: Alarm-to-recognition latency is a human-response detail with limited direct impact on SAL; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Standard alarm annunciation makes recognition prompt, though unattended operation can delay it. Alarm-to-recognition time is short with audible/visual alarms. Recognizing an alarm needs minimal skill. Quick recognition limits downstream cost of the event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of closures or load components damaged during placement
rows.imp_rat: Closures damaged during placement is a yield/handling loss with little effect on sterilization efficacy; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Closures are relatively robust and damage during placement is infrequent with normal handling. Careful placement to avoid damage adds a little handling time. Damage-free placement depends on operator handling care. Damaged closures or components are direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of positioning delay caused by an unavailable loading path or chamber
rows.imp_rat: Positioning delay from unavailable path is a logistics latency with minor bearing on SAL; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and single-chamber constraints make positioning delays a recurring, only partly managed problem. Unavailable loading path or chamber directly causes positioning delay. Little skill involved; it is a scheduling/availability issue. Waiting for a path ties up staff time and idles the unit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of processed closures damaged during removal
rows.imp_rat: Processed closures damaged during removal is a yield loss not affecting achieved sterility; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Routine removal rarely damages robust closures, so this is well served. Careful removal adds modest handling time. Damage-free removal depends on operator technique. Damaged closures during removal are wasted material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of processed closure material lost through dropping or spilling during removal
rows.imp_rat: Material lost during removal is a yield waste with negligible effect on achieved sterility; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Containers and trays make spillage during removal uncommon. Careful transfer to avoid spills adds slight time. Avoiding drops depends on operator handling care. Dropped or spilled closures are directly lost material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required load attributes conflicting across cycle and batch records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting load attributes across records is a documentation reconciliation issue with limited direct impact on SAL attainment.
rows.sat_rat: Manual transcription across cycle and batch records commonly produces small conflicts requiring reconciliation. Reconciling attributes across records adds review time. Detecting conflicts requires careful cross-checking skill. Conflicting records can trigger investigations and delays.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty about the immediate alarm response
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about immediate alarm response is a brief human-factors ambiguity with limited stakes; low and capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of composure loss under simultaneous or conflicting alarms
rows.imp_rat: Composure loss under conflicting alarms is a human-factors stress concern with limited direct stakes; low and capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of second-guessing the escalation decision as alarm conditions evolve
rows.imp_rat: Second-guessing escalation decisions is a human-factors doubt with minimal direct stakes to SAL; low and capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while conflicting cycle-acceptance evidence is reconciled
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while reconciling acceptance evidence is a human-experience ambiguity with minor stakes to SAL; low and capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when the release rationale is challenged
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when release rationale is challenged is a personal-poise error remote from the sterilization job (low relevance) and easily absorbed (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety triggered by unexpected heat or pressure cues during unloading
rows.imp_rat: Operator anxiety from heat/pressure cues during unloading is peripheral to sterilizing closures to SAL (low relevance) and consequences are personal discomfort, easily absorbed (low severity); capped below batch above.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of lingering doubt that closure sterility remains protected during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt about sterility during transfer is a post-cycle reassurance concern peripheral to the sterilization job; low and capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an audit observation being attributed to the operator's cycle records
rows.imp_rat: An audit observation attributed to operator cycle records is a personal-accountability concern peripheral to sterilizing (low relevance) with absorbable consequences (low severity); capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility loss with Quality Assurance over cycle-record accuracy
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss with QA over cycle-record accuracy is a perception error tangential to the sterilization act (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity); capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the operator being bypassed in disposition decisions because cycle records are distrusted
rows.imp_rat: Operator bypassed in disposition due to distrusted records is a role-perception concern remote from sterilizing (low relevance) with absorbable consequences (low severity); capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a rejected closure load being attributed to the operator's cycle execution
rows.imp_rat: A rejected load attributed to operator execution is an accountability-perception concern tangential to sterilizing to SAL (low relevance) with contained consequences (low severity); capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility loss when explaining cycle evidence during release review
