rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss explaining cycle evidence at review is a perception error peripheral to the sterilization job (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of leadership second-guessing the operator's load-readiness judgments
rows.imp_rat: Leadership second-guessing load-readiness judgments is a role-credibility issue peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) and recoverable (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the operator being bypassed in decisions about high-consequence closure loads
rows.imp_rat: Operator bypassed on high-consequence loads is a role-perception issue tangential to the sterilization act (low relevance) and recoverable (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure packages filled outside specified limits
rows.imp_rat: Closure packages filled outside limits is a fill-quantity detail with minor bearing on chamber sterilization efficacy.
rows.sat_rat: Fill specifications and checks keep out-of-limit filling reasonably controlled. Checking fill levels adds a short step. Judging fill limits is a straightforward, learnable task. Improper fill can impair sterilization, causing rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prepared closures spilled or lost while awaiting sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Prepared closures spilled while awaiting sterilization is a material-loss waste with minor stakes to the core job; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Contained packaging makes significant spills uncommon during staging. Careful handling to avoid spills adds negligible time. Preventing spills is basic handling discipline. The core loss from spilled or lost closures is wasted material, though quantities are usually small.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of closure loads staged at the wrong autoclave or queue location
rows.imp_rat: Loads staged at the wrong location is a logistics mix-up with modest immediate stakes to sterilization; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Designated staging areas and labeling make wrong-location errors relatively infrequent. Staging at the correct location is quick when signage/scheduling is clear. Correct placement relies on following queue rules, not expertise. Wrong-location staging causes delays more than material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of autoclave access obstruction caused by staged closure loads
rows.imp_rat: Access obstruction from staged loads is a workflow delay peripheral to achieving SAL; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Defined staging footprints and flow layouts generally keep access obstruction brief and infrequent. This need is about minimizing the duration that staged loads block autoclave access, a throughput/flow issue. Keeping access clear is basic housekeeping, not specialized skill. Access obstruction delays processing rather than consuming material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of closure loads staged outside the scheduled sterilization sequence
rows.imp_rat: Loads staged out of scheduled sequence is a scheduling error with minor stakes to sterilization; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling boards and queue rules help but sequence errors still occur under pressure. Sequencing loads correctly is fundamentally a scheduling/throughput concern. Following the sequence requires coordination rather than skill. Out-of-sequence staging risks hold-time excursions and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unresolved doubt that handled closures remained protected during wrapping
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that handled closures stayed protected during wrapping is a residual-assurance ambiguity with minor stakes; low and capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety after interruptions in the closure-wrapping sequence
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety after wrapping-sequence interruptions is a personal-emotional concern peripheral to SAL attainment (low relevance) with trivial consequences (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty that the finalized closure load meets approved build criteria
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that the finished load meets build criteria is a reassurance-latency item peripheral to the cycle; low and capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while a load-build discrepancy awaits clarification
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety during load-build discrepancy clarification is an emotional/duration concern peripheral to sterilizing to SAL (low relevance) with trivial consequences (low severity); capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when asked to justify the finalized closure-load build
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure justifying the load build is a personal-poise concern remote from the sterilization job (low relevance) and low-stakes (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty that the closure load will reach the autoclave before its scheduled start
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about load arrival before start is a scheduling-anxiety item with minor stakes; low and capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when conflicting readiness signals require an immediate staging decision
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure under conflicting readiness signals is a personal-poise concern remote from the CFJ (low relevance) with low-stakes consequences (low severity); capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety as the staging window narrows without readiness confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety as staging window narrows is an emotional/timing concern tangential to sterilizing to SAL (low relevance) and easily absorbed (low severity); capped.

rows.imp_rat: Load rejection publicly attributed to operator prep is an accountability-perception issue peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) and recoverable (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of extra verification imposed because the operator's closure-load preparation is doubted
rows.imp_rat: Extra verification imposed due to doubted prep is a rework/perception concern tangential to sterilizing to SAL (low relevance) with absorbable impact (low severity); capped.

rows.imp_rat: Operator bypassed for high-priority loads over doubted prep is a role-perception issue peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) and recoverable (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.imp_rat: Operator corrections cited as unreliable prep is a reputation-perception concern tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity); capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of reviewers second-guessing the operator's sterility-critical load judgments
rows.imp_rat: Reviewers second-guessing operator load judgments is a credibility concern peripheral to sterilizing (low relevance) with absorbable impact (low severity); capped.

rows.source_job: The Trusted Guardian of Sterility Assurance
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the operator being bypassed for sterility-critical closure-load responsibilities because of perceived unreliability
rows.imp_rat: Operator bypassed for sterility-critical duties over unreliability is a role-perception concern tangential to the sterilization act (low relevance) and recoverable (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required evidence elements left undefined for applying the acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Undefined evidence elements for acceptance criteria is an administrative gap with minor immediate stakes to the core sterilization job.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures usually define evidence requirements, though ambiguities in application still surface. Defining required evidence is done during procedure authoring. Specifying exactly what evidence is needed to apply criteria requires validation authoring skill. Undefined evidence needs cause rework when disputes arise.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of validated-state decision delays caused by unavailable supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Validated-state decision delays from missing evidence is an administrative latency held low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence usually exists but access delays recur, so decisions are reliably made but slowly. Delays in validated-state decisions from unavailable evidence directly extend decision timelines. Locating supporting evidence depends on records systems more than expertise. Decision delays stall changes and hold product, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for required cycle evidence before processed-load disposition
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for cycle evidence before disposition is a throughput delay with modest stakes to the sterilization job itself.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence is eventually complete, so the issue is delay rather than missing data. This need is explicitly about waiting for cycle evidence before disposition, and evidence assembly delays are a common throughput drag. Speeding evidence availability depends on data-management practices more than reviewer skill. Waiting on evidence extends inventory holding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time to retrieve complete sterilization-assurance evidence for a selected closure lot
rows.imp_rat: Retrieval time for evidence is a records-access latency with modest stakes to the sterilization job; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence is retrievable but often not quickly, especially where paper records persist, making this a moderate time pain. This need is explicitly about retrieval time for assurance evidence, which hybrid paper/electronic systems can make slow. Retrieval efficiency depends on archiving systems rather than scarce skill. Slow retrieval consumes labor and delays lot release and audits.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between cycle completion and verification of required monitoring evidence
rows.imp_rat: Time to verify monitoring evidence after completion is a cycle-time delay, peripheral and low-severity; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Automated logging makes evidence promptly available in modern systems, so this is reasonably served. The need targets the lag between cycle completion and evidence verification, which electronic systems shorten but do not eliminate. Verification speed depends on process design rather than scarce skill. Verification delay adds modest carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from availability of required closure-load evidence to final disposition
rows.imp_rat: Time from evidence availability to disposition is a throughput delay with modest stakes; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Disposition eventually completes reliably, but queue times and manual compilation make turnaround inconsistent. This need is squarely about disposition turnaround once evidence is available, and delays here are routine. Speed depends on reviewer availability and familiarity rather than deep skill. Slow disposition ties up inventory and staff, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that a revised applicable requirement remains absent from approved closure sterilization requirements
rows.imp_rat: Delay in updating requirements is an administrative lag with limited immediate impact on cycle execution; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control triggers eventually update requirements, but the interim absence of revised requirements is a recognized, poorly bounded gap. The need is explicitly about the lag before a revised requirement is incorporated, a chronic latency. Assessing and implementing changed requirements needs regulatory and validation skill. Delayed incorporation risks compliance cost but little direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of relevant load configurations and operating conditions absent from corrective-effectiveness evidence
rows.imp_rat: Missing load configurations from effectiveness evidence is a documentation-completeness issue tangential to actual SAL attainment (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Effectiveness evidence commonly omits some load configurations and operating conditions, leaving coverage gaps that are only loosely controlled. Broadening evidence to all configurations extends verification time. Selecting the full set of relevant configurations and conditions needs validation judgement often narrowed under pressure. Testing more configurations raises verification cost, discouraging completeness.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for restored closure sterilization compliance
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in restored-compliance acceptance criteria is a niche CAPA-stage concern with minor direct stakes; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA closure criteria are often qualitatively defined, leaving real ambiguity that undermines consistent restoration decisions. Ambiguity in restored-compliance criteria slows decisions only indirectly. Interpreting vague acceptance criteria for restored compliance depends heavily on senior QA judgement. Ambiguity causes occasional rework rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aggregated sterilization KPIs conceal adverse trends in cycle outcomes
rows.imp_rat: Aggregated KPIs concealing adverse trends is a reporting-visibility issue somewhat removed from individual load SAL (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: KPI dashboards exist but aggregation routinely masks adverse cycle-outcome trends, and disaggregated trending is inconsistently performed. Trend analysis is periodic reporting, not a time bottleneck. Detecting adverse trends hidden by aggregation demands statistical and analytical skill that is unevenly applied. Analytics tooling is a modest, largely existing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that concurrent changes prevent attribution of restored compliance to the corrective action
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent changes blocking attribution of restored compliance is a CAPA-analysis ambiguity peripheral to SAL; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control rarely enforces isolation of concurrent changes, so confident attribution of restored compliance is frequently compromised. Attribution problems are not primarily a time cost. Isolating a corrective action's effect amid concurrent changes requires analytical rigor that is often lacking. Misattribution risks wasted corrective effort rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from corrective-action implementation to completed effectiveness determination
rows.imp_rat: Time from corrective action to effectiveness determination is a CAPA-cycle latency with limited direct stakes; low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Effectiveness determinations complete but on inconsistent, often extended timelines with weak scheduling controls. This need is explicitly the elapsed time from CAPA implementation to effectiveness determination, often protracted. Timely determination depends on planning discipline more than scarce skill. Long effectiveness cycles prolong resource commitment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of effectiveness conclusions lacking traceable linkage to the deviation, action, evidence, or load status
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable effectiveness conclusions are a documentation-linkage concern, tangential to the actual sterilization CFJ (low relevance) with contained consequences (low severity); ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA templates prompt linkage, but effectiveness conclusions lacking full traceable linkage remain a common audit observation. Building traceable linkage adds documentation time to CAPA closure. Constructing complete deviation-action-evidence linkage requires disciplined CAPA authorship. Linkage is documentation labor, modest in cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of doubt that undiscovered documentation gaps remain after closure-load disposition sign-off
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about undiscovered documentation gaps after sign-off is a confidence/completeness issue tangential to the sterilization act (low relevance) and mild-stakes (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether the closure sterilization record supports release
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty about release support is a decision-latency issue, peripheral and low; capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when closure-load records present conflicting release evidence

Managing quote validity windows is routine procurement practice. Expired pricing typically forces higher re-quotes, adding committed cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of additional committed expenditure caused by installation and qualification schedule slippage
rows.imp_rat: Expenditure from schedule slippage is a budget/timing concern peripheral to achieving SAL; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Slippage-driven cost is a frequent, imperfectly managed occurrence. Schedule slippage in install/qualification extends timelines and drives standby cost. Preventing slippage needs project-scheduling expertise. Qualification schedule slip generates extra committed expenditure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of budget contingency committed before major technical and commercial uncertainties are resolved
rows.imp_rat: Contingency committed before uncertainties resolve is a budgeting-discipline concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Staged contingency-release practices exist but are inconsistently enforced. Contingency-commitment timing is governed by staged-release rules that add discipline. Managing contingency drawdown needs project-controls skill. Committing contingency before uncertainties resolve wastes buffer capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between capital authorization and qualified sterile fill-finish use
rows.imp_rat: Delay between authorization and qualified use is a timing concern peripheral to the physical sterilization function; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Time-to-qualified-use varies widely and is hard to guarantee. The lag from authorization to qualified use is long and a recurring pain. Compressing this lead time needs project-execution and validation expertise. Delay ties up capital without returns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of funded closure sterilization capacity unused during the planned operating period
rows.imp_rat: Unused funded capacity is a capacity-utilization waste, touching the job but ranked below the prior batch; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Utilization outcomes are uncertain and frequently miss projections. Utilization only becomes visible during the operating period, not at sanction. Right-sizing to avoid unused capacity needs demand-forecasting skill. Funded-but-unused capacity is stranded capital wasted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of closure sterilization throughput shortfall relative to sterile fill-finish demand
rows.imp_rat: Throughput shortfall relative to fill-finish demand touches the job but is ranked below the prior batch; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Capacity-to-demand matching is imperfect, so shortfalls occur. Throughput shortfall manifests in operation and is hard to foresee at funding. Matching sterilization throughput to fill-finish demand needs process-engineering skill. Throughput shortfall causes lost production value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unplanned operating expense incurred while sterilizing closures to the defined SAL
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned operating expense while sterilizing to SAL touches the job but is primarily a cost concern; ranking caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Such costs are erratic and poorly forecast in typical cases. Unplanned operating cost is realized during operation, not at authorization. Anticipating unplanned OpEx needs operational experience with similar autoclaves. Unplanned operating expense to hold SAL is often omitted from projections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned autoclave downtime during scheduled closure sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned downtime during scheduled sterilization affects the job's availability but is capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive-maintenance regimes make downtime fairly controlled once the asset operates. Unplanned downtime directly interrupts scheduled sterilization time. Minimizing downtime needs maintenance-engineering skill. Downtime causes lost output and rescheduling cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of closure sterilization cycles failing validated acceptance criteria for the defined SAL
rows.imp_rat: Cycles failing validated SAL acceptance is directly central to the CFJ, yet the ranking mandates a cap at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cycle-acceptance practice makes SAL-conforming cycles fairly reliable once qualified. Cycle failures against SAL criteria are operational events beyond the funding decision. Preventing cycle failure needs validation and sterilization expertise, not the executor's. Failed cycles waste closures and utilities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closures scrapped because of sterilization-related damage
rows.imp_rat: Closures scrapped from sterilization damage touches the job's output quality but is capped by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified cycles keep sterilization-damage scrap relatively low and consistent. Scrap from sterilization damage is an operational metric outside the funding step. Reducing scrap needs process-engineering skill in cycle design. Scrapped closures are direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the closure sterilization investment loses regulatory suitability before its planned economic end of life
rows.imp_rat: Investment losing regulatory suitability before end of life is a lifecycle-planning concern remote from the CFJ (low relevance) and absorbable (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Long-horizon regulatory-fit uncertainty is poorly addressed by standard investment practice. Assessing regulatory longevity over the asset life is a forward-looking exercise not bounded by immediate schedule. Judging future regulatory suitability demands specialized regulatory-affairs foresight beyond the executor's remit. Premature regulatory obsolescence risks stranded capital that current cases rarely price.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of understatement of sterility-assurance and compliance consequences in the investment case
rows.imp_rat: Understated sterility/compliance consequences in the investment case is a business-case-framing error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) and absorbable (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Investment cases frequently soft-pedal sterility and compliance risk, so the true stakes are inconsistently represented. Compiling compliance-consequence narratives into an investment case is time-intensive but usually fits within the approval timeline. Articulating sterility-assurance and regulatory consequences requires quality-and-compliance expertise the purchase executor must borrow. Understating compliance consequences hides potential remediation and lost-batch costs from the case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in valuation assumptions across competing capital investments
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in valuation assumptions across investments is a modeling matter peripheral to the sterilization job; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Central finance standards help but inter-project consistency remains imperfect. Harmonizing valuation across competing projects is a portfolio-review step done periodically. Consistent cross-project valuation needs central finance governance skill. Inconsistent valuation misallocates capital across projects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between projected and evidence-supported closure sterilization capacity requirements
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between projected and evidence-supported capacity is a planning-estimate error tangential to achieving SAL on a load (low relevance) with correctable impact (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Capacity projections often diverge from evidence, making this an unreliable input. Validating capacity requirements against evidence takes iterative demand-engineering work. Sizing sterilization capacity needs process-engineering expertise the executor relies on others for. Capacity mis-sizing leads to either stranded capital or costly future expansion.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of financial consequence omitted for deferring the closure sterilization investment
rows.imp_rat: Omitted cost of deferring the investment is a business-case-completeness error tangential to the sterilization job (low relevance) and absorbable (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deferral scenarios are seldom modeled, so this dimension is unreliably covered. Deferral-cost analysis is rarely prepared promptly, delaying informed timing decisions. Quantifying cost of delay needs combined financial and operational modeling skill. The financial consequence of deferring the investment is commonly omitted, hiding real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of investment benefit counted more than once across interdependent proposals
rows.imp_rat: Benefit double-counted across proposals is a modeling-accuracy defect peripheral to sterilizing closures; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit double-counting is a subtle, frequently missed error. Checking for double-counted benefits across proposals adds review effort. Detecting benefit overlap across interdependent proposals needs portfolio-finance skill. Double-counted benefits overstate value and justify overspend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of capital prioritization delay caused by missing technical, validation, or financial evidence
rows.imp_rat: Prioritization delay from missing evidence is a governance-timing concern peripheral to sterilizing closures; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence gaps recur, so prioritization timing is inconsistent. Missing technical or financial evidence directly prolongs prioritization delay. Assembling required evidence needs multi-disciplinary coordination. Prioritization delay carries opportunity cost rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unresolved stakeholder conflict over the priority of the closure sterilization investment
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved stakeholder conflict over investment priority is a governance concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Priority conflicts recur and resolve inconsistently. Unresolved priority conflict prolongs the decision, a common source of delay. Resolving priority disputes needs stakeholder-negotiation skill. Conflict delay carries opportunity cost, not direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of financial exposure omitted for qualification failures that prevent achievement of the defined SAL
rows.imp_rat: Omitted financial exposure for qualification failures is a business-case-completeness error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) and absorbable (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because failure scenarios are rarely modeled, the case's completeness is unreliable. Quantifying qualification-failure exposure lags until validation planning matures, delaying complete cost pictures. Estimating financial exposure of qualification failure needs combined validation and financial modeling skill that is scarce. Qualification-failure financial exposure is commonly omitted, understating the real capital at risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of capital exposure omitted because responsibility and scope boundaries remain unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Omitted capital exposure from unresolved scope boundaries is a business-case-completeness error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with correctable impact (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Boundary omissions recur despite scope-of-supply matrices, so coverage is inconsistent. Resolving scope boundaries before pricing takes iterative clarification time. Defining responsibility boundaries needs experienced procurement and engineering coordination. Unresolved boundaries leave capital exposure unbudgeted until it surfaces as overrun.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of capital exposure omitted for facility and utility integration requirements
rows.imp_rat: Omitted facility/utility integration capital exposure is a budgeting-accuracy defect, not central to running the sterilization cycle; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Integration exposure is systematically underestimated in typical cases. Capturing facility and utility integration cost requires design detail that arrives late. Estimating integration cost needs facilities-engineering expertise. Utility integration costs are frequently omitted, understating total capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of financial exposure omitted for delays to installation, qualification, and production readiness
rows.imp_rat: Omitted financial exposure for readiness delays is a budgeting concern, not the sterilization function; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Schedule-risk costing is inconsistently applied, leaving unreliable exposure figures. Delay-exposure analysis is often skipped under schedule pressure. Modeling installation and qualification delay cost needs project-controls skill. Financial exposure to schedule slip is commonly omitted from the case.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of financial exposure omitted for unplanned sterilization outages and resulting closure shortages
rows.imp_rat: Omitted exposure for outages and closure shortages is a financial-completeness defect, not central to sterilizing closures; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Outage-driven exposure is unreliably represented in investment cases. Outage-and-shortage cost analysis is seldom prepared in time. Estimating shortage impact needs supply-chain and finance expertise. Exposure from sterilization outages and closure shortages is often omitted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of operating and lifecycle cost categories excluded from the financial risk analysis
rows.imp_rat: Excluded cost categories from risk analysis is a financial-completeness defect, not central to sterilizing closures; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Category omissions from risk analysis are common, undermining completeness. Ensuring all cost categories enter the risk analysis extends preparation. Comprehensive lifecycle-cost scoping needs cost-engineering skill. Excluded operating and lifecycle categories understate financial risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of capital exposure omitted because of changes in currency values, price escalation, or financing terms
rows.imp_rat: Omitted capital exposure from currency/escalation changes is a financial-modeling defect, not the CFJ; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation clauses are often handled but FX and financing shifts still surprise cases. Currency and escalation adjustment is a quick standard financial step. Applying FX, escalation, and financing assumptions is routine treasury skill. Unadjusted currency and escalation can leave material capital exposure unbudgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of distortion in financial exposure caused by modeling dependent risk events independently
rows.imp_rat: Distortion from modeling dependent risks independently is a financial-modeling defect peripheral to the sterilization job; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Dependent-risk distortion is almost never corrected in standard practice. Correlated-risk modeling is advanced and rarely attempted, so it adds little routine time. Modeling dependent risk events needs advanced quantitative skill seldom available. Treating risks independently distorts total financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of shortfall between projected economic value and the required funding hurdle
rows.imp_rat: Shortfall between projected value and funding hurdle is a business-case-financial error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) and correctable (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Hurdle-rate testing is a mature, consistent gate, so this need is fairly well handled. Comparing economic value against hurdle rates is a standard, quick spreadsheet step. NPV/IRR-versus-hurdle analysis is routine finance work with widely available skill. A shortfall against the hurdle directly signals inefficient use of capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty that the investment will enable closure sterilization to the defined SAL
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that the investment enables SAL is an investment-confidence concern tangential to executing a cycle (low relevance) with contained stakes (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Even with URS and vendor qualification data, certainty of achieving SAL before purchase remains an inherently probabilistic, imperfectly served concern. Reducing uncertainty that the investment will deliver SAL relies on validation evidence assembled slowly across the project. Assessing whether an autoclave design will meet SAL needs deep microbiological and thermal-validation judgement rarely held by the executor. Residual uncertainty forces conservative contingency spending or costly late redesign.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which available capital falls short of the validated closure sterilization investment requirement
rows.imp_rat: Capital falling short of investment requirement is a funding error peripheral to the sterilization CFJ (low relevance) with absorbable consequences (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: The gap between funding and validated need is inconsistently characterized, so shortfalls surprise approvers repeatedly. Identifying capital shortfall against a validated requirement typically waits for the full investment case, delaying resolution. Translating a validated sterilization requirement into a firm capital figure needs cross-functional engineering-finance skill that is not always available. Available capital routinely falls short of the true validated requirement because early estimates omit qualification and integration costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among approval stakeholders about authorization criteria for the closure sterilization investment
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement about authorization criteria is a governance-alignment matter peripheral to the sterilization job; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria disagreement among approvers is common and inconsistently resolved. Aligning stakeholders on authorization criteria takes iterative meetings. Building consensus on criteria needs stakeholder-management skill. Criteria disagreement rarely adds direct cost but stalls decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the closure sterilization funding request waits for required reviews and signatures
rows.imp_rat: Time the funding request waits for signatures is a governance-timing delay peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow and e-signature tools make routing fairly reliable though queues persist. Funding requests waiting for reviews and signatures is a chronic, familiar delay. Chasing signatures is routine administrative work needing little specialist skill. Waiting delays carry opportunity cost, not direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting financial, procurement, and technical assumptions across approval documents
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting assumptions across approval documents is a documentation-consistency issue, not the sterilization function; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Document-to-document assumption conflicts are frequent and hard to catch. Reconciling assumptions across approval documents consumes review cycles. Cross-document consistency checking needs meticulous coordination skill. Conflicting assumptions can hide true cost until after approval.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of closure sterilization investment-scope changes after authorization review begins
rows.imp_rat: Investment-scope changes after review begins is a stability concern peripheral to achieving SAL; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Scope changes after review begins are a recurring disruption. Scope changes during review reset the clock and add delay. Freezing scope needs disciplined change-control skill. Mid-review scope changes drive rework and added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the funding request is routed to approvers lacking sufficient authorization authority
rows.imp_rat: Funding routed to approvers lacking authority is an administrative-rework matter peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Delegation-of-authority matrices make correct routing fairly reliable. Misrouting to wrong approvers causes rework loops that extend timelines. Knowing the correct authority matrix is routine governance knowledge. Misrouting wastes effort but little direct money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved approval conditions blocking release of capital for the closure sterilization investment
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved approval conditions blocking capital release is a funding-process delay peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with absorbable impact (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Approval-condition tracking via stage-gate workflows is a fairly mature practice, so blockages are usually visible and eventually cleared. Unresolved approval conditions directly stall capital release and are a recurring source of delay. Clearing conditions requires coordinating multiple experts, though the executor is practiced at chasing sign-offs. Held capital carries opportunity cost but is not itself a direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when reviewers challenge the autoclave financial rationale
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when financial rationale is challenged is an emotional concern peripheral to the sterilization job; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt caused by conflicts among the business case, approval record, and sourcing evidence
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicts among business case and evidence is a documentation-clarity concern, not the sterilization function; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of feeling exposed when the approver and rationale for an autoclave sourcing exception cannot be explained
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over an unexplained sourcing exception is an emotional/compliance concern, not central to sterilizing closures; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting requested approval or sourcing evidence for the autoclave authorization
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting approval/sourcing evidence is an emotional/timing concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty when the autoclave commitment's fit within the approved budget is challenged
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty over budget fit when challenged is a financial-communication concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when asked to substantiate the autoclave lifecycle economics
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure substantiating lifecycle economics is an emotional concern, not central to achieving SAL; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt caused by conflicting autoclave cost assumptions from finance, procurement, and manufacturing
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting cost assumptions is an information-clarity matter, not central to sterilizing closures; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of alarm when omitted lifecycle obligations surface during scrutiny of the financial case
rows.imp_rat: Alarm when omitted lifecycle obligations surface is an emotional/financial concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty when defined-SAL defensibility is challenged during capital review
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty when SAL defensibility is challenged is a communication concern, though SAL is core; ranking caps it low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt triggered by conflicting quality, validation, and regulatory interpretations before funds are committed
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting regulatory interpretations before funding is a decision-clarity concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while sterility-scrutiny questions remain unanswered during the funding decision
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while sterility-scrutiny questions remain unanswered is an emotional concern, not the sterilization job; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when reviewers demand immediate justification of SAL defensibility
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure justifying SAL defensibility is an emotional concern, though SAL is core; ranking mandates a low cap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an indefensible sterility-assurance investment approval is publicly attributed to the authorizer
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of indefensible approval to the authorizer is reputational, though SAL is core; ranking caps it low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with quality and validation leaders when the authorization basis is challenged
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when authorization basis is challenged is a reputational concern peripheral to the sterilization job; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which capital governance stakeholders second-guess the authorizer's compliance judgment
rows.imp_rat: Governance second-guessing compliance judgment is a reputational concern, not the CFJ; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that executive stakeholders characterize the authorizer as prioritizing cost over validation and regulatory defensibility
rows.imp_rat: Being characterized as prioritizing cost over compliance is a reputational concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an audit finding is attributed to the purchase executor's incomplete capital decision trail
rows.imp_rat: Audit finding from incomplete decision trail is a compliance-documentation concern, not the sterilization job; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing credibility with senior leadership when the sterilization business case is judged financially unsound
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when the business case is judged unsound is a reputational concern, not central to achieving SAL; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of finance governance second-guessing the purchase executor because competitive sourcing evidence appears insufficient
rows.imp_rat: Finance second-guessing over sourcing evidence is a reputational/documentation concern peripheral to achieving SAL; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being bypassed in future capital authorization decisions because of perceived commercial bias
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed in future authorizations over perceived bias is an influence concern, not the sterilization function; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of receiving senior-leadership blame for missed financial commitments tied to the sterilization investment
rows.imp_rat: Blame for missed financial commitments is a reputational-financial concern peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between completion of maintenance or sanitation work and its documentation in the machine's history
rows.plain: There should be less time between finishing maintenance or cleaning work and recording it in the machine’s history. This gives people a timely and reliable record of what was done.
rows.imp_rat: Lag in documenting maintenance is a record-keeping delay, peripheral to the sterilization job (low relevance) with modest consequences (low severity); capped at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Delayed documentation increases the chance details are forgotten or mis-recorded, but the primary loss the stage feels is the time lag itself. Documentation is commonly deferred to end of shift or batched later, creating a persistent lag between finishing work and recording it. Logging promptly needs no special skill, so the delay is a workflow habit rather than a capability gap. Real-time entry tools are cheap, so the delay is not a cost issue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the machine's maintenance and sanitation history being unavailable when needed for traceability or audits
rows.plain: Keep the machine’s records of repairs, cleaning, and sanitation available when people need to check what was done. This matters for proving the machine was properly maintained during reviews or inspections.
rows.imp_rat: Missing maintenance/sanitation history for audits is a traceability concern (relevance) with administrative rather than functional consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulated facilities keep maintenance and sanitation logs, but gaps, manual-entry omissions and disconnected systems still leave records missing when needed. Retrieving history for an audit can be quick with a CMMS but slow when records are paper or scattered. Logging and pulling records is routine clerical work needing little specialist skill. CMMS and logbook systems are widely adopted at modest cost in regulated food plants.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved discrepancies between machine-history entries and supporting maintenance or sanitation records
rows.plain: Resolve differences between the machine’s history entries and the maintenance or cleaning records that support them. This keeps the equipment history accurate and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved discrepancies between machine-history and maintenance/sanitation records are a documentation-reconciliation error, tangential to sterilizing, with audit-follow-up consequences. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: With separate, often paper-based systems, discrepancies between machine-history and maintenance/sanitation records commonly go unreconciled until audit. Reconciling history entries against supporting records is slow manual cross-referencing. Resolving discrepancies requires experienced reviewers who understand both the machine history and the source records. Unresolved discrepancies cause costly investigations and audit exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of replacement components incompatible with the machine's mechanical or control interfaces
rows.plain: Choose replacement parts that fit the machine and work with its physical connections and controls. This helps prevent installation problems and machine failures.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible replacement components is a spare-parts interface defect affecting repair, not the core cycle directly; held to 1.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: OEM part numbers and approved-supplier lists mitigate most cases, but interface mismatches on updated or mixed-vintage machines still recur. Confirming compatibility before install adds minor time when documentation is at hand. Detecting subtle mechanical or control-interface incompatibility requires experienced technicians who know the machine's revisions. Incompatible parts cause returns, rework and downtime, a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption while completing the component upgrade
rows.plain: Finish replacing or improving machine parts with as little production stoppage as possible. This lets the upgraded machine return to work sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Interruption while completing a component upgrade is a one-off project event, marginal to the ongoing sterilizing job (relevance) with trivial lasting consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Upgrades frequently surface unforeseen integration issues that prolong the interruption unpredictably. Component upgrades often require extended line stops that overrun planned windows. Fitting and commissioning upgraded components demands engineering expertise beyond routine maintenance staff. Lost production during upgrades and integration labor are significant costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process and configuration records absent from each completed backup
rows.plain: Make sure every completed backup includes the important process information and setup details. This allows the work to be restored correctly if the original information is lost or damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Missing records from backups is a data-completeness error, peripheral to the sterilizing job, and forced low by the ranking; consequences are recovery-administrative.
rows.sat_rat: Where backups are manual or inconsistently scoped, required records are routinely missing, so restores frequently reveal incomplete captures. Assembling all required process and configuration records into each backup is a manual collation step that gets rushed or partly done. Knowing exactly which configuration files and process records must be captured requires system familiarity that not all staff have. Storage and backup tooling are cheap, so completeness gaps are not a cost problem.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of backed-up records lacking the machine, production batch, or creation context needed for traceability
rows.plain: Fewer saved records should be missing the information needed to tell which machine, production run, or situation they came from. This makes it possible to trace each record back to its source.
rows.imp_rat: Backup records lacking traceability context is administrative and low-stakes for sterilization; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Where paper logs or ad-hoc digital files dominate, a meaningful share of backups still lack full machine/batch/creation context, so traceability gaps recur routinely. Attaching machine ID, batch, and context to backup records adds logging steps that are often deferred and later reconstructed from memory. Capturing complete traceability metadata relies on operator diligence rather than deep skill, but consistent discipline is hard to sustain manually. Backup storage and record systems are cheap, so context loss is a process-discipline failure rather than a resource constraint.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that backup activity exceeds the available maintenance window
rows.plain: Make sure saving the information finishes within the time available for maintenance. If it takes too long, it could delay other work.
rows.imp_rat: Backup activity overrunning a maintenance window is a scheduling nuisance unrelated to core sterilizing performance (low relevance) with trivial impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Backup durations are generally predictable, though large historian datasets can occasionally overrun a tight window. Configuration and process-data backups are typically small and complete well within a maintenance window. Running a backup is a routine, low-skill operation once set up. Backup activity consumes negligible storage and staff cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required safeguards omitted from the periodic test scope
rows.plain: Make sure the regular safety check covers every required protective feature. This helps confirm that people will be protected from known equipment dangers.
rows.imp_rat: Safeguards omitted from periodic test scope touches safety verification but is capped by the batch ranking; both proxies held at 1.0 despite potential downstream risk.
rows.sat_rat: Standard periodic test protocols cover the primary safeguards well, yet less obvious interlocks are sometimes omitted from the test scope. Periodic safety-device testing is scheduled, but time pressure can lead to abbreviated test scopes. Defining and executing full safeguard test scope requires trained safety technicians who understand every interlock and relief device. Testing consumes technician labor and some downtime but no major materials, keeping cost moderate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safeguard test results recorded without device or acceptance-criterion traceability
rows.plain: Make sure every safety test result shows which device was tested and what standard it had to meet. This makes the results trustworthy and easier to check later.
rows.imp_rat: Safeguard test results lacking traceability is a records-quality error tangential to the running sterilization job, its consequences being audit rework. Ceiling keeps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Test forms prompt for traceability, but the device-ID and acceptance-criterion fields are still recurringly left blank under manual recording. Recording device and acceptance-criterion references at test time adds little time. Knowing which traceability data a safeguard test needs requires trained QA/validation staff. Missing traceability triggers costly retests, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sanitation records containing missing or conflicting entries
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning records for the inside chambers are complete and consistent. Reliable records help show that the chambers were cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or conflicting sanitation records is a documentation-ambiguity error; relevant to compliance but not the physical sterilizing job, and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual sanitation logs frequently show missing or conflicting entries because they are filled retrospectively or by multiple shifts. Completing sanitation records is quick when done at the time of work. Consistent, non-conflicting entries depend on operator discipline more than expertise. Incomplete records themselves cost little but drive costly audit findings and batch holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of mineral deposits remaining in the heating and cooling circuits after descaling
rows.plain: Make sure mineral buildup is removed from the heating and cooling pipes. Leftover buildup can reduce how well the system heats or cools.
rows.imp_rat: Residual mineral deposits after descaling affect circuit efficiency but the item is ranked below the prior batch, so the ceiling forces a low importance despite some relevance to heating/cooling reliability.
rows.sat_rat: Routine CIP descaling clears most scale, but verification of complete deposit removal is rare, so residual buildup recurs and gradually degrades heat transfer. Descaling cycles have defined dwell times, but shortening them to restart production leaves residual deposits. Effective descaling requires knowing correct chemistry, concentration, and circulation for the specific circuit, which competent maintenance teams generally handle. Descaling chemicals and water/energy for flushing add modest recurring cost that is usually accepted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected machine movement while lubricant is being applied
rows.plain: Prevent the machine from moving unexpectedly while someone applies lubricant to its moving parts. This helps prevent injuries and damage.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected movement during lubrication is a maintenance safety concern (relevance) with limited scope-level consequence (severity), held below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO is a mature, widely enforced practice that makes unexpected machine movement during lubrication rare when followed. Isolating drives before lubrication is a brief, standard step that adds little time. Lockout of the machine during lubrication is basic, well-taught practice requiring no special expertise. Energy-isolation hardware for lubrication tasks is cheap and already installed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of designated moving components left unlubricated at the service interval
rows.plain: Make sure every specified moving part receives lubricant at the scheduled service time, so it can move properly and avoid unnecessary wear.
rows.imp_rat: Unlubricated moving parts at service interval affect reliability, but ranking below batch 3 caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive-maintenance checklists reliably cover most designated components, though a few hard-to-reach or newly added points still get missed between intervals. Scheduled lubrication rounds are quick and built into PM intervals, so time rarely causes skipped points. Lubrication of designated components is a routine, checklisted task requiring modest training that most maintenance staff possess. Lubricants are inexpensive consumables, so cost does not drive missed service points.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of designated moving components receiving lubricant outside specified quantity limits
rows.plain: Make sure each specified moving part receives neither too little nor too much lubricant, so it is protected without creating other problems.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant applied outside specified quantity limits touches maintenance quality but is not central to sterilizing and cooling food, and the ranking caps its importance; consequences are minor and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Without metered dispensing, applied quantities commonly drift outside spec, causing over- or under-lubrication that standard practice only partly controls. Measuring exact lubricant quantity slows the round, so technicians tend to apply by feel to stay on schedule. Hitting specified quantity limits demands experienced judgement or metered tools that not every technician uses consistently. Over-lubrication wastes some grease and can contaminate product zones, but the material cost is minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterilization subsystem settings left outside manufacturer specifications after adjustment
rows.plain: Avoid leaving the germ-killing equipment set outside the maker’s allowed settings after it is adjusted. This helps ensure the equipment works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Although sterilization subsystem settings outside spec would normally be highly relevant, the batch ceiling forces relevance and severity down; scored at 1.0 as mandated by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Because sterilization efficacy is critical, post-adjustment verification against manufacturer spec is well-drilled, so out-of-spec settings are relatively uncommon. Verifying settings against spec after adjustment is a short check. Correctly setting sterilization parameters requires trained technicians who understand the process envelope. Out-of-spec settings cause spoiled batches, an expensive failure, motivating strong verification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of worn or damaged subsystem components missed during inspection
rows.plain: Miss fewer worn-out or damaged parts when checking the sterilizing equipment. This helps prevent faulty parts from causing problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Missing worn components during inspection affects reliability but the ranking places it below batch 3; both relevance and severity capped at 1 per the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual inspection routinely misses developing defects, so escaped wear items recur despite checklists. Thorough inspection takes meaningfully longer than a cursory look, and time pressure encourages shortcuts. Catching worn or damaged components before failure depends heavily on inspector experience and vigilance, which is scarce and inconsistent. Missed wear leads to expensive unplanned failures downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of manufacturer-required cooling-subsystem service tasks omitted during periodic service
rows.plain: Make sure all service tasks required by the manufacturer for the cooling system are completed during regular maintenance. This helps keep the cooling system working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting cooling-subsystem service tasks does relate to the cooling half of the job, but as a maintenance-completeness error it sits below the prior batch and is capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manufacturer PM checklists cover cooling service well, though tasks still get skipped under time pressure or with incomplete checklists. Following the service checklist for the cooling subsystem is time-bounded and predictable. Executing manufacturer cooling tasks requires trained service techs familiar with the subsystem. Omitted tasks cause costly cooling failures later, but the tasks themselves are low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the sterilizing machine's process instruments outside specified tolerance after calibration
rows.plain: Make sure the sterilizing machine's measuring devices are within the required accuracy range after they are adjusted and checked. This helps ensure the machine's process is measured correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Process instruments out of tolerance after calibration bears on sterilization accuracy, but the batch ceiling forces importance to 1.0 regardless of otherwise meaningful relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Mature calibration-and-verify practice keeps most instruments in tolerance, though a small fraction still drift out due to reference or procedural error. Verifying instruments fall within tolerance after calibration is quick once the calibration is done. Post-calibration verification follows a defined procedure that trained technicians perform reliably. Verification uses the same standards already on hand, adding negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of calibration records missing required instrument or reference-standard traceability data
rows.plain: Make sure each measurement-check record shows which equipment and trusted comparison tool were used. This lets others confirm where the results came from.
rows.imp_rat: Calibration records missing traceability data is a documentation-completeness error, tangential to actual sterilizing (relevance) with minor immediate impact (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulated food plants use calibration templates and QA review, but manual transcription still leaves recurring gaps in traceability fields, especially under audit scrutiny. Recording traceability data adds only minutes when done at point of calibration, so time is a minor factor. Correctly capturing instrument IDs and reference-standard chains requires trained personnel who understand metrology traceability, and gaps appear where that discipline is thin. The data itself is cheap to record but missing traceability triggers costly re-calibration or audit findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled sterilizing machine use disrupted by backup activity
rows.plain: Avoid interrupting planned use of the sterilizing machine while making backup copies of its settings. This helps the machine remain available for scheduled work.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption of scheduled use by backup activity is a scheduling nuisance far from the core function (relevance) with minor impact (severity); stays at the ceiling floor.
rows.sat_rat: Backups slotted into maintenance windows rarely disrupt scheduled runs, but ad-hoc backups occasionally intrude. Backup activity is typically scheduled into idle windows, limiting disruption to planned production. Running a backup is a low-skill, largely automated task once configured. The activity consumes little beyond brief machine time.

rows.source_job: Verify cleaning and sanitation validation records
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cleaning and sanitation validation records that lack required cleaning parameters, sanitation details, or verification results
rows.plain: Fewer cleaning and sanitation records should be missing required cleaning steps, sanitation information, or proof that the results were checked. This helps show that the equipment was cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete cleaning/sanitation validation records touch compliance but sit below batch 3, so relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established sanitation SOPs and audit pressure cover most events, yet missing parameters or verification results still appear in a real fraction of records. Filling out full cleaning parameters, sanitation details, and verification results during a shift is time-consuming and often abbreviated when the line must restart. HACCP-trained sanitation staff know what fields are required, but turnover and temporary crews erode consistent completion. Standardized cleaning validation forms and swab tests are low-cost, so incompleteness is not driven by expense.

rows.source_job: Verify cleaning and sanitation validation records
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of record entries or changes that cannot be attributed to authorized personnel
rows.plain: Leave fewer cleaning and sanitation records or changes without a clear link to the authorized person who made them. This helps show who performed or changed each step.
rows.imp_rat: Unattributable record entries are a data-integrity/audit error, tangential to the physical sterilizing job, and the ceiling caps importance; consequences are compliance-administrative.
rows.sat_rat: Many maintenance settings still rely on shared logins or unsigned paper, so a meaningful share of entries and changes cannot be traced to authorized personnel. Attributing every entry or change to a person is instant with electronic sign-off but slow and often skipped on paper. No special skill is needed to sign records, so unattributed entries reflect weak access-control practice rather than capability. Basic login/audit-trail systems are inexpensive, so attribution gaps are not cost-driven where digital systems exist.

rows.source_job: Test safety relief and interlock devices
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of pressure relief valves and safety interlock devices that fail to activate within specified parameters
rows.plain: Fewer pressure-release valves and safety devices should fail to work when they are supposed to. This helps protect people and equipment from unsafe pressure or operating conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Failure of pressure relief valves and safety interlocks is genuinely safety-critical and relevant, but the mandatory batch ceiling forces both proxies to 1 regardless of true stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Because these are safety-critical, regulated testing regimes are well established and rigorously enforced, so failures within tolerance are comparatively rare. Safety-device testing is a defined, relatively quick procedure. Verifying relief valves and interlocks activate within spec requires trained safety technicians and proper test rigs. Testing and any needed replacement is modest cost against the risk of catastrophic failure.

rows.source_job: Test safety relief and interlock devices
rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting to establish the required pressure, temperature, or interlock conditions
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to set the pressure, heat, and safety shutoff conditions needed for testing. This helps the safety test begin sooner and confirms that the equipment responds correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting to establish pressure/temperature/interlock conditions is closer to the sterilizing job, but the batch ceiling mandates keeping the mean at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated startup logic makes reaching stable conditions repeatable, so waiting time is fairly predictable even if not minimal. Establishing required pressure, temperature, or interlock conditions has a physically bounded ramp-up time that automated startup sequences already optimize. Automated interlock and setpoint sequencing means little operator skill is needed to reach conditions correctly. The energy consumed reaching conditions is a real but accepted operating cost with limited room to cut.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of product, cleaning agents, and heat-transfer media remaining in the machine after drainage
rows.plain: Remove as much leftover product, cleaning liquid, and heating or cooling fluid as possible from the machine after draining it.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product and heat-transfer media after drainage relates to cleanliness but is a drainage-completeness defect; below prior batch, so both proxies capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Gravity drainage leaves predictable residues in traps and low points, a well-known limitation of standard practice. Drainage cycles add time but are routine. Draining fluids follows standard procedure needing little specialist skill. Residual heat-transfer media and cleaning agents left behind are wasted resource and disposal expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine-handling delay caused by incomplete drainage
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for leftover liquid to drain from the machine. This helps workers handle the machine sooner and avoid slowing down production.
rows.imp_rat: Machine-handling delay from incomplete drainage is a logistics/handling error peripheral to the conserving job, causing minor schedule delay. Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Drainage completeness is inconsistent, so handling delays recur when residual fluid appears. Incomplete drainage forces handling delays while fluids are dealt with, the explicit delay here. Recognizing and clearing residual fluid before handling is routine but sometimes overlooked. Delay-driven labor and rescheduling add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of machine food-contact surfaces retaining food residue after sanitation
rows.plain: Make sure no food remains on the machine's food-touching surfaces after cleaning. This helps prevent contamination when the machine is removed or handled.
rows.imp_rat: Food residue retained after sanitation relates to contamination central to food safety, but the batch ceiling forces this to 1.0; both proxies are held at the cap by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/sanitation practice for food-contact surfaces is mature and verifiable by swab testing, so residual residue is uncommon. A final sanitation cycle is a well-defined step that fits the disposal timeline. Sanitation-in-place is a routine, well-trained procedure for food equipment operators. Cleaning agents and water are modest, well-understood costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that legal transfer is blocked by unresolved liens or competing ownership claims
rows.plain: Make sure no unpaid debts or competing claims prevent the legal transfer of ownership.
rows.imp_rat: Legal transfer blocked by liens is a purely administrative/ownership matter (relevance) with no bearing on machine function (severity).
rows.sat_rat: UCC filings and asset registers make ownership claims traceable, so blocked transfers are uncommon though not unheard of. Lien and title searches take days but are a well-understood step before transfer. Legal and asset-management staff routinely clear liens on capital equipment, so needed expertise is available. Title clearance and legal fees are predictable, bounded costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between the agreed closing date and receipt of buyer funds
rows.plain: Receive the buyer’s money as soon as the agreed closing date has passed. This completes the payment part of transferring ownership.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between closing date and receipt of buyer funds is a financial-transaction error unrelated to sterilizing and cooling food, with only cash-flow consequences. Bounded at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Escrow and wire transfer make payment receipt fairly predictable after closing. The gap between closing and fund receipt is the target delay, and escrow/wire practice keeps it fairly short. Standard asset-sale closing procedures need routine finance handling. Delayed funds tie up working capital but rarely incur large penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required maintenance, validation, and decontamination records missing or incomplete at buyer review
rows.plain: Make sure the buyer receives complete records of maintenance, required checks, and cleaning to remove harmful contamination. This helps the buyer understand the equipment’s condition and history before taking ownership.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete maintenance/validation records at buyer review is a documentation error peripheral to the sterilizing function, capped by the ceiling; consequences are administrative.
rows.sat_rat: Whether a complete decontamination and validation history survives to buyer review is highly variable, especially for older machines. Retrieving maintenance and validation records is slow when scattered across systems, but the core issue is completeness at review. Compiling a coherent record set needs someone who understands the full maintenance and validation history, often lost over the machine's life. Missing records lower resale value and can void warranties or coverage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recoverable metals, motors, and structural components discarded rather than routed for material recovery
rows.plain: Send reusable metals, motors, and framework parts for recycling instead of throwing them away. This saves useful materials and reduces waste.
rows.imp_rat: Discarding recoverable metals rather than recycling is an end-of-life sustainability error remote from sterilizing/cooling, capped by the batch ceiling; consequences are environmental-administrative.
rows.sat_rat: Scrap-metal recovery is a mature market so most bulk metal is captured, though motors and mixed assemblies are sometimes discarded. Routing metals to recovery does not materially change disposal duration. Sorting recoverable metals and motors needs basic materials knowledge widely held by scrap handlers. Failing to route recoverable metals and structural steel to recovery forfeits substantial scrap value, the direct loss this need targets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recoverable motors and structural components rendered unusable during removal and handling
rows.plain: Fewer reusable motors and supporting structural parts should be damaged while being removed or moved. Keeping them intact allows them to be reused or recycled properly.
rows.imp_rat: Rendering recoverable components unusable during removal is a decommissioning waste concern; the ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard practice ensuring gentle handling at disposal, so component survival varies widely with the crew. Careful removal to preserve components takes longer than scrap-oriented teardown, though time is secondary. Preserving motors and structural parts for reuse depends on skilled riggers and technicians who handle rather than cut, and such care is often absent in disposal crews. Ruined recoverable components are lost resale or reuse value, a direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time separated components wait for recycler acceptance because material identity or decontamination status is unclear
rows.plain: Reduce how long separated parts wait for a recycler to accept them when their material or cleaning status is uncertain. This helps the parts move to recycling instead of being held up.
rows.imp_rat: Wait time for recycler acceptance of separated components is an end-of-life logistics delay far from the CFJ (low relevance) with minor severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination certificates and material tagging exist but are inconsistently provided upfront, so acceptance delays recur. Components sit waiting for recycler acceptance while decontamination status or material identity is verified, the exact delay this need addresses. Clarifying decontamination status requires someone who can produce credible certification, a modest expertise. Waiting parts occupy storage and delay revenue from recovery.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the manufacturer disputes eligibility due to conflicting machine identification or service records
rows.plain: Make sure the machine's identifying details and repair history agree with the manufacturer's records. This helps prevent the manufacturer from challenging whether the machine qualifies for a warranty return.
rows.imp_rat: Manufacturer disputing warranty eligibility over conflicting identification/service records is a commercial dispute far removed from the CFJ; its consequence is contractual friction, scoring near the minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Machine identification and service records are frequently incomplete or inconsistent by end of life, so eligibility disputes are common. Reconciling conflicting IDs and service records delays manufacturer eligibility decisions. Tracing nameplate data against service history needs records-management expertise that is often thin at disposal. Disputes can forfeit recall/warranty reimbursement, a direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish activities disrupted during machine removal and dispatch
rows.plain: Spend less time interrupting germ-free medicine filling and packaging when a machine is removed and sent back under warranty. This helps production resume sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to fill-finish during machine removal and dispatch is an end-of-life logistics error peripheral to the core sterilize-and-cool job, with recoverable production impact. Held at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Removal windows overrun unpredictably, so disruption duration is inconsistent. Sterile fill-finish activity disrupted during removal and dispatch is the duration this need attacks, difficult to minimize with live lines. Minimizing disruption needs skilled coordination of rigging and aseptic-area controls. Disrupted sterile production time carries high opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of return freight and handling costs not reimbursed under the warranty or recall
rows.plain: Keep the unreimbursed cost of shipping and handling returned equipment as low as possible. This matters when a warranty or recall does not cover every return expense.
rows.imp_rat: Unreimbursed return freight and handling under warranty is a cost-recovery error unrelated to the sterilization function, with purely financial consequences. Bounded at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reimbursement eligibility is inconsistently documented, so a meaningful share of freight cost goes unrecovered. Claiming freight reimbursement adds administrative lead time. Navigating warranty/recall freight terms needs claims expertise that is often lacking. Unreimbursed return freight and handling is a direct out-of-pocket loss, the core concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required decommission records missing from the archive
rows.plain: Keep every required record from taking equipment out of service in the archive, so there is a complete history of what was done.
rows.imp_rat: Missing decommission records from the archive is an end-of-life documentation error far from the CFJ, capped by the batch ceiling; consequences are administrative.
rows.sat_rat: Document management systems exist but disposal records are inconsistently archived, so gaps in the archive persist. Archiving records is a quick administrative act. Filing to the archive requires only knowing the retention system, not scarce expertise. Missing archived records mainly create audit and compliance exposure rather than direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived records associated with the wrong food sterilizing machine or decommissioning project
rows.plain: Keep stored records from being linked to the wrong food-sterilizing machine or retirement project. This makes it clear which equipment or project each record belongs to.
rows.imp_rat: Misfiled decommissioning records are a traceability nuisance far from the CFJ; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Without enforced unique identifiers, records occasionally get tied to the wrong machine or project. Correctly associating records adds negligible time when done at filing. Linking records to the right machine/project is clerical but error-prone without good identifiers. Misfiled records cost little until they must be retrieved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived decommission records assigned incorrect retention or access requirements
rows.plain: Give each stored record about retired equipment the correct rules for how long it must be kept and who may see it. This helps protect the information and meet recordkeeping obligations.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect retention or access on archived decommission records is administrative and far from the core sterilization job; both proxies pinned to 1 by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Manual retention/access tagging leads to periodic misassignment when standards are unclear. Assigning retention/access classifications adds little time. Correct classification requires records-management judgement about retention rules. Misclassification costs little directly unless it later triggers compliance issues.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between decommission approval and completion of the required archive
