rows.plain: Finish storing the required records as soon as the decision is made to retire something. This ensures important information is kept safely and can be found later.
rows.imp_rat: Time to complete the decommission archive is an end-of-life records delay, irrelevant to sterilizing food; held at 1.0 by the batch rule.
rows.sat_rat: Archives eventually get completed, but the interval varies widely with staff availability. Archiving after decommission approval is the explicit delay this need targets, and it depends on manual document assembly. Compiling the archive requires records staff familiar with what must be retained. Delayed archiving carries little direct cost beyond staff time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exclusively assigned services omitted from cancellation when the food sterilizing machine is retired
rows.plain: Make sure every service used only by the retired food-sterilizing machine is canceled. Leaving one active could cause unnecessary costs or confusion.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting service cancellations at retirement is a purely administrative loose end; both proxies capped at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without a complete service registry tied to the asset, omissions are common and only surface when invoices keep arriving. Cancelling assigned services is quick once identified, so duration is not the pain. It takes institutional knowledge to know which utility, maintenance, and monitoring contracts were exclusive to this machine, and that knowledge often lives with departing staff. Missed cancellations mean ongoing charges for services no longer needed, quietly wasting money for months.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time submitted cancellations remain pending after the food sterilizing machine is retired
rows.plain: Complete cancellation requests as quickly as possible after the food-sterilizing machine is taken out of service. This helps stop services that are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Pending cancellations after retirement is an administrative-closeout delay, wholly peripheral to the machine's job; held to 1.0 by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cancellation processing depends on manual follow-up, so pending items persist inconsistently. Pending cancellations lingering after retirement is precisely the delay in view, often left in queues for weeks. Processing cancellations is clerical and needs little specialized skill. Lingering cancellations may incur minor continued service or license charges.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of connected utility or process systems damaged while detaching the machine
rows.plain: Avoid damaging other connected utility or production systems while taking the machine out. This helps prevent extra repairs and disruption.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging connected utilities during detachment is a decommissioning risk unrelated to the core sterilization job; both proxies pinned to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout/isolation procedures and P&ID references exist but shared connections on older installations are often poorly documented, so accidental damage still recurs. Detachment is unhurried during planned decommissioning, so time pressure rarely drives the damage. Correctly isolating and detaching shared utility and process connections demands experienced technicians who can trace interdependent lines, and that expertise is inconsistently available. Damaging a connected utility or process system triggers costly unplanned repairs and downtime for adjacent equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of adjacent sterile fill-finish production disruption caused by decommissioning
rows.plain: Limit interruptions to nearby sterile production while the sterilizing machine is being taken out of service. This helps surrounding production resume quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Adjacent fill-finish disruption from decommissioning concerns end-of-life removal rather than the core sterilization job, with recoverable production-schedule impact. Capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Disruption duration varies with how well removal is sequenced against production, and overruns are common. Decommissioning next to live sterile fill-finish lines causes production downtime that is hard to compress, the core loss. Scheduling around aseptic production needs coordination skill to limit disruption. Lost sterile production output during disruption is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required decommissioning and disposal records left incomplete at project closure
rows.plain: Complete all required records about taking the sterilizing machine out of use and getting rid of it when the project ends. This provides proof that the machine was closed down and disposed of properly.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete decommissioning/disposal records at project closure are end-of-life paperwork errors, remote from the CFJ, and capped by the batch ranking; consequences are administrative.
rows.sat_rat: Closure checklists exist in mature shops but disposal is often deprioritized, so records are routinely left incomplete. Completing closure records is not primarily a speed problem. Knowing which decommissioning records are required draws on procedural knowledge that varies by organization maturity. Incomplete records risk audit findings and rework costs rather than immediate outlay.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required regulatory, quality, insurance, and asset stakeholders omitted from the retirement notification
rows.plain: Make sure everyone who needs to know about taking the machine out of use is included in the notice. This may include people responsible for legal rules, quality, insurance, and equipment records.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholders from a retirement notification is a decommissioning-communication error far from the CFJ, held low by the ranking; consequences are administrative and fixable.
rows.sat_rat: Distribution lists help but are seldom maintained for the rare retirement event, so omissions recur. Sending notifications is quick once the stakeholder list is known. Identifying every regulatory, quality, insurance, and asset stakeholder requires cross-functional knowledge that few individuals hold completely. Omitting a stakeholder risks fines or lapsed coverage rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the retirement notification is delivered after the applicable regulatory or organizational deadline
rows.plain: People who need to know about a machine being retired should be told as soon as possible after the required deadline. This reduces the delay in sharing important information.
rows.imp_rat: Late delivery of the retirement notification past a deadline is an administrative/compliance timing issue unrelated to sterilizing food (low relevance); ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deadline-tracking for the infrequent retirement event is weak, so timeliness is inconsistent without a dedicated reminder process. Notifications delivered after the regulatory or organizational deadline are the direct lateness this need targets, and disposal notifications are often an afterthought. Meeting the deadline is about awareness of the requirement more than skill. Late notification can trigger penalties, a direct financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inaccurate machine identity, retirement date, or disposition details included in the retirement notification
rows.plain: Make sure the retirement notice gives the correct machine identity, retirement date, and details about what will happen to the machine.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate retirement-notification details are administrative and remote from the sterilization CFJ; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Asset registers and templated notifications catch most fields, but stale or mismatched records still let wrong IDs, dates, or dispositions through. Producing the notification detail is fast once the data is gathered. Transcribing asset identity and disposition correctly needs someone familiar with the asset register conventions but is not highly specialized. Inaccurate details cause rework and occasional compliance penalties rather than large direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the retirement notification is released before required decommissioning approvals are complete
rows.plain: People should not be told that the machine is being retired until all required approvals are finished. This prevents an announcement from being made too early.
rows.imp_rat: Premature retirement notification is a decommissioning-paperwork sequencing error, peripheral to sterilization performance; its consequences are administrative and reversible. Held at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows are often informal, so retirement notifications sometimes release before decommissioning approvals are truly complete. Waiting on all approvals before notification adds sequencing delay. Coordinating who must approve decommissioning requires process knowledge that varies by site. Premature notification triggers rework and administrative cost to retract.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required stakeholders for whom receipt of the retirement notification remains unconfirmed
rows.plain: Make sure everyone who needs to know about the machine being retired has clearly received the notice. This matters so no one is surprised or misses an action they need to take.
rows.imp_rat: Confirming retirement-notification receipt among stakeholders is purely administrative and unrelated to sterilizing/cooling food (low relevance); consequences are minor and the ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Email read-receipts and acknowledgement requests exist but are unevenly enforced, so confirmation frequently remains open. Chasing receipt confirmations adds some delay but is secondary. Confirming receipt is a clerical follow-up requiring no special skill. Unconfirmed receipt costs little directly unless a compliance dispute arises.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury or facility damage during unloading and rigging
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that people get hurt or the building and equipment are damaged while the machine is being unloaded and moved into place. This helps make delivery safe.
rows.imp_rat: Injury during unloading and rigging is an install-phase safety error, not central to the CFJ, and held low by the ranking despite otherwise high real-world stakes.
rows.sat_rat: OSHA rules, lift plans and professional riggers make safe unloading standard practice, yet incidents still occur when procedures are shortcut. Rigging plans and lift schedules are normally prepared in advance, adding modest lead time to the receiving process. Unloading and rigging heavy machinery demands certified riggers and lift planning, expertise that professional rigging firms supply but that in-house teams often lack. Hiring qualified riggers and insurance carries real cost, occasionally leading sites to improvise with cheaper untrained labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the food sterilizing machine being damaged during transportation, unloading, or rigging
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the food-sterilizing machine while it is being transported, unloaded, or lifted into place. Careful moving protects the machine before it is installed.
rows.imp_rat: Transport/rigging damage is peripheral to the CFJ of sterilizing and cooling food (low relevance), and while damage could be costly (moderate severity), the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Transit insurance, professional packing and shock indicators make transport damage a well-mitigated, though not eliminated, risk. Protective packaging and inspection on receipt are routine steps that add little time. Proper crating, securing, and handling of machinery is well-understood by established freight and rigging providers. Adequate packaging and transit insurance are standard budgeted costs, keeping exposure manageable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved discrepancies in machine dimensions, weight, and lifting-point data before dispatch
rows.plain: Make sure the machine’s size, weight, and lifting information agree before it is sent. This helps the delivery team move it safely and use the right equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies in dimensions/weight/lifting data before dispatch are a logistics error, peripheral to the sterilizing function with modest severity, and are held at 1.0 by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: General-arrangement drawings and shipping specs are mature deliverables, so discrepancies before dispatch are uncommon though not eliminated. Dimensional and lifting data are provided on standard datasheets early, rarely delaying dispatch. Verifying dimensions and lifting points needs basic mechanical literacy that is widely available. Confirming physical specs is cheap relative to the cost of a mis-sized delivery.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering a route or receiving-site obstruction after shipment dispatch
rows.plain: Find any problem with the travel route or the place receiving the machine before the shipment leaves. This helps prevent delays when the machine arrives.
rows.imp_rat: Discovering a route or receiving-site obstruction after dispatch is a logistics error unrelated to the sterilizing function, and the ceiling caps it; consequences are delay-based and recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Obstructions discovered only after dispatch remain a common and costly surprise because upfront route verification is inconsistently performed. Route and site surveys can be scheduled ahead of dispatch, but they are often rushed against shipping deadlines and skipped when time is short. Effective route planning for oversized machinery requires specialized logistics and rigging expertise that many acquiring plants do not have in-house. Pre-shipment route surveys are inexpensive relative to a stranded shipment, yet buyers frequently forgo them to save money and then eat the far larger cost of a failed delivery.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required transportation, rigging, lifting, or receiving resources are unavailable during the scheduled delivery window
rows.plain: Make sure the needed transport, moving equipment, lifting help, and receiving staff are ready during the planned delivery period. This helps the machine be unloaded and placed safely.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable rigging/receiving resources during delivery is a logistics delay upstream of operation, low relevance to the sterilizing job; held at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Even with contracts and checklists, a missing crane, forklift, or receiving crew on the day still happens intermittently, so the outcome is dependably-but-not-perfectly served. Delivery windows are routinely coordinated in advance, so timing failures are the exception rather than the rule when logistics are planned early. Rigging and heavy-machine receiving is handled by experienced logistics and plant teams whose scheduling expertise is widely available. Standby cranes and rigging crews carry real idle-cost risk, but this is a known budgeted line item rather than a surprise.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that the food sterilizing machine shipment status remains unreported after a transit milestone
rows.plain: Keep the time without a reported update on the food-sterilizing machine’s shipment as short as possible after it reaches a delivery checkpoint. This helps the team quickly notice delays or problems.
rows.imp_rat: Unreported shipment status after a milestone is a logistics visibility gap far from sterilization; both proxies pinned to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Shipment tracking for oversized machinery is patchy and status often goes stale between milestones. Gaps between transit milestones and status reporting are the core error and remain common for capital freight. Little skill is needed to read tracking, but chasing carriers requires persistent expediting effort. Unreported status forces buffer planning and idle receiving resources, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a customs or port hold affecting the food sterilizing machine shipment remains undisclosed until delivery is delayed
rows.plain: Make sure delays caused by customs or a port are reported before they postpone delivery of the food-cleaning machine. This helps the recipient know about shipment problems early.
rows.imp_rat: Undisclosed customs/port holds affect procurement logistics, not the core sterilizing/cooling function (low relevance); delivery delay is recoverable (low severity), and the ceiling enforces 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Brokers and tracking exist, but timely visibility into holds is inconsistent, so undisclosed customs delays remain a recurring surprise. Customs status can be tracked, but disclosure of holds often lags until the delay already impacts the schedule. Navigating customs and port procedures requires customs-broker expertise that acquirers typically outsource. Customs brokers are affordable, yet proactive monitoring services are underused, so the cost of surprise delays is borne instead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency between shipment statuses reported by the carrier and freight forwarder
rows.plain: Make sure the carrier and shipping coordinator give the same information about where the equipment is and when it is expected to arrive.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent shipment statuses between carrier and forwarder is a logistics-tracking error far from the CFJ; low severity and held to 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Carrier and forwarder systems are not fully integrated for capital equipment, so status discrepancies still surface and must be manually reconciled. Carrier and forwarder tracking portals update within hours, so the delay in detecting a status mismatch is modest. Reconciling two shipment sources takes a logistics coordinator's routine attention rather than scarce expertise. The cost of an occasional mismatch is limited to expediting or clarification calls, not major spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the reported estimated arrival and the actual arrival of the food sterilizing machine
rows.plain: Make the reported delivery date for the food-sterilizing machine match the date it actually arrives. This helps the receiving team prepare for it.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between estimated and actual delivery arrival is a logistics-reliability error with no operational effect on food conservation; ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor ETAs for made-to-order sterilizers drift often, and tracking milestones is inconsistent, leaving this only moderately served. Estimated-vs-actual arrival deviation is the core error and shipment ETAs for capital equipment remain frequently unreliable. Expediters can improve forecasts, but predicting arrival of custom machinery needs vendor cooperation more than internal skill. Arrival slippage triggers installation-crew and downtime costs that are only partly buffered.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between receipt of the food sterilizing machine at the site and confirmation of final delivery
rows.plain: Reduce the time between the food-sterilizing machine arriving at the site and confirming that delivery is complete. This helps everyone know quickly that the machine has been received.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between receipt and final delivery confirmation is a logistics timing issue peripheral to sterilizing; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Goods-receipt and inspection workflows reliably confirm delivery, though sign-off sometimes lags for complex machinery. The duration from site receipt to final delivery confirmation is short where standard goods-receipt processes exist. Receiving and sign-off is a routine warehouse task needing little specialized skill. Delays in confirmation tie up little cost but can hold retention payments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required documentation items absent from the acquisition package
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase package contains every required document about the machine. This lets the buyer check what was received and operate or maintain the machine safely.
rows.imp_rat: Missing acquisition-package documentation is a procurement completeness defect with low relevance to the CFJ and low severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard document checklists exist, yet items still go missing without automated completeness checks. Assembling the acquisition package is time-bounded but often last-minute. Package assembly is largely clerical with established templates. Missing items rarely cost money beyond rework effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplied regulatory certificates that cannot be validated as current and authentic
rows.plain: Make sure the supplied official certificates are still valid and genuinely issued. This helps confirm that the machine’s documents can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Certificates that cannot be validated as current/authentic is a documentation-authenticity issue peripheral to the core job (low relevance) and correctable pre-purchase (low severity). Held at 1.0 per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Most certificates can be checked against registries, so unvalidated ones are relatively uncommon though not zero. Validating certificate currency and authenticity can be done quickly against issuing bodies. Confirming certificates needs modest QA effort widely available. Certificate validation is a low-cost administrative step.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of documentation items whose equipment identifiers or configuration scope conflict with the acquired machine
rows.plain: Make sure the documents received with the machine identify the correct equipment and describe the same setup that was bought. This prevents people from using instructions for the wrong machine.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting equipment identifiers in documentation are clerical defects with low relevance to the sterilize-and-cool function and minor consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation packages are assembled manually with no enforced identifier control, so configuration-scope conflicts recur frequently. Cross-checking identifiers across documents is time-consuming but rarely the main pain felt. Matching configuration scope to the delivered machine needs technical familiarity that admin staff often lack. Identifier mismatches rarely cost money directly unless they cascade into wrong-part orders.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between the documented machine specifications and purchase requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the machine information in the documents matches what was requested for purchase. This helps confirm that the right machine was bought and delivered.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between documented specs and purchase requirements is a procurement/administrative error, peripheral to actually sterilizing and cooling food; consequences are contractual rework, not process failure. Ceiling holds it at or below 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement-traceability practices catch major mismatches, but detailed spec deviations still slip through with some regularity. Spec-to-requirement checking is a defined review step that fits within normal acquisition timelines. Engineers cross-checking machine specs against requirements need domain knowledge that is generally available in-house. Undetected spec gaps cause costly retrofits, only partly prevented by current reviews.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between receipt of the machine and availability of its complete documentation package
rows.plain: There should be less time between receiving a machine and having all of its documents available. This helps people check, install, operate, and maintain the machine properly.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in documentation availability after receipt is a logistics/timing concern peripheral to the sterilize-and-cool function (low relevance) with easily absorbed consequences (low severity). Ceiling caps at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Late or incomplete doc delivery is a recurring, poorly controlled failure across suppliers. Complete documentation packages frequently lag machine delivery by weeks or months, a chronic delay. Chasing and compiling documentation needs coordination effort but little specialized skill. Documentation delays stall commissioning, incurring idle-asset cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that warranty registration is submitted after the supplier deadline
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the warranty is registered too late for the supplier to accept it. This helps preserve the customer's warranty protection.
rows.imp_rat: Late warranty registration is a trivial administrative deadline miss with negligible impact on sterilizing food; comfortably held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: With reminders and standard procurement checklists this is reliably completed, though the occasional slipped deadline still occurs. Warranty registration is a short, deadline-bound clerical step easily handled within the window. Submitting a warranty form requires minimal skill and clear supplier instructions. The task is essentially free, and missing it forfeits coverage worth far more than the effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required registration fields that cannot be completed from available machine and purchase records
rows.plain: Make sure the warranty registration can be completed using the machine and purchase records available, so the warranty is properly recorded.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete registration fields are a peripheral administrative error with little bearing on the machine's core job of sterilizing and cooling food; consequences are trivial and easily corrected, and the batch ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Fields regularly cannot be completed because source machine and purchase records are scattered or missing. Completing registration fields is fast when source records exist. The task is mostly transcription requiring little expertise. Unfillable fields cost little beyond follow-up effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incorrect machine, purchaser, installation-site, or commissioning details in the registration
rows.plain: Make sure the warranty registration has the correct machine, buyer, installation location, and start-up details.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect registration details are administrative record errors with low relevance to actual sterilizing and low severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual entry of machine, purchaser, and commissioning details is error-prone without validation, producing recurring inaccuracies. Filling registration details is quick data entry when records are at hand. The task needs little expertise beyond careful transcription. Errors seldom carry direct cost unless they void warranty registration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between registration submission and supplier confirmation that warranty coverage is active
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting after sending in a registration before the supplier confirms that the warranty is active. This lets the owner know sooner that repairs may be covered.
rows.imp_rat: Time to confirm active warranty coverage is an administrative registration delay unrelated to core sterilization (low relevance) and trivially absorbed (low severity). Held at 1.0 by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmations do sometimes get lost or delayed in email queues, but the coverage itself is generally granted consistently once the machine is invoiced and registered. Warranty registration for capital food equipment is increasingly handled through online supplier portals that issue near-automatic confirmation, though many industrial suppliers still process registrations manually with multi-day turnaround. Submitting a registration and reading back a confirmation requires little expertise, so skill is rarely the bottleneck here. Registration itself carries no meaningful cost beyond a few minutes of administrative time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between activated warranty terms and the terms in the equipment purchase agreement
rows.plain: Make sure the warranty that was recorded matches the warranty promises in the equipment purchase agreement. This helps protect the buyer's coverage.
rows.imp_rat: Warranty-term discrepancies versus the purchase agreement is a contractual ambiguity with no functional bearing on sterilization; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contracts specify warranty terms but activation paperwork discrepancies still surface with moderate frequency. Warranty activation is usually done at handover with modest delay. Comparing activated warranty against contract terms requires careful contract-reading skill that is not always applied. Warranty mismatches can create uncovered repair costs later that are hard to recover.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that target product and production-line requirements are incomplete or outdated when machines are assessed
rows.plain: Make sure the product and production-line needs are complete and current before judging whether the machines are suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete or outdated product/line requirements during assessment is a specification ambiguity, only indirectly relevant to actually sterilizing food; ceiling fixes it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement documents are frequently incomplete or stale when machines are evaluated, making mismatch a routine occurrence. Compiling current product and line requirements is often rushed, delaying meaningful machine assessment. It takes production and process expertise to translate target products into complete line requirements, and that knowledge is often siloed. Outdated requirements drive selection errors whose rework cost is high but deferred.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in candidate operating data under intended product and container conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the information about how a possible process operates is reliable when tested with the intended product and containers.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in candidate operating data affects selection confidence, but ranking below batch 3 forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors rarely provide data under the buyer's precise conditions, so this uncertainty is largely unmitigated today. Obtaining operating data under the exact intended product and container conditions takes long lead times or trials. Interpreting whether vendor data applies to specific product/container conditions demands scarce process expertise. Uncertain operating data risks buying a machine that underperforms on the actual product, a costly error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product or container damage at the required sterilization conditions is overlooked during evaluation
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that damage to the product or containers is missed when they are exposed to the required sterilizing treatment. This helps show whether the process can be used without causing damage.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking product or container damage at sterilization conditions touches the core job more directly, but as an evaluation-phase omission it still sits below the prior batch and is held to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without routine damage-trial protocols, container degradation at sterilization conditions is frequently overlooked during evaluation. Evaluating product or container damage under sterilization conditions may require trials that extend evaluation. Recognizing damage risk at sterilization conditions requires materials and process expertise that is specialized. Trial testing carries cost that buyers sometimes defer, accepting the risk instead.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between candidate throughput and target production-line demand
rows.plain: Make sure a possible machine or process can produce enough to meet the production line’s needs, without producing too little or too much.
rows.imp_rat: Throughput-to-demand mismatch is a selection concern, not central to sterilization execution; both proxies held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Throughput sizing is a well-practiced calculation, so gross mismatches are uncommon though edge-case demand still surprises. Matching candidate throughput to line demand is done during technical review within normal timelines. Process engineers routinely size throughput against line demand using established methods. Throughput mismatch causes over- or under-capacity spend, partly guarded against.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of utility, line-interface, or container-handling incompatibilities discovered after candidate selection
rows.plain: Find and resolve any problems that would prevent the selected equipment from working with the site’s services, production lines, or containers. Doing this before the choice is final helps avoid costly surprises.
rows.imp_rat: Utility/interface/container incompatibilities found after selection cause rework but are peripheral to the core sterilization job and recoverable, capped at 1.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without a rigorous pre-selection compatibility checklist, hidden interface mismatches surface after candidate selection fairly often. Utility and container-handling checks are often done late, after selection, forcing rework delays. Matching line interfaces and utilities to the machine demands integration expertise that is unevenly distributed among buyers. Late-discovered incompatibilities trigger costly site modifications or adapters.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required machine configuration details omitted from the RFQ
rows.plain: Include every needed machine detail in the request sent to suppliers, so they can prepare accurate price and equipment offers.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted configuration details in the RFQ is a procurement-documentation defect, low relevance to whether food gets sterilized; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: RFQs commonly omit configuration specifics, so incomplete requests to suppliers remain a frequent issue. Drafting the RFQ configuration section adds effort but fits the procurement schedule. Specifying complete configuration details requires engineering knowledge of the sterilization process that varies among buyers. Omitted configuration details cause costly change orders later, a real recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the commercial terms specified in the RFQ
rows.plain: State the business and payment terms in the request for a supplier’s price clearly and consistently. This helps suppliers prepare comparable offers and reduces misunderstandings.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous RFQ commercial terms is a purchasing-documentation error with no functional link to sterilizing food; ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard RFQ templates and legal review cover common terms, but specialized machine terms still leave recurring ambiguity. RFQ drafting with templates is reasonably fast but clarification cycles add delay. Skilled buyers using standard commercial templates reduce ambiguity, but drafting quality varies with experience. Ambiguous terms cause rework and disputes that carry cost, only partly avoided today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts across the configuration, option, and commercial requirements in the RFQ documents
rows.plain: Make sure the request for prices does not contain different instructions about the equipment choices, options, or business terms. This helps suppliers price the same request.
rows.imp_rat: RFQ document conflicts are procurement-stage defects with low centrality to sterilizing food and low severity; capped at 1.0 per batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: RFQ templates and review steps catch many conflicts, but multi-author documents still ship with residual configuration and commercial inconsistencies. Cross-checking RFQ documents for internal conflicts adds a review pass before issuance. Spotting configuration and commercial conflicts requires an experienced procurement author. RFQ conflicts drive requoting and rework, adding modest cost and delay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required internal authorization is unavailable by the planned RFQ issue date
rows.plain: Make sure the necessary internal permission is ready before the request for supplier prices is sent. This prevents the request from being delayed or held up.
rows.imp_rat: Missing internal authorization by the RFQ date is a procurement-timing delay, largely administrative and peripheral to the sterilizing CFJ; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard approval workflows make timely authorization common, though occasional bottlenecks still delay RFQ issuance. Internal authorization ahead of RFQ is a scheduled workflow step that established approval calendars usually deliver on time. Securing authorization is procedural and requires little specialized skill beyond knowing the approval chain. Obtaining authorization consumes negligible direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of shortlisted suppliers that do not receive the complete RFQ by the issue deadline
rows.plain: Make sure every chosen supplier receives the full request for pricing by the deadline. This gives each supplier a fair chance to respond.
rows.imp_rat: RFQ distribution to shortlisted suppliers is a procurement-administrative error with minimal relevance to the machine's actual sterilization/cooling job; consequences are recoverable and the ceiling caps this batch at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: With e-procurement tools and mailing lists, complete on-time RFQ distribution is largely reliable, with only occasional omissions. Issuing an RFQ to a shortlist by a deadline is a straightforward, well-supported distribution task. Sending complete RFQs to suppliers is administrative work needing little specialized skill. The distribution itself costs little; failure merely narrows the competitive field.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required CapEx documents missing or incomplete at submission
rows.plain: Submit all required documents for approval to buy a major item or make a major investment, with no missing information. This helps the request be reviewed and approved without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Missing CapEx documents at submission are administrative approval defects peripheral to sterilizing food with correctable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checklist-driven CapEx submission is fairly mature, though items still occasionally arrive incomplete. Gathering CapEx documents at submission is a known, scheduled task. Compiling capital documents is a familiar finance function needing modest skill. Incomplete CapEx packages delay funding rather than waste money directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the food sterilizing machine business case fails internal investment criteria due to unsupported assumptions
rows.plain: Make sure the proposal to buy the food-sterilizing machine is based on reliable information and assumptions that can be supported. This improves the chance that it meets the organization’s investment rules.
rows.imp_rat: A business case failing investment criteria on unsupported assumptions is a financial-justification defect, remote from the machine's functional sterilizing job; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unsupported assumptions causing failed approval are a common, recurring stumbling block despite established templates. Building a business case fits within normal capital-approval cycles without major delay. Sound investment cases hinge on financial-modeling and process-assumption expertise that varies widely between buyers. A rejected case wastes preparation effort but avoids far larger misspend, so net cost impact is contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the total investment estimate understates site preparation, integration, qualification, or operating costs
rows.plain: Make sure the investment estimate includes preparing the site, connecting the equipment, proving it works as required, and running it. This helps prevent the project from costing more than approved.
rows.imp_rat: Understated investment estimate for site prep and qualification is a cost-estimation error peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: The frequent understatement of ancillary costs makes the investment estimate an unreliable basis for the CapEx decision. Building a full cost estimate including site prep and qualification takes weeks of cross-functional gathering. Estimating integration, utility and qualification costs demands experienced project engineers who are often unavailable early. Total-cost-of-ownership models exist but hidden site-prep, integration and qualification costs are routinely understated, causing budget overruns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among quality, production, engineering, and finance requirements affecting the proposed food sterilizing machine purchase
rows.plain: Make sure the quality, production, engineering, and finance needs do not contradict one another before buying the proposed food-sterilizing machine. This supports a purchase that meets operational and financial expectations.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among departmental requirements is a coordination error peripheral to the machine's sterilizing function; capped at 1.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Competing departmental requirements are frequently left in tension at purchase, making conflict a common, poorly managed condition. Resolving cross-functional requirement conflicts drags out approvals when it happens. Reconciling quality, production, engineering, and finance demands strong cross-functional facilitation skill that is unevenly present. Unresolved conflicts lead to costly rework or a machine that satisfies no one fully.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the CapEx request waits for required reviews and decisions
rows.plain: Get the request for spending on major equipment reviewed and decided sooner. This helps the needed investment move forward without unnecessary waiting.
rows.imp_rat: CapEx request waiting for reviews is a procurement-approval delay with low relevance to actually sterilizing food and only mild consequences; batch ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Structured CapEx approval processes exist and are predictable, though queueing for committee reviews reliably adds weeks. CapEx requests routinely wait through gated review cycles and committee calendars. Preparing the CapEx package needs finance and engineering skill but is a well-defined task. Approval delay defers the investment's return but has little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inadequate floor or structural capacity remains unidentified before purchase commitment
rows.plain: Make sure the floor and building can safely support the equipment before agreeing to buy it.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooked floor or structural capacity is a facility-readiness defect that could damage installation, yet it is upstream of the machine's actual sterilizing function; the batch ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Load calculations against machine weight are standard when done, but inadequate capacity still occasionally goes unidentified before commitment. Structural capacity checks require engineering assessment that can lengthen the pre-purchase timeline. Evaluating floor load-bearing capacity needs structural-engineering expertise typically brought in externally. A structural assessment costs modestly and is sometimes skipped to avoid the expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that steam, water, electrical, or drainage limitations remain unidentified before purchase commitment
rows.plain: Find any limits in the site’s steam, water, electricity, or drainage before committing to buy the system. This prevents buying equipment the site cannot support.
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified steam/water/electrical/drainage limits is a utility-readiness reliability gap that matters for operation, but as a pre-purchase check it sits below the prior batch and is held to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utility constraints are often only partially documented before signing, so unpleasant surprises after commitment remain common. Site utility surveys take time to arrange but rarely dominate the acquisition timeline when done early. Assessing steam, water, electrical, and drainage adequacy needs facilities-engineering expertise that is available but not always consulted before commitment. Missed utility limits translate into costly retrofits discovered after purchase, a frequent and expensive failure mode.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that access-route obstructions or dimensional constraints remain unidentified before purchase commitment
rows.plain: Find out before buying whether anything blocks the equipment’s route into the site or whether the spaces are too small. This helps prevent buying equipment that cannot be moved into place.
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified access-route obstructions before purchase is a pre-installation planning issue, tangential to the sterilizing job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences; ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Route and dimensional constraints are often discovered only at delivery because upfront verification against machine dimensions is inconsistently performed. Access-route and dimensional checks can be done pre-purchase but are frequently deferred until too late. Measuring clearances and mapping delivery paths for large equipment requires site-survey competence not consistently applied. A pre-purchase site survey is low-cost, yet skipping it to save time or money leads to expensive rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that footprint or service-clearance conflicts remain unidentified before purchase commitment
rows.plain: Find any space or access problems before agreeing to buy, so the equipment will actually fit and can be reached for use and maintenance.
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified footprint/service-clearance conflicts before purchase can force costly rework, but relative to the CFJ of sterilizing and cooling food this is a peripheral procurement-planning concern; ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Layout drawings help, but service-clearance conflicts are still discovered post-commitment when data is not carefully cross-checked. Footprint and clearance layout checks add planning time that is often condensed near purchase. Reconciling machine service clearances with the plant layout requires layout-engineering competence not always applied. CAD layout verification is low-cost and increasingly standard, limiting the money barrier.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incomplete or conflicting facility and machine data produces an incorrect readiness conclusion
rows.plain: Use complete and accurate information about the building and machine, so the decision about whether the site is ready is correct.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting facility/machine data producing a wrong readiness conclusion is an administrative ambiguity error, only indirectly tied to the sterilizing job; capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete or conflicting data producing wrong readiness conclusions is a common failure because there is no standard integrated verification tool. Reconciling facility and machine data takes coordination time across parties that is often incomplete. Spotting conflicts in disparate facility and machine data requires integrative judgement that is hard to systematize. Data reconciliation is mostly labor and cheap, so cost is not the main obstacle.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shortlisted suppliers that lack the required technical capability for cup and lid sterilization in sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: Choose suppliers that can properly sterilize the cups and lids used in sterile production and packaging. This helps prevent contamination of the product.
rows.imp_rat: Shortlisted suppliers lacking cup/lid sterilization capability is a selection-stage screening error, not the machine performing its job (low relevance), and is caught before commitment (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Shortlists sometimes include suppliers lacking true capability because capability claims are hard to test pre-award. Assessing supplier technical capability takes time but is part of standard qualification. Judging cup-and-lid sterilization capability for sterile fill-finish demands rare specialist evaluators. Capability assessment adds moderate cost via audits and reference checks.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shortlisted suppliers with unresolved regulatory-standing deficiencies for the intended sterile fill-finish context
rows.plain: Choose suppliers without unresolved problems that could show they do not meet required rules for sterile production and packaging. This helps avoid working with a supplier whose official standing is in question.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved regulatory-standing deficiencies among shortlisted suppliers is a vendor-vetting error tangential to the sterilize-and-cool function (low relevance) and resolvable during evaluation (low severity). Ceiling limits to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory deficiencies among shortlisted suppliers are usually flagged, though some standing issues surface later. Checking regulatory standing is a defined step with acceptable turnaround. Regulatory-affairs staff can assess supplier standing for fill-finish contexts. Regulatory due diligence is a modest, standard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in applying minimum qualification criteria across candidate suppliers
rows.plain: Apply the same basic qualification rules to every possible supplier. This makes the shortlist fair and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in applying supplier qualification criteria is a sourcing-process variability error, far from the sterilizing function, with procurement-quality consequences. Bounded at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Prequalification checklists standardize much of this, though subjective judgement still causes uneven application. Applying qualification criteria across suppliers is a defined step that proceeds at normal pace. Consistent application of qualification criteria depends on evaluator discipline, which varies across staff. Inconsistent screening risks selecting an unqualified supplier with costly consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate suppliers whose customer references for comparable sterilization applications cannot be verified
rows.plain: Choose suppliers whose customers can confirm their past work on similar sterilization jobs. This helps show that the suppliers can handle the required work.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable supplier references introduce mild sourcing risk but are peripheral to the machine's sterilize-and-cool function and low in severity; capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Reference contacts for niche sterilization uses are often unavailable or unverifiable, leaving supplier claims unconfirmed. Verifying references for comparable sterilization applications takes time chasing contacts. Judging whether a reference is truly comparable needs domain knowledge that is scarce. Unverified references raise the risk of a bad purchase, a large downside cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misclassifying a candidate supplier's financial stability because supporting evidence is incomplete or outdated
rows.plain: Use current and complete evidence when judging whether a possible supplier is financially dependable. This helps prevent a weak supplier from being chosen or a suitable one from being rejected.
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying supplier financial stability is a procurement due-diligence error, peripheral to sterilizing/cooling, and capped by the batch ceiling; consequences are commercial not functional.
rows.sat_rat: Standard tools like Dun & Bradstreet and audited statements catch most instability, but data can be stale or incomplete for private or foreign suppliers, leaving real recurring gaps. Pulling and reviewing financial reports takes days but is a routine part of standard procurement due diligence. Credit-agency reports and financial-ratio analysis are widely available, though correctly interpreting them still requires finance judgement not every buyer possesses. Third-party credit and financial-stability reports are cheap and commonly subscribed to, so cost is a minor barrier.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the technician waits for a required utility supply to become available for connection
rows.plain: The technician should receive the needed supply, such as water, power, or air, quickly enough to connect it. This prevents the connection work from being delayed.
rows.imp_rat: Technician waiting on a utility supply for connection is an installation-phase delay peripheral to the operating job, with recoverable scheduling consequences. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utility availability at commissioning is inconsistently coordinated across facility trades, so this waiting recurs regularly despite readiness checklists. Technicians frequently wait for steam, water, compressed air or power to be brought to the connection point, as utility readiness rarely aligns perfectly with the commissioning arrival. Connecting utilities is standard trade work, but coordinating their availability requires no special skill so the delay is logistical, not competence-driven. Waiting technicians represent wasted skilled labour on site, and temporary utility provisions add cost when the permanent supply lags.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled training disruption caused by unavailable or non-operational machine controls
rows.plain: Lose as little scheduled training time as possible when the machine controls are missing or do not work. This helps operators complete their training as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Scheduled training disruption from non-operational controls affects staff readiness rather than the core sterilize-and-cool job, with recoverable scheduling consequences. Held at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Because commissioning readiness is often uncertain, training slotted before controls are proven is a frequent and repeated disruption with only partial mitigation. Training is commonly scheduled to a fixed date, so when controls are unavailable or non-operational the whole session is disrupted or postponed with little slack. Trainers can partly substitute with manuals, simulators or dry-run walkthroughs, but hands-on control operation genuinely needs the live machine, limiting workarounds. A disrupted training day wastes assembled staff time and sometimes travel costs, a moderate but real recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of encountering an immovable obstruction within the designated installation footprint
rows.plain: Check that nothing fixed in place is blocking the area where the machine will go. This helps the machine be placed and made level without unexpected work.
rows.imp_rat: An immovable obstruction in the installation footprint is a site-preparation delay error, tangential to running sterilization cycles; consequences are schedule-based. Ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Site surveys and drawings usually reveal obstructions beforehand, though legacy utilities occasionally surprise crews. An unexpected obstruction found late can halt positioning and cause delay. Site surveying to confirm a clear footprint is routine facility-engineering work. Removing an immovable obstruction can be costly if discovered late.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between final machine placement and the specified coordinates, orientation, and clearances
rows.plain: Place the machine as close as possible to the planned location, direction, and surrounding space. This helps it fit and operate correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Placement deviation from specified coordinates is an installation-accuracy defect, peripheral to the sterilization cycle itself; capped at 1.0 by the ordering rule.
rows.sat_rat: Laser alignment and reference-point layout make hitting specified position and clearances highly repeatable. Placing to specified coordinates with laser tools is efficient and seldom a bottleneck. Accurate placement to spec still depends on skilled riggers and surveyors interpreting layout references. Placement itself consumes little material; only gross errors trigger costly repositioning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the specified machine levelness tolerance after positioning
rows.plain: Keep the machine as close as possible to the required level after it is placed. This helps it operate properly and avoid unnecessary wear.
rows.imp_rat: Levelness deviation after positioning is an installation-tolerance defect, marginal to sterilization outcomes; capped at 1.0 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Precision levels and adjustable mounts make meeting levelness tolerance a highly reliable, standard outcome. Leveling with precision levels and adjustable feet is a fast, well-practiced routine. Achieving tight levelness still relies on a technician's iterative shimming and reading skill. Leveling consumes cheap shims and grout, a negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of condensate or process water remaining trapped in discharge piping after drainage
rows.plain: Leave as little leftover liquid as possible in the drain pipes after they are emptied. This helps prevent leaks, buildup, and problems when the system is used again.
rows.imp_rat: Trapped condensate in discharge piping is a drainage contamination-risk error, secondary to the main sterilize-cool job; ceiling forces 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice relies on gravity slope and manual purging that frequently leaves pockets of condensate, so this outcome varies install-to-install and is a recurring unmitigated pain. Draining and blowing out trapped condensate adds unpredictable minutes at commissioning, and pooling water is often only discovered later when it causes problems. Correctly sloping and self-draining discharge piping depends on experienced pipefitters who understand condensate traps, and that judgement is not consistently applied on food-plant installs. Trapped process water is cheap to bleed off but the residual moisture drives hidden costs in microbial risk, corrosion and rework that the status quo does not reliably prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that missing delivered components are not identified during inspection
rows.plain: Check that every promised part arrived, so missing items are found during the inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to identify missing delivered components at inspection is an administrative receiving error (relevance) with minor, recoverable impact (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Packing lists and receiving inspection reliably surface most shortages, though small fasteners or sub-parts are occasionally overlooked. Component receipt checks against a packing list are quick and standard on delivery. Counting parts against a bill of materials needs little specialized skill, only careful reading. Detecting a missing part early avoids expensive line-down surprises, and the check itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of inspection delay while the delivered machine is not safely accessible
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to inspect a delivered machine when it cannot yet be reached safely. This helps staff complete the inspection sooner without taking unnecessary risks.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection delay while the machine is inaccessible is a delivery-access delay error, tangential to operating the sterilizer; ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established handling and safety procedures make safe access fairly predictable once the machine lands, though uneven site readiness still causes recurring delays. Guarding, lockout and safe-access provisions during delivery are covered by standard rigging and site-safety practice, but inspection still routinely stalls until the machine is fully positioned and secured. Making a delivered machine safely accessible is routine work for site riggers and EHS staff, requiring no scarce expertise. Access delays waste labour hours of waiting inspectors but incur little material cost, so the economic hit is modest and mostly schedule-driven.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspection findings lacking evidence needed to resolve delivery claims
rows.plain: Make sure every inspection problem has enough proof to support a claim about equipment delivered in poor condition. Without evidence, it may be difficult to show what was wrong when the equipment arrived.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection findings lacking evidence to resolve delivery claims is a purely commercial/contractual error far from the CFJ; its consequence is a disputed claim, not food-safety failure, so it scores near the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Photo documentation practice is improving but inspection findings still often lack the evidence needed to win delivery claims. Gathering claim evidence after the fact adds delay to dispute resolution. Documenting findings adequately needs attentive inspectors who know claim requirements. Weak evidence causes unrecoverable claim costs, a direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of failed coordination between machine and conveying-equipment operating states during line commissioning
rows.plain: Make sure the machine and the equipment that moves products along the line work in step during start-up testing. This prevents one from running when the other is not ready.
rows.imp_rat: Failed machine-conveyor coordination during commissioning is a setup-phase integration issue, peripheral with low-severity consequences. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard handshake protocols help, but state-coordination mismatches during commissioning are still commonly encountered. Failed state coordination stops the line and prolongs commissioning cycles. Handshake and interlock logic between machine and conveyors demands skilled controls programming. Coordination failures cost time rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed interfaces requiring rework because adjacent-equipment specifications were incorrect or incomplete
rows.plain: Make sure connections to nearby equipment are designed correctly the first time. This avoids having to redo completed work because important equipment details were missing or wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Interface rework from incorrect adjacent-equipment specs is an integration/administrative error unrelated to core sterilizing (low relevance) with absorbable rework cost (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Adjacent-equipment specs are frequently incomplete or wrong, so interface rework remains a recurring, poorly controlled problem. Interface rework driven by bad adjacent-equipment specs causes real schedule slips during commissioning. Reconciling incomplete third-party specifications demands experienced integration engineers. Rework of built interfaces wastes fabricated material and labor, a meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for facility network access authorization after commissioning is scheduled
rows.plain: Get permission to access the facility’s computer network quickly after the connection work is scheduled. This lets the equipment send information to the facility’s monitoring system sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for network access authorization after commissioning is an IT/logistics delay peripheral to the CFJ, causing schedule slip only. Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approval timelines are highly inconsistent and poorly coordinated with commissioning schedules, making this a chronic, unpredictable bottleneck with weak standard remedies. Facility network access authorization runs through IT/security queues that are notoriously slow and outside the commissioning team's control, so waiting after scheduling is common and often lengthy. Obtaining authorization is administrative rather than skill-limited, needing paperwork and approvals rather than expertise. Idle commissioning technicians waiting on network access burn expensive on-site labour and can extend costly project timelines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required facility network connection point is unavailable when connection work begins
rows.plain: The plant’s monitoring network should have a ready connection point when the connection work starts. This prevents the work from being delayed.
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable facility network connection point at install is a site-readiness scheduling error, peripheral to sterilization function; the delay is recoverable, keeping both proxies at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-commissioning IT coordination usually secures the drop, though occasional miscommunication leaves it unready. An unavailable network drop can stall connection work until IT provisions it. Confirming the connection point is basic coordination, not specialized skill. A missing port costs waiting time and a small provisioning expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required machine data points absent from or incorrectly represented in facility monitoring after connection
rows.plain: Make sure all important machine readings appear correctly in the facility’s monitoring system after connection. This helps staff see the machine’s condition and operation accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or misrepresented data points in facility monitoring is a reporting/integration defect distant from the sterilizing job (low relevance) with low direct consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual tag mapping and testing catch many errors but missing or mislabeled points are a common commissioning complaint. Verifying every data point maps correctly into facility monitoring is time-consuming point-by-point checking. Correct tag mapping needs controls engineers who understand both the machine and the SCADA/historian schema. Rework of missing or mismapped points is moderate but rarely material-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of machine network connection losses during commissioning verification
rows.plain: Keep the machine connected to the factory’s monitoring network during start-up checks. This allows staff to observe its status without repeated interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Network connection losses during commissioning verification are a one-time setup nuisance with trivial consequences to the CFJ. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Industrial networking practice is mature, but transient losses during commissioning still occur often enough to be a nuisance. Network dropouts during verification stall commissioning and force retesting. Diagnosing intermittent connection loss needs skilled network technicians. Connection losses waste labor hours rather than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine joints or ancillary connections left unsecured after assembly
rows.plain: Make sure every machine joint and connected part is firmly secured after assembly. Loose connections could cause the machine to work poorly or become unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Unsecured joints after assembly is a peripheral setup error (relevance) with modest consequences (severity), well below the ceiling for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Torque marking, checklists and second-person sign-off catch most unsecured joints, yet human oversight still lets a few slip through. Torque-checking and marking every joint adds inspection time but is a routine, budgeted step. Securing connections is low-skill work, though catching every unsecured joint depends on disciplined attention rather than expertise. A missed connection is cheap to fix at assembly but costly if it fails later, so the direct preparation-stage cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the operator waits for the processing chamber to become available for loading
rows.plain: Make the processing chamber available quickly so the operator can load the product without waiting.
rows.imp_rat: Operator wait for chamber availability is a throughput-comfort delay with trivial consequences to the sterilizing outcome. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Chamber-ready indicators are generally consistent on current PLC-controlled units, so operators rarely load prematurely. Modern batch sterilizers with automatic door sequencing and pre-heated jackets make the chamber available fairly quickly once a cycle ends, but operators still routinely wait through cooldown and drain phases before reload. Loading readiness depends mostly on the machine's automatic state signaling rather than operator skill, so little expertise is required to know when to load. Idle chamber wait represents modest lost throughput cost but does not consume significant material or energy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batch-to-batch turnaround during extended production runs
rows.plain: Move from one production batch to the next as quickly as practical during long production periods. This helps maintain a high level of output.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-to-batch turnaround duration is an efficiency metric (relevance) with recoverable delay consequences (severity); ranking places it low.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized turnaround routines make timing fairly predictable, though variability between batches persists. Batch-to-batch turnaround is a well-known time sink that established procedures only partly compress. Efficient turnaround depends on trained crews executing changeover steps quickly. Turnaround time is lost production capacity with associated labor and utility cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine holds while downstream packing cannot receive sterilized cups or lids
rows.plain: Keep machines from sitting idle when the packing team is not ready to receive the cleaned cups or lids. This helps the facility keep producing large batches without unnecessary waiting.
rows.imp_rat: Machine holds from downstream packing bottlenecks are peripheral to the sterilizing job (relevance) and cause only recoverable delay (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Accumulators and line signaling buffer short downstream stalls, but longer packing outages still force the machine to hold. Holds while packing cannot receive output are pure lost time until the downstream clears. Managing these holds is mostly operational coordination rather than specialized skill. Holding costs energy and idle capacity but usually spares material if handled promptly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption caused by the machine changeover
rows.plain: Make the switch from one product type to another as quickly as possible. This reduces the time production must stop between products.
rows.imp_rat: Changeover-related interruption is a throughput concern (relevance) causing recoverable delay (severity), held below the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: SMED-style practices and quick-change tooling exist but are unevenly applied, so changeover interruptions remain lengthy. Changeover interruption is a major and directly felt block of lost production time. Fast changeover requires skilled, well-drilled operators to hit setup targets. Extended changeover is costly idle time plus setup material and cleaning consumption.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the wait for final cycle status after sterilization and cooling end
rows.plain: Shorten the wait for confirmation that the sterilizing process finished successfully after the equipment has cooled. This helps workers know sooner whether the process is complete.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for final cycle status after the cycle ends is a minor latency issue with negligible impact on the conserved food. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated status determination is consistent, though occasional sensor faults can delay a definitive result until reviewed. PLC-controlled sterilizers display pass/fail cycle status almost immediately at cycle end on the HMI, so the wait for final status is typically short. Reading a final status light or HMI message requires minimal operator judgement. The brief status wait carries negligible resource cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the sterilization or cooling cycle stopping before the demonstration is complete
rows.plain: Prevent the machine’s cleaning or cooling process from stopping before observers can see the full demonstration. This helps people understand how the machine works from start to finish.
rows.imp_rat: A cycle stopping mid-demonstration is confined to demo scenarios and has low relevance and mild consequences for the core job. Capped per batch-3 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature interlock and utility-monitoring systems make modern sterilizers quite robust against unplanned cycle interruption, so completion is usually assured. An unexpected mid-cycle stop forces a restart that costs significant time, though such stops are infrequent. Preventing premature stops relies on properly maintained equipment more than on operator skill during the demo itself. An aborted cycle wastes energy and potentially the product load, but this is uncommon.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of observer misunderstanding of the machine functions demonstrated
rows.plain: Make sure people watching the demonstration understand what the machine does and how it operates.
rows.imp_rat: Observer misunderstanding during a demonstration is only weakly tied to actually sterilizing food (relevance) and has trivial operational consequences (severity), so it stays at the ceiling floor.
rows.sat_rat: Without standardized visual aids or interfaces, comprehension is inconsistent across audiences and misunderstandings recur. Explaining functions clearly during a demo takes presenter time but is not a throughput bottleneck. Avoiding observer misunderstanding depends entirely on the demonstrator's communication skill and the observers' baseline knowledge, which varies widely. Clarifying misunderstandings costs mainly extra presenter time rather than material.

rows.source_job: Scale processing output to batch size
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish line disruption from machine output mismatching production demand
rows.plain: Spend less time stopping the germ-free medicine filling and packaging line when the machine makes more or less than the current order requires. This helps the line match the size of each production batch.
rows.imp_rat: Output-to-demand mismatch on the fill-finish line is a planning concern (relevance) with limited direct consequence (severity), kept below the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Production planning and rate adjustment help, but demand-output mismatch on sterile fill-finish lines still causes recurring disruption. Output mismatching demand causes disruption windows that extend line downtime. Matching output to demand relies on planning skill and scheduling judgement. Over- or under-supply wastes capacity and can spoil sterile product waiting too long.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surfaces with residual product soil after sanitation
rows.plain: Leave less product residue on surfaces after cleaning. This helps prevent leftover material from contaminating the next use.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product soil on contact surfaces after sanitation bears on contamination but this batch's ceiling forces importance to 1.0 regardless; consequences would otherwise be serious but ranking decides it here.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sanitation and ATP/visual checks catch most residual soil, yet complex geometries and dead legs leave occasional residue that standard practice does not fully eliminate. Thorough sanitation of product-contact surfaces takes significant cleaning time that is squeezed by production schedules. Trained sanitation crews following validated CIP/COP procedures handle most surfaces, though manual disassembly cleaning still depends on operator thoroughness. Cleaning agents, water, and energy are recurring costs but broadly affordable and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of temperature and pressure sensors that remain outside allowable sterilization tolerances after calibration
rows.plain: Avoid leaving temperature and pressure measuring devices outside the allowed accuracy range after checking and adjusting them. This helps ensure the germ-killing process is controlled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Sensors outside sterilization tolerance after calibration would ordinarily be central, but the batch ceiling caps both proxies; scored at 1.0 per the mandated ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated calibration and post-calibration verification make out-of-tolerance sterilization sensors fairly rare where the practice is followed. Sensor calibration and verification is a defined procedure of predictable duration. Bringing temperature and pressure sensors within tolerance requires skilled calibration technicians and reference standards. Calibration labor and standards cost, but out-of-tolerance sensors risk far costlier batch failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the required sterilization operating range left unverified during sensor calibration
rows.plain: Leave less of the temperature range needed for safe sterilizing unchecked when testing the temperature sensors. This helps show that the sensors work across the full range they must measure.
rows.imp_rat: Unverified sterilization operating range during calibration matters to safety, but the batch ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1 each.
rows.sat_rat: Standard calibration procedures reduce but do not eliminate unverified range portions, leaving recurring blind spots at the temperature extremes where sterilization safety actually matters most. Multi-point calibration across the full sterilization range takes time, so technicians under pressure often verify only the operating setpoint band and skip the extremes. Proper range coverage demands a trained metrology technician who understands the machine's critical control points, and that expertise is unevenly available in food plants. Reference standards and traceable calibration equipment exist and are affordable enough that cost is not the binding constraint here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected movement of pumps, valves, or conveyors during lubrication
rows.plain: Prevent pumps, valves, and conveyor belts from moving unexpectedly while they are being lubricated. This helps keep the worker safe and prevents equipment damage.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected movement of pumps/valves/conveyors during lubrication mirrors item 17 — peripheral to the core job (relevance) with contained impact (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Component-level lockout is standard, though multiple independent drives increase the chance one is missed, leaving residual movement risk. Isolating pumps, valves and conveyors individually adds minor, predictable time to the lube task. Identifying and locking each moving component requires basic training that most maintainers have. Isolation points on these components are standard and inexpensive to use.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required lubrication points left untreated during the maintenance cycle
rows.plain: Make sure every moving part that needs oil or grease receives it during scheduled maintenance, so the equipment keeps working properly and is less likely to wear out.
rows.imp_rat: Untreated lubrication points is a maintenance-completeness reliability error; relevant to longevity but peripheral to a single cycle, and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Lubrication schedules and point diagrams are standard, but hard-to-reach points still get occasionally missed. Lubrication rounds are quick and routine. Following a lube-point map needs only basic trained maintenance staff. Lubricant is cheap; missed points cause expensive premature wear.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production disruption caused by the update
rows.plain: Spend less time stopping germ-free medicine production when the equipment’s operating software is changed. This helps production restart sooner after the change.
rows.imp_rat: Production disruption caused by an update is a transient software/config event, marginal to sterilizing (relevance) with trivial lasting impact (severity).
