rows.sat_rat: Update rollouts usually go planned but occasionally trigger unexpected reboots or revalidation needs that extend disruption. Software/firmware updates cause bounded but sometimes underestimated production pauses. Applying and verifying updates in a validated environment requires controls expertise. Update downtime and revalidation effort carry moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required update and validation records left incomplete after the update
rows.plain: Reduce the number of records that are left incomplete after updating the control software. Complete records show what was changed and whether the updated software still works correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete update and validation records are a documentation gap, only distantly related to the sterilization job itself, and consequences are administrative follow-up. Bounded by the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Post-update validation documentation is commonly left partially incomplete because the update work is prioritized over the paperwork. Documenting update and validation results is a bounded task done alongside the work. Completing validation records correctly requires QA knowledge of protocol requirements. Incomplete validation records force costly revalidation and delay release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of displayed performance parameters that misrepresent actual machine conditions
rows.plain: Show fewer readings that give a false picture of the machine’s actual condition. Accurate readings help workers notice problems and respond appropriately.
rows.imp_rat: Misrepresented performance parameters could mislead operators, but this batch's ranking forces relevance and severity to 1 each.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic calibration catches many display errors, but drift between calibrations still leaves displayed parameters misrepresenting actual conditions at times. Cross-checking displayed values against reference readings is a moderately time-consuming manual task. Recognizing that a displayed parameter misrepresents actual conditions requires skilled interpretation and independent measurement. Misleading displays can cause bad decisions and spoiled batches, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between the onset of an anomalous machine condition and its visibility to the administrator
rows.plain: Spend less time between a machine beginning to work unusually and the administrator being able to see it. This helps the administrator respond sooner when a machine may have a problem.
rows.imp_rat: Delayed visibility of an anomalous condition to the administrator matters more but the batch ceiling still forces relevance/severity down to keep importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Where alerting is manual or local-only, the onset-to-visibility delay is inconsistent and can be long, though modern monitored sites do better. Without continuous remote monitoring, anomalies often surface only at the next round or alarm review, delaying administrator visibility. Recognizing an anomaly as significant can require experienced interpretation of readings, which is not always present at the moment it occurs. Basic alarm and SCADA notification hardware is affordable, so cost is not the main barrier to faster visibility.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the administrator misclassifying a machine condition because of conflicting status indications
rows.plain: Avoid recording the wrong machine condition when different signs give conflicting information. A correct status helps people respond appropriately to the machine.
rows.imp_rat: Administrator misclassifying machine condition from conflicting indicators is a data/record error (relevance) with limited operational consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Legacy machines commonly present conflicting or ambiguous status indications with no single source of truth, so misclassification remains a recurring problem. Reconciling conflicting indicators forces the administrator to spend extra time cross-checking multiple screens and logs. Correctly interpreting contradictory status signals demands experienced judgement that not every administrator possesses. The tools involved are existing HMIs and SCADA at no added cost, but misreads can cause costly wrong actions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that preventive maintenance is scheduled after the machine's required maintenance date
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the machine’s regular upkeep is planned after it is due. This helps keep required maintenance on time.
rows.imp_rat: Preventive maintenance scheduled past the due date is an administrative timing error (low relevance) with gradual, manageable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated due-date reminders make overdue PM uncommon, though production pressure and deferrals still cause occasional slips. CMMS scheduling with lead-time alerts generally triggers PM before the due date. Scheduling PM is a planner function needing modest skill, well supported by software. CMMS scheduling tools are affordable and widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualified maintenance personnel are unavailable for the scheduled maintenance window
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that people with the needed maintenance skills are unavailable during the planned maintenance period. This helps ensure the work happens as scheduled.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable maintenance personnel is a staffing/scheduling error peripheral to sterilizing performance (low relevance) with reschedulable impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cross-training and contractor pools help, but skilled-labor shortages still leave windows under-staffed with some regularity. Securing qualified staff for a window is a planning task usually done ahead of time. The core issue is scarcity of qualified maintenance personnel, who are hard to schedule and cover. Overtime, contractors or specialist callouts to fill gaps add meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed maintenance and sanitation events omitted from the machine's history
rows.plain: Fewer completed maintenance and cleaning events should be missing from the machine's history. This helps workers know how the machine has been cared for and cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: Missing maintenance/sanitation history entries reduce recordkeeping quality but not core function; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Manual and semi-manual history keeping predictably leaves a notable share of completed maintenance and sanitation events unrecorded, weakening the audit trail. Logging every completed event into the machine history competes with immediate return-to-production, so entries get dropped when work is finished off-hours. Recording an event requires little skill, so omissions stem from inconsistent habit rather than lack of capability. The logbook or CMMS entry costs almost nothing, making omissions a discipline gap rather than a cost issue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine-history entries containing incorrect event details
rows.plain: Keep the machine’s history free of wrong information about what happened. Accurate records help people understand past work and make sound maintenance decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect event details in machine-history entries is a recordkeeping error entirely peripheral to the sterilize-and-cool job, with only administrative consequences, so it sits near the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Manual machine-history logging remains error-prone, with wrong dates, part numbers and event descriptions recurring frequently. Entering history details is quick, so time is not the constraint. Accurate event capture depends on technician diligence and understanding of what to record, which varies widely. Incorrect history entries themselves are cheap but cause costly downstream misdiagnosis and audit issues.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of intended product formats, container types, and processing volumes omitted from the fit requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the evaluation covers every planned product size, container type, and amount processed. This shows whether the process will work for all intended products.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting product formats/container types/volumes from fit requirements is a specification error tangential to the core job with contained severity, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Fit-requirement lists routinely omit less-frequent formats and volume scenarios, so gaps are common despite checklists. Compiling product formats, container types, and volumes is a defined upfront task with modest time cost. It draws on production and product knowledge generally held by the buying team. Missed formats drive expensive retrofits, but capturing them costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the machine's documented limits for product, container, and volume compatibility
rows.plain: Clearly document which products, containers, and amounts the machine can and cannot handle. This helps people decide whether the machine is suitable for the work they need to do.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous compatibility limits for product/container/volume relates to fit, yet the batch ordering forces relevance and severity to stay at or below 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation of compatibility limits is often imprecise, leaving recurring uncertainty about what the machine can actually handle. Clarifying compatibility limits during evaluation is achievable within the acquisition schedule. Interpreting documented limits for product, container, and volume compatibility needs process-engineering judgement. Ambiguous compatibility limits risk buying a machine unfit for the intended product range, an expensive error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in the fit evaluation caused by unavailable representative products, cups, or lids
rows.plain: Test whether the process works with suitable products, cups, and lids without waiting for samples to become available. This helps identify problems before choosing or changing equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Fit-evaluation delay from unavailable representative products is a scheduling error only indirectly tied to sterilization; held at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Availability of representative materials is chronically unreliable, so this delay recurs frequently in practice. Fit evaluations stall for weeks when representative products, cups or lids are not on hand. Assembling representative samples is logistical rather than skill-intensive. The delay ties up staff and evaluation slots, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between evaluation conditions and intended production conditions for sterilization and cooling
rows.plain: Make sure the test conditions are similar to the conditions the equipment will face during real production, especially when sterilizing and cooling products.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between evaluation and production conditions for sterilization/cooling matters to validity, but the batch ranking caps both proxies at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor demos rarely replicate the buyer's real product mix, container types and throughput, leaving a persistent mismatch that undermines the decision. Arranging evaluations that mirror production conditions adds lead time for setup and scheduling. Specifying representative sterilization and cooling conditions requires deep thermal-process knowledge that few buyers possess. Off-spec evaluation conditions risk buying an unsuitable machine, a costly error the evaluation cost cannot offset.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in the machine's projected processing capacity for the intended products and containers
rows.plain: Be more certain about how much of the intended product the machine can process in the available containers. This helps show whether the machine is suitable for the planned work.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in projected processing capacity relates to fit, yet this batch's mandatory ceiling limits relevance and severity to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Projected capacity is usually based on idealized vendor assumptions rather than the buyer's exact products and containers, leaving real uncertainty. Getting a capacity projection from the vendor is reasonably prompt but validating it against real products takes time. Translating cycle times into realistic throughput for specific containers needs process-engineering judgement that is scarce on the buyer side. Overstated capacity leads to buying an under-sized or over-sized line, a significant capital misallocation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assessing the purchase without references from comparable sterile fill-finish cup-and-lid applications
rows.plain: Do not judge the purchase without checking references from similar uses involving sterile products sealed in cups and lids. Comparable customer experience can help show whether the supplier is suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Assessing a purchase without comparable-application references is a procurement-vetting error, tangential to the machine's sterilizing job, and the ceiling holds it low; consequences are limited to decision quality.
rows.sat_rat: Comparable sterile cup-and-lid references are scarce and reference-checking is inconsistently done, leaving this poorly served. Sourcing references from comparable cup-and-lid fill-finish applications takes time and networking. Knowing which references are truly comparable requires niche application expertise. Buying without comparable references risks a costly misfit that reference-checking would avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recurring sterilization, cooling, or supplier-service problems are omitted from collected references and reviews
rows.plain: Make sure reviews and customer feedback include any repeated problems with cleaning, cooling, or help from the supplier. This gives a more complete picture of the supplier's reliability.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting recurring service problems from references is a due-diligence defect with modest bearing on whether food is sterilized; ranking caps relevance and severity at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: References are commonly collected but recurring sterilization, cooling and service problems are routinely omitted or downplayed, so coverage is unreliable. Gathering references and reviews takes outreach time that is often abbreviated. Eliciting candid accounts of recurring problems requires skilled reference interviewing that many buyers lack. Reference checking is low-cost, so money is not the constraint.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of collected claims about machine results and supplier support that cannot be verified
rows.plain: Fewer claims about how well a machine works or how well a supplier supports customers should be impossible to verify. This helps buyers make decisions based on reliable information.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable supplier claims are a due-diligence concern peripheral to the core job with modest consequences, capped at 1.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Many supplier claims about results and support cannot be independently verified, and buyers frequently take them on trust. Verifying supplier claims and references takes time that compressed acquisition schedules rarely allow. Distinguishing verifiable performance claims from marketing needs experienced evaluators. Reference visits and independent verification add cost that buyers often skip.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency between vendor claims, reference accounts, and reviews concerning sterilization results, cooling results, and supplier support
rows.plain: Get consistent information about how well the equipment sterilizes and cools products, and how much help the supplier provides after purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among vendor claims and reviews is a due-diligence error, only indirectly tied to sterilization outcomes; the batch cap limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Independent reviews for specialized food sterilizers are sparse and vendor claims are self-serving, so inconsistencies persist with no authoritative source to resolve them. Chasing references and independent reviews for niche sterilization machines drags out over weeks with slow callbacks. Judging whether vendor claims match reality demands scarce process-engineering expertise to interpret contradictory evidence. Being misled about performance risks a large mis-investment, but verification itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time reference contacts take to provide usable feedback before the purchase decision
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for people who have used a provider to give useful feedback before deciding whether to buy. Timely feedback supports a quicker, better-informed choice.
rows.imp_rat: Time for reference contacts to provide feedback is a purchase-diligence delay peripheral to the machine's function (low relevance) with minor consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: References are self-selected favorable contacts who may withhold problems, so the feedback is inconsistent in candor and usefulness. Chasing reference contacts for candid usable feedback depends entirely on their goodwill and availability, so turnaround is slow, unpredictable, and often stretches over weeks of unanswered calls. Eliciting genuinely useful reference feedback takes buyer judgement to ask the right probing questions, which not every procurement team has. The activity costs mainly staff time and occasional travel to site visits, a modest but non-trivial drain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of quote requests issued with missing cup-and-lid sterilization, throughput, or facility-integration requirements
rows.plain: Send fewer price requests without requirements for sterilizing the container and cover, the needed production rate, or how the equipment must connect with the site. This helps suppliers price the right solution.
rows.imp_rat: Quote requests missing sterilization/throughput/integration requirements are a procurement-stage omission peripheral to the machine's function, low in severity, held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Quote requests frequently go out missing key integration or throughput requirements because inputs are siloed. Assembling RFQ requirements is a standard early step with manageable duration. Specifying sterilization, throughput, and integration needs requires cross-functional expertise not always coordinated. Incomplete quote requests trigger requote cycles that add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualified sterilizing-machine suppliers that fail to submit formal quotes
rows.plain: Fewer approved suppliers of sterilizing machines should fail to send a formal price offer. This gives the buyer enough complete offers to compare.
rows.imp_rat: Qualified suppliers failing to submit quotes is a sourcing-completeness issue peripheral to the CFJ with low severity; capped at 1.0 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: RFQ processes are established, but for niche sterilizing machines qualified suppliers still non-respond, leaving gaps. Chasing suppliers for formal quotes stretches the sourcing timeline. Identifying and engaging qualified suppliers needs market knowledge that varies by buyer. Fewer quotes weakens price competition, indirectly raising cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in receiving formal quotes beyond the procurement response deadline
rows.plain: Receive official price offers by the purchasing deadline. This gives the buyer enough time to compare them and choose a supplier.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in receiving formal quotes past the deadline is a procurement-schedule error remote from the CFJ; the batch ceiling limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Quote turnaround varies widely by vendor with no enforceable standard, so deadline overruns recur. Formal quotes for configured sterilization machines frequently arrive past the procurement deadline due to engineering effort. Chasing and clarifying quotes needs a competent buyer but no rare expertise. Late quotes can stall the project schedule but carry little direct monetary loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among the scope and operating assumptions in supplier quotes
rows.plain: Make sure supplier price offers are based on the same work, conditions, and operating expectations. This makes the offers fair and easier to compare.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in scope and operating assumptions across supplier quotes is a pre-purchase evaluation ambiguity, distant from the sterilizing job, with negotiation-level consequences. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers quote to differing assumptions and there is no standard normalization, so inconsistency persists routinely. Normalizing scope assumptions across quotes takes significant manual effort and time. It takes an experienced evaluator to surface hidden scope and assumption differences between quotes. Unaligned quote scopes lead to apples-to-oranges decisions and unbudgeted add-ons.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of quotes omitting required price components, payment terms, delivery commitments, warranties, or quote-validity conditions
rows.plain: Receive fewer price offers that leave out required costs, payment rules, delivery promises, guarantees, or how long the offer remains valid. This makes offers clearer and easier to compare.
rows.imp_rat: Quotes omitting price/terms/warranty components are a commercial-completeness error, peripheral to sterilizing food and recoverable, capped at 1.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established RFQ formats mean most quotes carry required price, terms, and warranty fields, though some still arrive incomplete. Standard quote templates speed the return of complete commercial terms. Procurement staff are trained to spot missing price and warranty components. Missing terms cause negotiation rework but are usually caught before commitment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required space and clearance measurements recorded incorrectly
rows.plain: Make sure all measurements of the available space and required clearance are recorded correctly. Accurate measurements help confirm that the equipment will fit and can be accessed safely.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly recorded space and clearance measurements affect installation logistics rather than the sterilization/cooling job directly, so relevance is low; consequences are recoverable and the ceiling holds this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Measurements are taken manually and often once, so transcription and reading errors recur. Recording space and clearance measurements is a quick on-site task. Accurate measurement requires basic surveying discipline that is generally available. A mismeasurement can force costly rework at installation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required utility connections with unverified capacity or compatibility
rows.plain: Reduce the number of needed connections to site services whose available capacity or ability to work with the system has not been confirmed. This helps prevent problems when the system is installed.
rows.imp_rat: Unverified utility capacity/compatibility is a procurement-stage concern only tangentially tied to the core functional job, with modest severity, and the batch ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utility verification is inconsistently performed, leaving connections unchecked until installation surprises appear. Verifying utility capacity and compatibility takes coordination time with facilities. Confirming electrical, water, steam, and air compatibility needs engineering expertise. Unverified utilities lead to expensive emergency upgrades at commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting utility, space, and clearance requirements supplied by involved parties
rows.plain: Make sure everyone gives the same information about needed utilities, room, and open space around the equipment. This helps confirm that the site can safely fit and operate it.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting utility and clearance requirements are installation-planning ambiguities peripheral to the CFJ with modest severity; batch ceiling caps at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements from vendor, integrator and facilities are seldom fully aligned, so conflicting specs recur and must be manually reconciled. Reconciling conflicting utility and clearance requirements across parties takes back-and-forth iterations. Resolving utility and space conflicts requires facilities and process engineering judgement that is not always coordinated. Unresolved conflicts cause site-prep rework, a meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that concealed site obstructions invalidate the accommodation assessment
rows.plain: Make sure hidden obstacles at the site are found before deciding whether the equipment will fit. Otherwise, the equipment may not fit or be usable after delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Concealed site obstructions invalidating the accommodation assessment is a site-planning error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with recoverable impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Concealed obstructions invalidating the accommodation assessment are a recurring, poorly-mitigated surprise since standard practice relies on visible inspection. Uncovering concealed obstructions may require intrusive investigation that most acquirers skip to stay on schedule. Detecting hidden site conditions demands engineering survey expertise beyond a routine walk-through. Thorough concealed-condition surveys cost more than visual checks, so they are commonly omitted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of technician waiting to inspect required site areas
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for the technician to inspect the parts of the site needed for the work. This helps the technician understand the site requirements sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Technician waiting to inspect site areas is a peripheral scheduling delay with low relevance to the sterilizing machine's core sterilize-and-cool job, and its consequences are easily absorbed; capped at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Site-inspection scheduling is a routine practice but access coordination across facilities is inconsistent, causing recurring waits. Technicians often wait for site access windows to inspect required areas, adding schedule slack. Coordinating site access is administrative and needs no rare skill. Idle technician time is a modest cost when scheduling slips.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contractual discrepancies between the purchaser and seller at signing
rows.plain: Resolve any remaining differences in the contract between the buyer and seller before they sign. This helps prevent later disputes about what was agreed.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contractual discrepancies at signing are a commercial matter peripheral to sterilizing food, with limited direct severity, and are capped at 1.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Standard redline/counter-signature workflows catch most contradictions, yet residual ambiguities in technical annexes still slip through to signing occasionally. Contract negotiation and clause reconciliation take weeks but mature legal review cycles usually close them before signing. Procurement and legal specialists are typically available to spot discrepancies, though niche capital-equipment terms still demand scarce domain judgement. Legal and procurement review adds moderate transactional cost but is a standard, budgeted line for capital purchases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required internal purchase approvals are missing when the transaction is presented for signature
rows.plain: Make sure all required company approvals are in place before the purchase is sent for signing. This helps the transaction be completed without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Missing purchase approvals at signature is a signature-workflow delay with low consequence for the sterilizing function itself; ceiling fixes it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: ERP and e-signature approval workflows make missing sign-offs at signature uncommon where such systems are in place. Approval routing before signature is a defined step that workflow systems generally complete on schedule. Assembling required approvals is administrative and needs little specialized expertise. The approval process itself costs little beyond staff time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unbudgeted taxes, fees, and currency charges included in the final transaction price
rows.plain: Avoid unexpected taxes, fees, and exchange-rate costs being added to the final purchase price. This helps keep the transaction within the expected cost.
rows.imp_rat: Unbudgeted taxes and currency charges is a financial-transaction error remote from the CFJ; consequences are absorbable and the batch cap fixes it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: These charges surprise buyers often, especially on imported machines, making the outcome unreliable without specialist review. Clarifying taxes, duties, and currency charges can extend negotiation but is usually manageable. Anticipating cross-border taxes, fees, and FX exposure requires trade-finance knowledge often lacking in-house. Unbudgeted taxes, fees, and currency swings regularly inflate the final price beyond plan.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of payment-release delay beyond the contractual deadline
rows.plain: Release payment no later than the agreed deadline. This helps complete the purchase on time and avoids delays for the seller.
rows.imp_rat: Payment-release delay beyond contractual deadline is a financial-timing error peripheral to the machine's function; the batch cap fixes it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established AP and milestone-tracking processes make payment timing fairly predictable, though internal approvals still cause occasional overruns. Payment-release delays are common but bounded by ERP AP workflows and contractual milestones. Releasing payment is a routine finance task needing no scarce skill. Late payment may trigger interest or strain vendor goodwill, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required quality, compliance, or acceptance documents omitted from the executed purchase package
rows.plain: Make sure the completed purchase file includes every required quality, rule-following, and acceptance record, so the transaction is fully documented.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted quality/compliance documents in the purchase package are administrative defects with low centrality to the sterilize-and-cool job and low severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Executed purchase packages follow standard document sets, but quality/acceptance items are still sometimes omitted. Compiling acceptance and compliance documents adds closing-stage effort. Identifying required quality and compliance documents needs regulatory awareness. Omissions cause rework rather than direct overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of qualifying a supplier whose machine cannot meet sterile fill-finish sterilization and cooling requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of choosing a supplier whose machine cannot properly clean, sterilize, and cool products during final sterile preparation. This helps ensure the machine can perform the required job.
rows.imp_rat: Qualifying a supplier whose machine can't meet sterilization/cooling requirements is highly relevant to the CFJ, but the batch ceiling forces importance to 1.0 as the ranking already placed it below prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Established FAT and specification-matching practices reduce mismatch risk, but subtle performance gaps against sterile requirements still surface after purchase. Confirming a machine meets sterile fill-finish specs requires technical review and sometimes trial runs that extend evaluation time. Assessing sterilization and cooling performance against microbial requirements demands specialized process and validation expertise that is scarce. Detailed technical validation and factory acceptance testing add cost that some buyers underfund.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required supplier quality and regulatory evidence that cannot be verified
rows.plain: Make sure all required proof of a supplier’s product quality and legal compliance can be checked. This matters so the business does not rely on claims it cannot confirm.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable supplier quality/regulatory evidence is a procurement-administrative concern peripheral to the machine's core sterilize-and-cool job (low relevance); consequences are absorbable during vendor review (low severity). Capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier quality evidence is usually provided but some items remain unverifiable, leaving recurring gaps. Collecting and checking quality and regulatory evidence adds review time but is routine. Verifying quality/regulatory documents needs QA expertise that is generally available in regulated food/pharma buyers. Audit and verification effort is a budgeted, moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of qualifying a supplier unable to fulfill delivery, service, and support commitments
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of approving a supplier that cannot deliver the goods or provide the promised service and help. This helps avoid problems after the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Qualifying a supplier unable to meet commitments is a procurement error tangential to the machine's function, held at the ceiling by ranking; consequences are commercial.
rows.sat_rat: Standard supplier-qualification checklists catch obvious weaknesses, but over-promising on service and support still slips through frequently. Vetting delivery, service, and support capability requires reference checks and site visits that lengthen the qualification cycle. Judging whether a supplier can truly honor its commitments demands experienced procurement judgement beyond checklist verification. Supplier audits and reference travel cost money, and thorough vetting is often trimmed to control acquisition expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of total acquisition cost omitted from supplier quotations
rows.plain: Make sure supplier price quotes include all costs of buying and receiving the equipment, not just the headline price. This makes it easier to compare suppliers fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting total acquisition cost from quotations is a quotation-completeness error, only tangentially related to the sterilizing job; low severity and the ceiling limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because suppliers quote inconsistently, significant cost elements are frequently missing from the quoted price. Compiling total acquisition cost takes extra effort that is often skipped under time pressure. Building a true total-cost-of-ownership view demands cost-analysis expertise that many buyers do not fully apply. Quotations routinely omit installation, commissioning, spares, and lifecycle costs, so hidden spend is a chronic problem.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in applying commercial, technical, service, delivery, and support criteria across potential suppliers
rows.plain: Use the same standards to compare possible suppliers on price, equipment, service, delivery, and support.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent application of evaluation criteria across suppliers is a procurement-process error remote from the machine's function; ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard weighted-scorecard practice exists and is widely used, though criteria are still applied unevenly across commercial, technical and support dimensions in practice. Applying a scoring matrix across suppliers is quick once a template exists. Consistent multi-criteria evaluation requires a trained procurement lead and cross-functional input to avoid drifting weightings. Inconsistent criteria can pick a costlier or weaker supplier, but the evaluation process itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that defined sterilization requirements do not achieve the required microbial control for cups and lids
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning instructions reduce germs on the cups and lids enough for their intended use.
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient microbial control for cups/lids strikes the heart of the sterilizing CFJ and would normally score high, yet the batch-4 ceiling mandates it stay at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature microbial-control standards make correct sterilization specification the norm, though edge cases in cup/lid geometry can still cause gaps. Setting sterilization requirements draws on established microbial-control standards, which streamlines spec time. Defining validated sterilization parameters requires microbiology and process-validation expertise that is specialized but generally accessible in this industry. Referencing existing sterilization standards is inexpensive relative to a validation failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between specified capacity and peak production throughput
rows.plain: Make sure the capacity requested for the equipment is enough to handle the highest expected production rate.
rows.imp_rat: Capacity-to-peak-throughput mismatch is a specification issue not central to sterilizing; both proxies held at 1 per the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Peak-capacity analysis is mature practice, yet peak-demand assumptions can still be understated. Capacity-vs-peak checking is a standard evaluation task performed on schedule. Sizing to peak throughput is routine engineering but depends on accurate peak-demand data. Undersizing forces costly future upgrades; oversizing wastes capital, only partly controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of in-scope cup and lid formats omitted from the purchase requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the purchasing requirements include every cup and lid size or type that is needed, so nothing important is left out when buying supplies.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting in-scope cup/lid formats from requirements is an upstream specification gap, peripheral to the actual sterilizing function and recoverable, held to 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Format lists are often incomplete because edge-case SKUs get overlooked, though standard requirement templates catch the common ones. Cataloguing in-scope cup and lid formats is quick when done during requirements definition. Listing formats needs packaging knowledge that production teams usually possess. Omitted formats cause costly change orders later, but the upfront listing itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that defined cooling requirements leave sterilized cups and lids unsuitable for downstream handling
rows.plain: Make sure the cooling instructions keep the cleaned cups and lids suitable for the next handling steps.
rows.imp_rat: Inadequate cooling requirements leaving cups/lids unsuitable relates directly to the cooling half of the CFJ, but the batch ceiling forces the mean to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established specification templates cover cooling, but requirements that leave containers unsuitable for handling still occasionally get through evaluation. Defining cooling requirements for downstream handling is part of normal spec development but can be iterative and slow. Specifying cooling to keep cups and lids suitable requires process engineering knowledge that is specialized. Getting the cooling spec right upfront is low-cost relative to downstream rejects it prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required utility, footprint, and control-interface constraints omitted from the purchase requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the number of purchase requirements that leave out needed services, space, and ways to connect the system to the controls. This helps ensure suppliers know exactly what the site can support.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting utility/footprint/control constraints from purchase requirements is an upstream specification error, peripheral to actually sterilizing food, with limited consequences; capped at 1.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Purchase requirements are often drafted without a full constraints checklist, so items are frequently omitted. Documenting utility, footprint, and interface constraints upfront adds requirement-writing time. Capturing control-interface and footprint constraints needs technical specification skill. Omitted constraints drive costly change orders later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required validation evidence absent from the food sterilizing machine review package
rows.plain: Make sure the review file for the food-sterilizing machine contains all the required proof that it works safely and as intended. This matters so the machine’s performance can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Missing validation evidence in the review package affects paperwork completeness rather than the actual sterilization function (low relevance), and gaps are correctable pre-purchase (low severity). Ceiling caps this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Required validation evidence is often partially absent from review packages because supplier documentation is inconsistent. Assembling the validation review package is slow when suppliers deliver evidence piecemeal. Judging validation-evidence completeness demands validation specialists in short supply. Missing evidence forces added testing, raising acquisition cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which documented tests fail to represent intended cup-and-lid sterilization and cooling conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the documented tests accurately reflect the planned conditions for sterilizing and cooling the cups and lids, so the test results can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Tests not representing intended cup-and-lid conditions is a validation gap, yet the batch cap pins both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Documented tests routinely use generic conditions rather than the intended cup-and-lid case, making this among the worst served. Getting tests that represent the actual cup-and-lid sterilization/cooling conditions requires slow bespoke trials. Judging test representativeness for specific cup-and-lid formats needs specialized sterilization expertise. Non-representative tests risk validation failure and expensive post-purchase remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validation records that cannot be traced to approved protocols, test runs, or measurement sources
rows.plain: Make sure every test record can be linked to an approved test plan, the test that was performed, and the source of the measurement. This makes the results trustworthy and easier to check.
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable validation records are a documentation-integrity concern only indirectly tied to the sterilizing job, with contained severity, and are held to 1.0 by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability gaps between records, protocols, and measurement sources are common because supplier documentation practices vary widely. Tracing validation records to protocols is time-consuming when documentation is fragmented across suppliers. Establishing traceability chains requires quality-assurance and validation specialists who are scarce. Untraceable records force re-validation, a significant cost hit during acquisition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions among test results and conclusions about sterilization effectiveness, temperature control, cooling performance, or repeatability
rows.plain: Resolve contradictions between test results and the conclusions about whether sterilizing works, temperatures stay controlled, cooling performs properly, and results can be repeated. This helps confirm that the process works reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Contradictions among sterilization/temperature/cooling test conclusions touch the core job but sit below the prior batch, so despite relevance the ceiling forces both proxies to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Test conclusions from suppliers and buyers often contradict each other with no standard reconciliation practice, so unresolved discrepancies routinely persist. Reconciling test reports from multiple runs and labs is slow and often deferred to late in evaluation. Interpreting sterilization, thermal, and repeatability data requires scarce process-validation expertise that many buyers lack in-house. Re-testing or arbitration to resolve contradictory results is expensive and frequently unbudgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the evidence review delay caused by unavailable validation records or responses
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to review proof that the system works properly when test records or answers are unavailable. This helps the review finish sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence-review delay from missing validation records is an administrative bottleneck peripheral to the CFJ, and its consequences are minor rework; capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors' validation documentation for sterilization is often incomplete or slow, making this review delay a persistent problem. Evidence review stalls when validation records or vendor responses are slow to arrive. Interpreting validation records requires quality-engineering competence that is somewhat scarce. The delay defers the decision at little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which trial cups and lids differ from those intended for production
rows.plain: Make sure the cups and lids used in the test are the same as, or very similar to, those planned for regular production. This helps make the test results relevant.
rows.imp_rat: Trial cups and lids differing from production is a narrow representativeness error; low stakes and the ceiling fixes it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Trials often substitute readily-available containers for the exact production cups and lids, so trial validity is undermined. Sourcing production-intended cups and lids for a trial commonly delays the trial. Recognizing whether trial containers materially differ from production ones needs packaging knowledge that is only moderately available. Wrong-container trials can lead to seal or heat-transfer failures discovered only after purchase, a costly surprise.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which trial conditions differ from intended production conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the test is carried out under conditions that are like the conditions planned for regular production. This helps show whether the equipment will work as expected in real use.
rows.imp_rat: Trial conditions differing from production is a validation error somewhat relevant to sterilization confidence, but held to 1.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Trials are typically run at vendor sites under favorable, non-representative conditions, so results generalize poorly to actual production. Setting up a trial that mirrors production conditions adds scheduling delay before evidence is available. Defining production-representative trial parameters requires process expertise the buyer often lacks. A misleading trial can drive an unsuitable purchase, dwarfing the trial's own cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterilization and cooling results not captured during the trial
rows.plain: Make sure all important results from the germ-killing and cooling tests are recorded during the trial. Missing results can make it harder to tell whether the process worked properly.
rows.imp_rat: Uncaptured trial sterilization/cooling results are a documentation gap during evaluation, peripheral to the running machine's job and modest in severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Trials are frequently ad hoc without a rigorous capture protocol, so critical F-value or cooling-curve data is inconsistently recorded. Capturing trial results requires dedicated test-run time that is often compressed under acquisition schedules. Interpreting sterilization and cooling data demands specialised process expertise that is not always present during factory acceptance trials. Missed critical results can force costly retrofits or re-runs, but the trial itself is a bounded expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trial samples contaminated through handling before evaluation
rows.plain: Prevent trial samples from becoming contaminated while people handle them before they are checked. This helps ensure the evaluation reflects the samples themselves, not handling mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Trial-sample contamination during handling is an evaluation-process error rather than the machine's sterilization performance (low relevance) and affects only test samples (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Good aseptic practice keeps handling contamination low, but occasional breaches still invalidate samples. Aseptic handling of trial samples adds handling time but within normal trial schedules. Contamination-free handling requires trained aseptic technique that fill-finish staff generally have. Contaminated samples waste trial material and repeat runs, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required trial products, utilities, or authorized personnel are unavailable on the scheduled day
rows.plain: Make sure the products, supplies, and people needed for the trial are available on the planned day so the trial is not delayed or canceled.
rows.imp_rat: Missing trial products/utilities/personnel on the scheduled day is a trial-coordination delay, peripheral to the machine's core sterilizing performance; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Aligning several scarce resources on one day is fragile, and no-shows or missing trial batches remain a common recurring surprise. Trial days are scheduled ahead, yet reslotting utilities or authorized personnel around a missed window causes multi-week delays. Coordinating trial product, calibrated utilities, and authorized staff demands cross-functional project-management judgement that not every buyer possesses. Wasted trial product and idle expert time carry cost, but these are usually modest relative to the machine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the acceptance criteria for sterilization, cooling, and sterile fill-finish readiness
rows.plain: Clearly define what must be true for cleaning, cooling, and sterile product filling to be accepted as ready. This prevents disputes about whether the equipment and process are ready to use safely.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for sterilization/cooling readiness is more central to the CFJ, but this batch's ranking mandates a ceiling of 1.0 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria are frequently left vague, so disagreement over readiness is a common recurring failure. Defining acceptance criteria upfront adds negotiation time but prevents far longer disputes later. Writing unambiguous sterilization, cooling, and fill-finish acceptance criteria requires validation and process expertise that is scarce. Ambiguous criteria drive costly re-testing and acceptance disputes at commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of financial exposure left unallocated for sterility failures, product loss, and consequential damages
rows.plain: Make sure money is set aside or assigned to cover possible losses from failed cleanliness, ruined products, or related damage. This prevents the buyer from being left responsible for costs that were not planned for.
rows.imp_rat: Unallocated financial exposure for sterility failures touches contractual risk but not the machine's direct functional performance; capped at 1.0 per the batch ceiling despite real liability stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Because this risk is intangible and easy to postpone, exposure is commonly left unbudgeted, a persistent weakness. Allocating reserves for sterility failure exposure is often deferred late in the deal. Quantifying financial exposure for sterility failures and consequential damages needs actuarial and quality-risk expertise rarely applied at acquisition. Unallocated exposure for product loss and consequential damages is a large, frequently overlooked financial gap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the total negotiated acquisition cost exceeds the approved budget
rows.plain: Keep the agreed buying cost as close as possible to, and no higher than, the approved budget.
rows.imp_rat: Acquisition cost exceeding budget is a purely commercial/administrative error with no bearing on the machine's sterilizing function (low relevance) and absorbable financial consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal budget sign-off processes make budget breaches uncommon but not eliminated when late change orders appear. Budget-vs-quote reconciliation happens quickly once quotes arrive, but negotiation rounds can drag timelines. Experienced procurement staff and finance approvers routinely check negotiated totals against approved budgets. Standard purchasing controls and approval gates catch most budget overruns before commitment, though scope creep and options still cause overruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the agreed delivery obligations allow the machine to arrive after the required installation window
rows.plain: The purchase agreement should require delivery early enough for installation. This prevents the machine from arriving after the planned installation period.
rows.imp_rat: Delivery obligations allowing late arrival is a schedule-delay error, peripheral to the sterilizing CFJ; ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers still slip lead times despite contracts, so late arrival against the install window remains a real recurring risk. The whole point of this need is arrival timing, and delivery-date clauses with penalties are standard contracting practice. Negotiating enforceable delivery obligations is routine procurement work requiring moderate, widely held contracting skill. Late arrival can trigger penalty and idle-install costs, but liquidated-damages clauses partially offset the exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required warranty and post-acceptance support commitments left uncovered
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase agreement covers all promised warranty repairs and support after the equipment is accepted. This matters so the buyer is not left without help when problems arise.
rows.imp_rat: Uncovered warranty/post-acceptance commitments are a contractual matter peripheral to executing sterilization (low relevance) and remediable through negotiation (low severity). Held at 1.0 by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard warranty clauses cover most cases, but some post-acceptance support gaps recur in contracts. Negotiating warranty and support commitments fits within normal contracting time. Procurement typically knows which support commitments to demand. Uncovered support commitments create unbudgeted future costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required financial, technical, quality, safety, or procurement approval records missing from the authorization package
rows.plain: Put all required money, technical, quality, safety, and purchasing approvals into the machine-buying authorization package. This shows that the purchase was properly reviewed before it was approved.
rows.imp_rat: Missing approval records in the authorization package are administrative governance defects peripheral to the machine's function with mild consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Formal authorization workflows and sign-off matrices are well established, catching most missing records. Collecting approval records can stall the authorization timeline. Knowing which approvals are required across finance, technical, quality, safety, and procurement needs cross-functional familiarity. Missing approvals delay rather than directly waste spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine purchase is authorized despite unresolved sterile fill-finish quality or safety requirements
rows.plain: Do not approve buying the machine until all unresolved requirements for safely filling products while keeping them germ-free have been addressed. This helps prevent the purchase of equipment that cannot meet quality or safety needs.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing purchase despite unresolved sterile quality/safety requirements carries real contamination risk, but as a pre-purchase gate it still ranks below the prior batch and is capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Formal quality-gate and change-control practices make authorizing a purchase with open sterility issues relatively uncommon in regulated settings. Quality sign-off gates add steps but are built into the approval timeline rather than causing surprise delay. Sterile fill-finish requirements demand specialized quality and regulatory expertise that regulated food/pharma buyers typically have on staff. Skipping quality resolution risks catastrophic recall cost, and the mature gate practice guards against that spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency between the selected machine configuration, quotation, and approval records
rows.plain: Make sure the machine chosen, the supplier's price offer, and the approval records all describe the same machine and purchase. This helps prevent buying something different from what was approved.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among configuration, quotation, and approval records is a procurement documentation error, tangential to sterilizing food, with administrative reconciliation consequences. Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows link records but manual handoffs still allow configuration inconsistencies to occur. Reconciling configuration, quote and approval records adds a checkpoint but not major delay. Ensuring the approved config matches the quoted and ordered config needs disciplined coordination. Configuration-approval mismatches cause change orders and cost leakage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the total purchase commitment exceeds approved project funding
rows.plain: Keep the total amount committed to buying the machine as close as possible to, and no higher than, the project funding that was approved.
rows.imp_rat: Purchase commitment exceeding approved funding is a procurement-budgeting error with essentially no bearing on the machine's core sterilize-and-cool job; consequences are financial, not functional. Capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Encumbrance and approval systems make over-commitment against funding rare where ERP controls are enforced. Funding checks are embedded in the PO release workflow and add little delay. Finance and project controllers routinely verify commitment against funding, requiring modest specialized judgement. Purchase-order controls and funding gates reliably prevent most commitments from exceeding approved funding.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the authorization package remains pending because review dependencies are unresolved
rows.plain: Approve the machine purchase without delays caused by missing reviews or decisions from other people.
rows.imp_rat: Authorization package pending due to unresolved review dependencies is an internal process delay unrelated to sterilization performance (low relevance) and absorbable (low severity). Capped at 1.0 per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows are inconsistent across organizations and often lack transparency into who is blocking, so stalls recur unpredictably. Authorization packages routinely sit pending while sign-offs, budget approvals, and cross-departmental review dependencies stack up, and standard practice offers little to accelerate serial approvals. Assembling a complete, review-ready authorization package takes procurement know-how, and gaps in that skill trigger avoidable rejections and resubmissions. The waiting mainly ties up staff attention and delays deployment rather than incurring direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of configuration, option, or quantity discrepancies between the order and the approved machine selection
rows.plain: Make sure the machine ordered has the correct setup, choices, and quantity shown in the approved selection. This prevents ordering a machine that does not match what was agreed.
rows.imp_rat: Order-versus-selection discrepancies are procurement clerical errors with limited relevance to the core function and correctable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Order-confirmation and change-control practices catch most discrepancies, though manual transcription still lets some slip through. Cross-checking the order against the approved selection is a quick review step. Line-item verification needs care but no rare expertise. Configuration discrepancies can cause costly rework or re-orders if missed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between the order terms and the agreed pricing or contractual terms
rows.plain: Make sure the order agrees with the accepted prices and other agreed terms. This prevents the buyer and seller from relying on different versions of the deal.
rows.imp_rat: Order-versus-contract term conflicts are commercial administrative defects, peripheral to the CFJ with easily-absorbed consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PO-to-contract matching and approval workflows catch most discrepancies, yet manual handoffs leave recurring gaps. Reconciling order terms against agreed pricing takes routine review time but is not the primary loss when a conflict slips through. Procurement staff and legal review provide moderate competence, though cross-checking terms against contracts still leans on individual diligence. Undetected conflicts can trigger disputes and unplanned charges, but these are occasional rather than systemic overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required purchasing authorization is invalid or unavailable at submission
rows.plain: Make sure the permission to buy the machine is valid and available when the order is submitted. This prevents the order from being rejected or delayed.
rows.imp_rat: Invalid or unavailable purchasing authorization at submission is a clerical procurement error, only tangential to conserving food; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Delegation-of-authority matrices and system controls make invalid or unavailable authorization at submission rare where they are enforced. Verifying authorization validity at submission is a quick check within standard procurement controls. Confirming valid purchasing authority is procedural and low-skill under defined delegation-of-authority rules. Authorization validation carries essentially no direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between order submission and seller acceptance
rows.plain: Get the seller to accept an order soon after it is sent. This confirms quickly that the order can move forward.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between order submission and seller acceptance is a transactional timing error unrelated to the sterilizing job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PO acknowledgement processes make acceptance fairly predictable, with occasional silent lags requiring follow-up. Order acknowledgement from capital-equipment sellers typically lands within days but can lag during busy periods. Confirming acceptance is a clerical follow-up needing little specialized skill. Acceptance delay ties up planning but rarely incurs direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which total order charges exceed the approved funds
rows.plain: Keep all charges for the order within the approved funds. This prevents the machine purchase from going over budget.
rows.imp_rat: Order charges exceeding approved funds is a budget-control error, peripheral to the CFJ of sterilizing and cooling food; severity is bounded and the batch ranking holds it at or below 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Late add-ons and scope creep still push charges over approved funds often enough to be a recurring concern. Reconciling order charges against approved funds happens within the normal PO cycle. Tracking total order charges against a budget is standard procurement bookkeeping requiring modest skill. This need IS about overspend, and approval-limit controls plus quote reviews catch most overruns before they land.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant food sterilizing machine types omitted from the market review
rows.plain: Include all the important types of food-sterilizing machines in the market review. This helps the review give a complete picture of the available options.
rows.imp_rat: Machine types omitted from market review is an evaluation-scope defect with low centrality to the operating job and modest consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Market reviews depend on individual expertise and are inconsistently comprehensive. Scanning the market for machine types adds research time. Knowing the full landscape of relevant sterilizing machine types is specialist knowledge that generalist buyers lack. Missing a suitable type can lock in a costlier or less-fit solution.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of technologies unsuitable for cup and lid sterilisation included as viable options
rows.plain: Avoid treating methods that cannot properly remove germs from cups and lids as suitable choices. This helps ensure the selected equipment can do the job safely.
rows.imp_rat: Including unsuitable cup/lid sterilization technologies as viable options is a selection-stage error, tangential to the core job and low in severity here, held to 1.0 by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Without a suitability filter, inapplicable technologies commonly remain on the option list. Screening out unsuitable technologies happens during review with modest time cost. Judging which technologies suit cup and lid sterilisation demands narrow process expertise that is scarce. Including unsuitable options risks wasted evaluation effort and wrong buys.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of gathered information about machine operating principles and technologies that is inaccurate
rows.plain: Reduce the share of collected information about how sterilizing machines work and what technologies they use that is wrong. This helps people make informed comparisons between machine types.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate information about operating principles is an information-quality defect in evaluation, only indirectly tied to the sterilizing outcome; ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor-biased or misunderstood technical information frequently seeds inaccurate understanding during evaluation. Researching operating principles and technologies is time-consuming and easily under-resourced. Judging the accuracy of technical claims about sterilization technologies requires deep process expertise that many buyers lack. Acting on inaccurate technology information risks buying the wrong machine, an expensive but deferred loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in technical information between market sources
rows.plain: Make sure different sellers and information sources give consistent technical details about sterilizing machines, so people can compare them and make a sound choice.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent market-source technical information is peripheral to actually sterilizing food and has low consequence, so both proxies stay at 1 within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Market and vendor technical data often disagree and there is no standard reconciliation, leaving this modestly served. Reconciling conflicting technical data from market sources takes extended research time. Judging which conflicting technical source is authoritative requires strong domain expertise. Acting on wrong technical data risks specifying an unsuitable, costly machine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required technical information when evaluating machine types
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for the technical details needed to compare different kinds of sterilizing machines. This helps people choose the right type sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for technical information during machine-type evaluation is an information-gathering delay peripheral to the core job (low relevance) with trivial impact (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor-supplied technical data is generally consistent for standard parameters but can be optimistic or incomplete for edge-case food products, creating gaps. Technical datasheets and spec sheets for machine types are usually available online or via sales engineers, but detailed application-specific answers still require back-and-forth that introduces waiting. Interpreting thermal/sterilization specs against a product's process needs requires process-engineering knowledge that gates how fast the info becomes usable. Gathering technical information is mostly a labor-time cost with little material expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required technical specifications missing from candidate machine documentation
rows.plain: Make sure the documents for each possible machine include all the technical details needed for a fair and informed comparison.
rows.imp_rat: Missing technical specs in candidate documentation is an ambiguity in evaluation inputs, peripheral to the core sterilizing job; held at 1.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Candidate documentation routinely lacks some required specifications, making incomplete comparison a common condition. Chasing missing specs from candidate documentation lengthens the evaluation cycle. Identifying which technical specifications are missing requires specialist familiarity with sterilizer performance parameters. Selecting on incomplete specs risks costly mismatches discovered post-purchase.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate machine specifications compared under non-equivalent operating conditions
rows.plain: Avoid comparing machine details when the machines were tested or described under different working conditions. Fair comparisons help people choose the machine that best fits the job.
rows.imp_rat: Comparing candidate specs under non-equivalent conditions is a pre-purchase evaluation error, peripheral to the operating machine's CFJ, with modest severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors quote specs under differing assumptions and there is no standard basis, so apples-to-oranges comparisons persist. Normalizing specs to equivalent operating conditions adds analysis time before comparison. Recognizing non-equivalent operating conditions across datasheets requires process expertise that is not always present. Comparing on unequal bases can select a poorer machine, an expensive error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of technical specifications with conflicting values across source documents
rows.plain: Avoid having different source documents give conflicting technical details. This helps people know which machine information is correct.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting specification values across documents create ambiguity but are peripheral to the physical sterilization job, with recoverable consequences, so both proxies remain at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation is manual with no single source of truth, so conflicting specs recur across documents. Cross-comparing specification values across documents is tedious and time-consuming. Spotting conflicting values requires technical judgement about which figure governs. Acting on the wrong value can cause costly procurement mistakes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of facility requirements omitted from the machine comparison
rows.plain: Make sure every requirement of the facility is included when comparing machines. Missing a requirement could lead to choosing a machine that does not fit the site.
rows.imp_rat: Facility requirements omitted from comparison is a pre-purchase evaluation defect peripheral to the CFJ with modest severity; batch ceiling caps at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Comparisons are typically built from vendor spec sheets without a standardised checklist, so facility items are routinely left out. Compiling facility requirements into a comparison adds upfront effort that is often rushed. Knowing which facility constraints matter requires engineering judgement that is unevenly available. Omitted facility requirements surface as expensive installation surprises later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specification values incorrectly converted to common units
rows.plain: Convert machine measurements into shared units correctly when comparing machines. This prevents incorrect comparisons caused by calculation mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Unit-conversion mistakes in specification values are a low-relevance clerical error relative to the sterilizing/cooling job and carry minor severity, so it stays at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Conversions are done manually and occasionally wrong, but reviews usually catch gross errors. Unit conversions are quick once flagged. Correct conversion between metric and imperial units relies on careful attention that is easily lapsed. A bad conversion can cause costly wrong-sizing but such errors are individually rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of applicable food-safety, pressure-vessel, and regional requirements lacking documentary evidence
rows.plain: Reduce the share of required food, equipment-safety, and local compliance records that have no supporting proof. This helps show that the equipment meets the rules that apply to it.
rows.imp_rat: Missing documentary evidence for food-safety/pressure-vessel requirements is a compliance-documentation defect, important for approval but peripheral to the functional job here; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Gaps in required documentary evidence are common because coverage depends on the buyer's regulatory checklist maturity. Assembling documentary evidence for food-safety and pressure-vessel compliance is time-consuming but scheduled into acquisition. Knowing which food-safety, pressure-vessel, and regional requirements apply demands specialized regulatory knowledge that is not uniformly present. Missing documentation drives later certification and rework costs, a meaningful and recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compliance records apply to a different machine configuration than the one offered
rows.plain: Make sure the required compliance documents apply to the exact machine setup being offered. Documents for another setup may not show that this machine meets the requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched compliance records versus machine configuration is a documentation-accuracy issue during procurement (low relevance), and the ceiling caps consequences at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance paperwork is routinely collected, but mismatches between certified and offered configurations still slip through document review. Cross-checking compliance records against the offered configuration takes documentation review time that is often compressed. Verifying that certifications match the specific configuration requires regulatory and technical literacy that is unevenly present. Document verification is cheap, so cost is rarely the barrier to doing it properly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of submitted certificates or declarations that are expired or based on superseded requirements
rows.plain: Avoid submitting certificates or written statements that have expired or rely on old requirements. This helps show that the system still meets current rules.
rows.imp_rat: Expired or superseded certificates are a documentation/compliance issue only loosely connected to the core job; severity is contained and the batch ranking caps this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Certificate expiry checks are inconsistent, so expired or superseded declarations still slip through. Checking certificate validity dates adds review time. Recognising superseded requirements needs current regulatory knowledge that is specialised. Accepting an expired certificate rarely costs money directly until an audit fails.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among machine identifiers, declarations, and test records
rows.plain: Make sure the machine names and numbers, official statements, and test records all refer to the same machines. This helps ensure the compliance paperwork is accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among machine identifiers, declarations, and test records is a traceability/paperwork error, peripheral to food conservation and with mostly administrative fallout. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Document sets from suppliers frequently carry small inconsistencies that manual checking does not reliably reconcile. Cross-checking identifiers, declarations and test records is tedious and slows document acceptance. Detecting inconsistencies among certificates and test records demands meticulous, expert document review. Mismatched documentation can trigger compliance rework and rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to obtain missing or corrected compliance documentation before purchase commitment
rows.plain: Get any missing or corrected documents showing that the product meets required rules before agreeing to buy it. This helps prevent a purchase based on incomplete or incorrect information.
rows.imp_rat: Time to obtain corrected compliance documentation before commitment is a pre-purchase paperwork delay tangential to sterilizing food (low relevance) and easily accommodated (low severity). Ceiling limits to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance packages frequently arrive incomplete, outdated, or wrong on first pass, so rework loops are common and outcomes uncertain. Obtaining missing or corrected compliance documents (food-contact certificates, CE/hygiene declarations) from suppliers routinely stalls the purchase for weeks because it depends on the supplier's third parties and paperwork backlogs. Knowing which documents are legally required and spotting an incorrect one demands specialist regulatory/quality expertise that many buyers lack in-house. Delays and re-issuing of documentation add administrative cost and can push back commissioning, but the direct spend is limited.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of lifecycle cost components—including maintenance, downtime, consumables, and end-of-life obligations—omitted from each candidate machine assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the assessment of each possible machine includes all major costs over its useful life, including upkeep, lost working time, supplies, and what happens when the machine is retired. Leaving out any of these costs can make one machine seem cheaper than it really is.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted lifecycle cost components affect purchase economics but are peripheral to the machine's sterilize-and-cool function with modest severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Assessments vary widely in completeness, so lifecycle cost coverage is inconsistent across candidates. Assembling full lifecycle cost data lengthens the assessment considerably. Total-cost-of-ownership modelling requires financial and engineering skill many buyers lack. Omitting maintenance, downtime, consumables, or end-of-life obligations is precisely the overspend this need targets, and it happens often.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in evaluation periods, utilization assumptions, and financial assumptions across candidate machine assessments
rows.plain: Use the same time period, expected use, and money-related assumptions when comparing the machines. This makes the cost comparisons more reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in evaluation periods and financial assumptions across assessments is a financial-analysis error unrelated to the sterilization job, with decision-quality consequences. Bounded at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without an enforced common assumption set, candidate assessments frequently vary in period and utilization inputs. Aligning evaluation periods and financial assumptions across candidates is labor-intensive. Financial modeling consistency requires specialized analyst skill that is not always uniformly applied. Divergent assumptions distort TCO comparisons and can drive the wrong, costlier choice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage deviation between forecast and realized utility and consumable costs over the evaluation period
rows.plain: Keep the predicted costs for power, water, and supplies closer to what they actually cost during the review period. This makes ongoing expenses easier to budget for.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between forecast and realized utility/consumable costs is a budgeting error tangential to sterilizing food, low in severity, and held at the 1.0 ceiling.
