rows.sat_rat: Supplier consumption figures are often optimistic, so realized costs deviate from forecasts with fair frequency. Utility and consumable forecasts are made early but only verified over a lengthy evaluation period. Modeling energy, water, and consumable use for a sterilizer requires process-costing expertise not always at hand. Deviations between forecast and realized utility and consumable costs recur and eat into operating budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage by which estimated site preparation, installation, commissioning, and qualification costs fall below realized costs
rows.plain: Make the early estimate for preparing the site, setting up the equipment, starting it, and checking that it meets requirements closer to the actual cost. This makes the total cost easier to plan for.
rows.imp_rat: Underestimated site-prep/commissioning costs are a financial-forecast error peripheral to the machine's functional job, with recoverable consequences, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Forecast-vs-actual gaps are the norm because scope creep and site surprises are hard to predict reliably. Cost estimates are prepared upfront but realized costs only emerge after commissioning, long after the estimate. Accurate site-prep and qualification cost estimation needs experienced project engineers who are in short supply. Estimates for installation, commissioning, and qualification chronically underrun actual spend, and this overspend is routinely absorbed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of ownership cost supported only by incomplete or unverified supplier information
rows.plain: Base the equipment’s full ownership cost on supplier information that is complete and checked. This helps the buyer make a realistic cost comparison.
rows.imp_rat: Ownership cost resting on unverified supplier data is a procurement-estimation ambiguity with no direct impact on sterilizing or cooling food; capped at 1.0 by the batch rule.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers routinely provide incomplete or optimistic ownership-cost data with little independent verification, so this remains poorly served. Chasing complete supplier ownership-cost data takes considerable time and often stalls the evaluation. It takes real expertise to know which lifecycle-cost inputs are missing and how to challenge supplier claims. Decisions built on unverified TCO inputs risk large downstream operating-cost surprises.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of eligible machine material rejected by recycling facilities because of residual process chemicals, product, or lubricants
rows.plain: Reduce the share of machine materials that recycling centers reject because they still contain process chemicals, product, or oil. This helps more of the machine be accepted for recycling.
rows.imp_rat: Recycling rejection due to residual chemicals is an end-of-life disposal error, unrelated to the operating job of conserving food; held to 1.0 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning reduces but does not consistently eliminate residues in lubricated or chemical-contact parts, causing periodic rejection. Extra decontamination to meet recycler thresholds extends the process. Judging residual-chemical levels against recycler limits needs specialist knowledge. Material rejected for residual contamination is lost recovery value plus added disposal cost, the core loss here.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of eligible machine material routed to an incompatible recycling stream
rows.plain: Fewer usable machine materials should be sent to a recycling process that cannot handle them. This helps ensure the materials are recycled correctly instead of being rejected or wasted.
rows.imp_rat: Routing end-of-life material to the wrong recycling stream is an environmental/disposal concern outside the food-conservation CFJ (low relevance) with limited severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established recycler intake sorting catches many errors, but mixed-material assemblies still get misrouted with some regularity. Correct stream routing does not itself change how long disposal takes. Matching machine materials to the compatible recycling stream needs materials-identification knowledge that general labor may lack. Wrong-stream routing causes rejection fees and lost recovery value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that no certified recycling facility will accept a sorted material stream when routing is required
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that no certified recycling facility will accept the separated materials when they need to be sent away. This helps ensure the materials can actually be recycled.
rows.imp_rat: A recycling facility rejecting a sorted material stream is an end-of-life disposal concern far removed from sterilizing/cooling food (low relevance) with low operational stakes (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established recycler networks exist for common stainless and steel, but acceptance of mixed or contaminated food-machine streams is inconsistent. Finding an accepting facility can take time when material streams are unusual, delaying routing. Knowing which certified recyclers accept specific alloys and contaminated streams requires specialist waste-broker knowledge. Rejected streams force costly re-routing or landfill disposal fees.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of eligible machine material sent to disposal because it remains attached to mixed-material assemblies
rows.plain: Send as much usable machine material as possible for recycling instead of throwing it away because it is still stuck to other materials. This reduces waste and preserves materials that can be reused.
rows.imp_rat: Eligible material sent to disposal via mixed-material assemblies is an end-of-life recycling waste issue, irrelevant to the CFJ of conserving food; its consequences are minor material loss. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without design-for-disassembly, standard teardown routinely leaves recoverable material trapped in mixed assemblies. Disassembling mixed-material assemblies to recover parts takes extra labor time. Separating bonded or fastened dissimilar materials needs skilled dismantlers. Recyclable material discarded because it stays attached to mixed assemblies is lost recovery value, the central waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recycling shipments delayed by incomplete material, custody, or destination records
rows.plain: Fewer recycling shipments should be delayed because records about the materials, who handled them, or where they are going are incomplete. Complete records help shipments move on time.
rows.imp_rat: Delayed recycling shipments from incomplete records are peripheral to the CFJ of sterilizing and cooling food, and the batch ceiling caps this low; consequences are administrative and reversible.
rows.sat_rat: Manifest and custody templates exist but are frequently filled inconsistently, so delays remain a recurring occurrence. Incomplete material, custody, or destination records stall shipments at the dock while paperwork is chased, the core delay this need targets. Assembling recycling manifests needs some regulatory familiarity but is routine clerical work. Delays incur demurrage, storage, and rescheduling costs.
